passages.title: The Ant and the Grasshopper
passages.attribution: Aesop, translated by V. S. Vernon Jones (1912). Public domain.
passages.text: In a field one summer's day a Grasshopper was hopping about, chirping and singing to its heart's content. An Ant passed by, bearing along with great effort an ear of corn he was taking to the nest. "Why not come and chat with me," said the Grasshopper, "instead of toiling and moiling in that way?" "I am helping to lay up food for the winter," said the Ant, "and recommend you to do the same." "Why bother about winter?" said the Grasshopper; "we have got plenty of food at present." But the Ant went on its way and continued its toil. When the winter came the Grasshopper had no food, and found itself dying of hunger, while it saw the ants distributing every day corn and grain from the stores they had collected in the summer. Then the Grasshopper knew: it is best to prepare for the days of need.

questions.stem: What lesson does the fable teach?
choices: Hard work in good times prepares you for hard times.
Singing is a waste of a summer day.
Ants and grasshoppers cannot be friends.
Winter always arrives sooner than expected.
questions.explanation: The Ant stores food in summer and survives winter, while the idle Grasshopper starves — the moral is to prepare during times of plenty.

questions.stem: What was the Ant carrying when it passed the Grasshopper?
questions.explanation: The text says the Ant was "bearing along with great effort an ear of corn."

questions.stem: In this passage, "toiling and moiling" most nearly means:
questions.explanation: "Toiling and moiling" is an old phrase for laboring hard — contrasted with the Grasshopper's idle chatting.

lesson.strategy: This is a fable: two animals, opposite choices, one lesson. All summer the Ant works and the Grasshopper plays. Watch for the turn at "When the winter came" — it flips who is happy. The last line says the moral out loud, so read it slowly.

signals.phrase: Why bother about winter?
signals.means: The Grasshopper waves off the future. This careless choice is the mistake the fable will punish.

signals.phrase: lay up food for the winter
signals.means: The Ant's plan: store now for later. This is the smart choice the fable will reward.

signals.phrase: When the winter came
signals.means: The turn. The season flips, and now you see the result of each animal's summer choice.

signals.phrase: it is best to prepare for the days of need
signals.means: The moral, said plainly at the end. This one line is the point of the whole story.

vocab.inContext: carrying — the Ant is bearing an ear of corn to the nest.
working hard; doing heavy, tiring work, like the Ant hauling corn.
an old word for hard work, paired with 'toiling' to mean the same tiring effort.

skillTips.main-idea: Don't guess the lesson from the middle. This fable states it in the last line: 'it is best to prepare for the days of need.' The whole story is built to prove that one sentence.
skillTips.inference: The story never calls the Grasshopper lazy. You infer it from what it does — 'hopping about, chirping and singing' while the Ant hauls corn. Judge each animal by its actions, not by a label.
skillTips.vocabulary: For old hard words like 'toiling and moiling', use the action next to them. The Ant carries corn 'with great effort', so both words mean hard, tiring work.
skillTips.detail: To keep who-did-what straight, follow each animal on its own. Ant: carries corn, warns, keeps working. Grasshopper: plays, asks 'Why bother', then in winter 'had no food'.

passages.title: The Gettysburg Address
passages.attribution: Abraham Lincoln, Gettysburg Address (1863). Public domain.
passages.text: Four score and seven years ago our fathers brought forth on this continent, a new nation, conceived in Liberty, and dedicated to the proposition that all men are created equal. Now we are engaged in a great civil war, testing whether that nation, or any nation so conceived and so dedicated, can long endure. We are met on a great battle-field of that war. We have come to dedicate a portion of that field, as a final resting place for those who here gave their lives that that nation might live. But, in a larger sense, we can not dedicate — we can not consecrate — we can not hallow — this ground. The brave men, living and dead, who struggled here, have consecrated it, far above our poor power to add or detract. It is for us the living, rather, to be dedicated here to the unfinished work which they who fought here have thus far so nobly advanced.

questions.stem: "Four score and seven years" refers to how many years?
questions.explanation: A "score" is twenty. Four score (80) plus seven equals 87.

questions.stem: Why does Lincoln say the living cannot truly consecrate the ground?
choices: Because the ceremony was too short
Because the soldiers who fought already made it sacred through their sacrifice
Because the war was not yet over
Because the ground belonged to the nation, not individuals
questions.explanation: Lincoln argues the brave men "have consecrated it, far above our poor power to add or detract" — their sacrifice, not the speech, hallows the ground.

questions.stem: What does Lincoln urge the living to do?
choices: Build a monument on the battlefield
Dedicate themselves to the unfinished work the soldiers advanced
Honor only the soldiers who died
questions.explanation: He calls the living "to be dedicated here to the unfinished work" the fallen had "so nobly advanced."

lesson.strategy: This is a wartime speech, not a report. Read it in three beats: the founding claim that all men are created equal, the test the civil war puts it to, then the turn at 'But, in a larger sense' where Lincoln shifts from honoring the dead to charging the living.

signals.phrase: Four score and seven years ago
signals.means: Biblical cadence; a score is 20, so 87 years back to 1776. He opens by dating the nation's founding.

signals.phrase: But, in a larger sense
signals.means: The hinge of the speech. Everything before honors the dead; after this, he redirects to the living.

signals.phrase: we can not dedicate — we can not consecrate
signals.means: Three rising near-synonyms. He denies the crowd's power to bless the ground, so the soldiers' act outweighs it.

signals.phrase: It is for us the living, rather
signals.means: The pivot lands: the real subject is not the dead but a duty handed to those still alive.

vocab.inContext: begun or brought into being; the nation was started with liberty as its founding idea.
to make sacred; he says the crowd cannot do it, the soldiers already did.
to last or survive; the war tests whether such a nation can 'long endure.'

skillTips.main-idea: The point is not the burial. Read past the tribute to 'But, in a larger sense' and 'It is for us the living' — the main idea is the charge that the living finish the soldiers' work.
skillTips.inference: He never says what 'the unfinished work' is. Infer it from 'all men are created equal' and 'so conceived and so dedicated' — the work is keeping that founding promise alive.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Dedicate,' 'consecrate,' and 'hallow' are stacked as near-synonyms meaning 'make sacred.' The rising repetition tells you they climb in force, not in new meaning.
skillTips.detail: Anchor the facts he gives: 'a great civil war,' 'a great battle-field,' 'a final resting place.' These set when, where, and why the crowd stands there before the argument turns.

passages.title: An Entangled Bank
passages.source: Charles Darwin, On the Origin of Species (1859)
passages.attribution: Charles Darwin, On the Origin of Species, closing passage (1859). Public domain.
passages.text: It is interesting to contemplate an entangled bank, clothed with many plants of many kinds, with birds singing on the bushes, with various insects flitting about, and with worms crawling through the damp earth, and to reflect that these elaborately constructed forms, so different from each other, and dependent on each other in so complex a manner, have all been produced by laws acting around us. These laws, taken in the largest sense, being Growth with Reproduction; Inheritance; Variability; a Ratio of Increase so high as to lead to a Struggle for Life, and as a consequence to Natural Selection, entailing Divergence of Character and the Extinction of less-improved forms. Thus, from the war of nature, from famine and death, the most exalted object which we are capable of conceiving, namely, the production of the higher animals, directly follows.

questions.stem: What is Darwin's central claim in this passage?
choices: Complex, interdependent life forms arise from a few natural laws.
Birds and insects compete for the same food.
Extinction is rare in the natural world.
questions.explanation: Darwin marvels that diverse, interdependent forms were "all been produced by laws acting around us," which he then lists.

questions.stem: What does Darwin find striking about the outcome of "the war of nature"?
choices: That it produces the higher animals, an exalted result, from famine and death
That humans are exempt from it
That it favors plants over animals
questions.explanation: He notes that from "famine and death" follows "the most exalted object" — a striking, almost paradoxical, conclusion.

questions.stem: As used here, "contemplate" most nearly means:
questions.explanation: To contemplate the bank is to look at it thoughtfully and reflect, as the rest of the sentence makes clear.

lesson.strategy: Read this as one argument in three moves: first the picture (the entangled bank full of life), then the named laws that made it, then the payoff at the very end. Do not stop at the pretty image. The point is that all that tangled life came from a few laws, and it "directly follows."

signals.phrase: laws acting around us
signals.means: The hinge: Darwin turns from describing the living scene to claiming a few natural laws produced all of it.

signals.phrase: Struggle for Life
signals.means: A capitalized law in a causal chain; it leads to Natural Selection. Read the semicolons as a list of causes.

signals.phrase: war of nature, from famine and death
signals.means: The dark cost. Watch how Darwin reframes it as the source of something grand, not something to mourn.

signals.phrase: directly follows
signals.means: The landing word. The argument aims here: higher animals result from those laws. The main claim sits at the end.

vocab.inContext: to think deeply about, to consider with care
tangled together; the plants, birds, insects, and worms all woven into one system
highest, most noble; here the grandest thing we can imagine

skillTips.main-idea: The main claim is not the bank scene; it is the sentence ending in "directly follows": many tangled life forms came from a few natural laws. Read the passage as building to that end.
skillTips.inference: Darwin never says he admires this. Infer his attitude from word choice: he calls the result of "war of nature" the "most exalted object." His tone is awe, not sorrow.
skillTips.vocabulary: The capitalized terms (Growth, Inheritance, Variability, Struggle for Life, Natural Selection) are named laws, not everyday words. Read them as a list of causes, each feeding the next.
skillTips.detail: The specific laws live in the middle sentence, split by semicolons. Track the order: a high Ratio of Increase leads to Struggle for Life, then Natural Selection, Divergence and Extinction.

passages.title: The Time Traveller Explains
passages.source: H. G. Wells, The Time Machine (1895)
passages.attribution: H. G. Wells, The Time Machine (1895). Public domain.
passages.text: The Time Traveller (for so it will be convenient to speak of him) was expounding a recondite matter to us. His pale grey eyes shone and twinkled, and his usually pale face was flushed and animated. "You must follow me carefully. I shall have to controvert one or two ideas that are almost universally accepted. The geometry, for instance, they taught you at school is founded on a misconception." "Is not that rather a large thing to expect us to begin upon?" said Filby, an argumentative person with red hair. "I do not mean to ask you to accept anything without reasonable ground for it. You will soon admit as much as I need from you. You know of course that a mathematical line, a line of thickness nil, has no real existence. They taught you that? Neither has a mathematical plane. These things are mere abstractions."

questions.stem: How does the Time Traveller expect his listeners to react to his ideas at first?
choices: With skepticism, which he plans to overcome with reasoning
questions.explanation: He says he will "controvert" widely accepted ideas but won't ask them to accept anything "without reasonable ground," anticipating doubt he intends to argue past.

questions.stem: What example does the Time Traveller give of an abstraction with "no real existence"?
choices: A mathematical line of zero thickness
questions.explanation: He cites "a mathematical line, a line of thickness nil" and a "mathematical plane" as mere abstractions.

questions.stem: "Recondite" as used here most nearly means:
questions.explanation: A recondite matter is a deep or obscure one — fitting, since he must explain it carefully.

lesson.strategy: A Victorian scene built on one argument. Track two things: who speaks (the Time Traveller making the claim vs. Filby doubting it) and the claim-then-proof shape. He warns his idea sounds strange, then uses the line and plane to prove it.

signals.phrase: You must follow me carefully
signals.means: He warns the argument is hard; read the next sentences slowly and track each step of his reasoning.

signals.phrase: ideas that are almost universally accepted
signals.means: His claim goes against what everyone believes — the bold point he must defend.

signals.phrase: an argumentative person with red hair
signals.means: Marks Filby as the doubter; his questions are the pushback the Time Traveller must answer.

signals.phrase: These things are mere abstractions
signals.means: The payoff of his proof: a line and plane are only ideas, not real, so school geometry rests on a false start.

vocab.inContext: deep and hard to understand — the matter he explains is not simple everyday talk
argue against, dispute — he plans to attack ideas most people accept as true
ideas only, with no real existence — a line or plane lives just in the mind

skillTips.main-idea: Read past the fancy talk to his real claim: school geometry is 'founded on a misconception' because a line and plane are 'mere abstractions.' That claim, not the room, is the point.
skillTips.inference: He never says 'I am excited.' Infer it from his body — eyes that 'shone and twinkled,' a face 'flushed and animated' — and his warning to 'follow me carefully.'
skillTips.vocabulary: Read the whole sentence around a hard word. 'Controvert one or two ideas that are almost universally accepted' shows controvert means go against — you cannot accept and attack an idea.
skillTips.detail: Match each line to its speaker. Filby challenges with 'Is not that rather a large thing...'; the Time Traveller replies he will not ask you 'to accept anything without reasonable ground.'

passages.title: On Rising in the Morning
passages.attribution: Marcus Aurelius, Meditations, Book V, trans. George Long (1862). Public domain.
passages.text: In the morning when thou risest unwillingly, let this thought be present: I am rising to the work of a human being. Why then am I dissatisfied if I am going to do the things for which I exist and for which I was brought into the world? Or have I been made for this, to lie in the bed-clothes and keep myself warm? But this is more pleasant. Dost thou exist then to take thy pleasure, and not at all for action or exertion? Dost thou not see the little plants, the little birds, the ants, the spiders, the bees working together to put in order their several parts of the universe? And art thou unwilling to do the work of a human being, and dost thou not make haste to do that which is according to thy nature?

questions.stem: What is the author urging the reader to do?
choices: Rise and do the work proper to a human being
questions.explanation: He reminds himself he was made "for action or exertion," not to lie warm in bed.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention plants, birds, ants, and bees?
choices: To show nature is more beautiful than humans
As examples of beings that do their natural work without complaint
To suggest humans should live outdoors
To prove animals are happier than people
questions.explanation: They all "work together to put in order their several parts of the universe" — a model for doing one's own natural work.

questions.stem: What excuse for staying in bed does the author consider and reject?
choices: That it is too cold to work
That staying warm is more pleasant
That there is no work to do
questions.explanation: He raises "But this is more pleasant" and rejects it by asking whether he exists merely to take pleasure.

lesson.strategy: This is Marcus Aurelius talking to himself, not to you. He argues with his own wish to stay in bed. His rhetorical questions like "Dost thou exist then to take thy pleasure?" each expect "no" and push one claim: do the work you were made for. Translate the old "thou/dost" as you read.

signals.phrase: But this is more pleasant
signals.means: The lazy voice speaks. He plants his own objection so he can knock it down — this is not his real view.

signals.phrase: Dost thou exist then to take thy pleasure
signals.means: A rhetorical question, not a real one. It expects the answer 'no' and drives his argument forward.

signals.phrase: the little plants, the little birds, the ants
signals.means: Argument by analogy: all of nature works, so a human avoiding work is out of step with it.

signals.phrase: the work of a human being
signals.means: His thesis, repeated: you exist to act, and rising to work is your nature, not a burden.

vocab.inContext: against his own wish; he gets up even though he does not want to.
effort, hard work of body or mind — the opposite of lying warm in bed.
unhappy, discontented; he asks why he feels this about doing his purpose.

skillTips.main-idea: The point comes through questions, not a flat statement. Read 'the things for which I exist and for which I was brought into the world' as his answer: humans exist to act.
skillTips.inference: The 'thou' and 'thy' aim at himself, not the reader. No other person is named — this is a private pep talk. Speaker and listener are one man arguing to get out of bed.
skillTips.vocabulary: The English is old. Swap 'dost thou' to 'do you', 'thou risest' to 'you rise', 'thy' to 'your'. Modernize the pronouns and the meaning is plain — don't let spelling stall you.
skillTips.detail: The creatures each have a task. Track the shared idea in 'working together to put in order their several parts of the universe' — every one of them is working.

passages.title: The Woman
passages.source: Arthur Conan Doyle, A Scandal in Bohemia (1891)
passages.attribution: Arthur Conan Doyle, The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes (1891). Public domain.
passages.text: To Sherlock Holmes she is always the woman. I have seldom heard him mention her under any other name. In his eyes she eclipses and predominates the whole of her sex. It was not that he felt any emotion akin to love for Irene Adler. All emotions, and that one particularly, were abhorrent to his cold, precise but admirably balanced mind. He was, I take it, the most perfect reasoning and observing machine that the world has seen, but as a lover he would have placed himself in a false position. He never spoke of the softer passions, save with a gibe and a sneer. They were admirable things for the observer — excellent for drawing the veil from men's motives and actions. But for the trained reasoner to admit such intrusions into his own delicate and finely adjusted temperament was to introduce a distracting factor which might throw a doubt upon all his mental results.

questions.stem: Why does the narrator say Holmes avoids emotions like love?
choices: Because he had been hurt before
Because they could disturb his precise reasoning
questions.explanation: Emotions would "introduce a distracting factor which might throw a doubt upon all his mental results."

questions.stem: "Abhorrent" as used here most nearly means:
questions.explanation: Emotions were "abhorrent" to his mind — that is, repugnant or hateful to it.

questions.stem: How does the narrator characterize Holmes overall?
choices: As a warm and romantic man
As a flawless reasoning machine wary of emotion
As someone secretly in love with Irene Adler
As a careless observer of people
questions.explanation: He calls Holmes "the most perfect reasoning and observing machine" for whom emotion would be a liability.

lesson.strategy: The narrator (Watson) never shows a scene — he explains Holmes's mind. Read to split two things: what Holmes feels for Irene Adler (nothing "akin to love") from why she still matters (she beats his cold logic). Watch the "not X, but Y" turns that correct your guess before you make it.

signals.phrase: she is always the woman
signals.means: The opening claim the whole paragraph exists to explain: why this one woman is singular to Holmes.

signals.phrase: It was not that he felt any emotion akin to love
signals.means: A denial turn — the narrator blocks the obvious guess (love) so you look for a different reason she matters.

signals.phrase: reasoning and observing machine
signals.means: Holmes defined as a machine, not a man; emotion is the thing that would break him.

signals.phrase: a gibe and a sneer
signals.means: How Holmes treats feelings — with mockery; fixes his cold character before any plot begins.

vocab.inContext: outshines and blots out — she overshadows every other woman in his eyes
hated, deeply unwelcome — emotions disgust his precise mind
a mocking, cutting remark — how he speaks of soft feelings

skillTips.main-idea: Every sentence answers one question: why Irene Adler is "the woman" to Holmes. Trace each line back to that. The paragraph is about Holmes's mind, not a romance.
skillTips.inference: He calls it "not... love" and emotions "abhorrent," yet spends the whole paragraph on her. Read the gap: she matters because she threatens his logic, not because he loves her.
skillTips.vocabulary: Hard words — "eclipses," "predominates," "abhorrent" — cluster on one idea. Pin each using the plain words nearby: "always the woman" and "cold, precise" mind.
skillTips.detail: For a fact about Holmes's character, look at the machine lines: "reasoning and observing machine" and "cold, precise but admirably balanced mind" state exactly how he handles feeling.

passages.title: The Cost of a Scrolling Mind
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Every feed you scroll is engineered to interrupt you. The designers measure success in seconds of attention captured, so each post is short, vivid, and quickly replaced by the next. Over time this trains a particular habit of mind: skim, react, move on. The trouble is that deep understanding works the opposite way. To follow a long argument, hold several ideas at once, or notice what an author implies but never states, the mind must stay still and sustain effort across paragraphs. That stamina is a skill, and like any skill it weakens without practice. Reading a difficult page slowly is not nostalgia for an older medium; it is a workout for the exact muscles that feeds quietly let atrophy. The encouraging part is that the muscle responds. People who read demanding material a little each day report that focus returns, often within weeks.

questions.stem: What is the passage's central argument?
choices: Social media feeds should be banned.
Scrolling trains shallow attention, but deliberate reading can rebuild deep focus.
Reading is only valuable for nostalgia.
questions.explanation: The passage contrasts the skim-and-react habit feeds create with the sustained focus reading builds, and ends on the hopeful note that the "muscle responds."

questions.stem: Why does the author compare difficult reading to a "workout"?
choices: Because focus is a trainable capacity that strengthens with effortful practice
Because reading is unpleasant like exercise
Because both should be done at a gym
questions.explanation: The author frames sustained attention as a "muscle" that "weakens without practice" but "responds" to training — a workout metaphor for a trainable skill.

questions.stem: "Atrophy," as used in the passage, most nearly means:
questions.explanation: The feeds "let atrophy" the focus muscles — i.e., let them weaken or waste away from lack of use.

lesson.strategy: This whole argument rests on one metaphor: focus is a muscle. Watch three moves — how feeds train you to skim, react, move on; the cost at "The trouble is"; the fix at "The encouraging part." Track those turns and you hold the essay.

signals.phrase: engineered to interrupt you
signals.means: The opening claim: feeds break your focus on purpose, by design — not by accident.

signals.phrase: The trouble is
signals.means: The pivot. The author turns from how feeds work to the harm they cause; the real argument starts here.

signals.phrase: workout for the exact muscles
signals.means: The core metaphor: focus is a muscle that hard reading trains and easy feeds let waste away.

signals.phrase: The encouraging part
signals.means: The hopeful turn. The author moves from the problem to the fix — focus can come back.

vocab.inContext: deliberately built and tuned to hit a goal — here, to grab and hold your attention
staying power; endurance to hold effort across paragraphs without drifting
waste away and weaken from lack of use, like an unexercised muscle

skillTips.main-idea: One claim, one metaphor. Chain the turns — "The trouble is" (feeds train skim, react, move on) and "The encouraging part" (focus returns): feeds weaken focus, slow reading rebuilds it.
skillTips.inference: The author rarely attacks feeds head-on; harm rides in his loaded verbs — "engineered to interrupt you," muscles "quietly let atrophy." Read the word choice, not the plain statements.
skillTips.vocabulary: The hard words share one metaphor — the mind as a muscle. Read "stamina," "sustain effort," and "atrophy" through exercise: stamina is staying power, atrophy is wasting from no use.
skillTips.detail: Details hide in the contrast sentences: "deep understanding works the opposite way" and "not nostalgia... it is a workout." For detail questions, find what is pitted against what.

passages.title: When the Machine Reads For You
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: It is now trivial to ask a machine to summarize a book in three bullet points. The summary will often be accurate, and for some purposes that is enough. But a summary is a destination handed to you without the journey, and the journey is where most of the value lay. When you read the full argument yourself, you do not merely learn its conclusion; you watch how the author builds it, where the evidence is strong and where it quietly leans on assumption, which sentence made you pause and reconsider. That experience leaves you able to reason in the author's domain, not just to repeat a verdict. Outsourcing the reading returns the answer but keeps the understanding for the machine. The wise use of these tools, then, is not to replace your reading but to sharpen it: let the machine fetch and translate and define, while you keep, for yourself, the slow work of judgment.

questions.stem: What distinction does the author draw between a summary and reading the full text?
choices: Summaries are always inaccurate; full texts are accurate.
A summary gives the conclusion, while full reading builds the understanding and judgment behind it.
Full reading is faster than reading a summary.
Summaries are only useful for fiction.
questions.explanation: The author calls a summary "a destination handed to you without the journey" — you get the verdict but not the reasoning ability that full reading develops.

questions.stem: What does the author suggest is the wise use of AI tools for reading?
choices: Use them to replace reading entirely
Let them assist (fetch, translate, define) while you keep the work of judgment
questions.explanation: The closing line says to "let the machine fetch and translate and define, while you keep, for yourself, the slow work of judgment."

questions.stem: What does the phrase "keeps the understanding for the machine" imply?
choices: When you outsource reading, you gain the answer but not the comprehension.
Machines store text more reliably than people.
questions.explanation: It is a pointed way of saying the reader who skips the reading is left without the understanding that the effort would have produced.

lesson.strategy: One metaphor carries the argument: reading is a journey, the summary a destination. Note the concession ("that is enough"), the turn at "But," then the final rule. That closing prescription, not the opening line, is the thesis.

signals.phrase: for some purposes that is enough
signals.means: A concession — the author grants the summary's value first, so the real claim can push against it.

signals.phrase: But a summary is a destination
signals.means: The pivot. 'But' flips the concession into the argument, using the journey/destination metaphor.

signals.phrase: Outsourcing the reading returns the answer
signals.means: The cost stated plainly: you get the answer, but the machine keeps the understanding.

signals.phrase: not to replace your reading but to sharpen it
signals.means: The thesis and rule — the not-X-but-Y frame naming what the tools are actually for.

vocab.inContext: So easy it takes almost no effort — asking a machine for a summary costs nothing.
Handing off a task you should do yourself — here, giving your reading to the machine.
A final judgment or conclusion — the bare answer, without the reasoning behind it.

skillTips.main-idea: The thesis is the last sentence, not the first. The opening grants that summaries work; the real point comes after 'But' and lands on 'not to replace your reading but to sharpen it.'
skillTips.inference: The author never calls summaries bad. What 'keeps the understanding for the machine' implies: the loss is hidden because the answer is still correct. The cost is what you didn't build.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Journey' and 'destination' aren't about travel. Destination = the summary handed to you; journey = reading it yourself. Decode each abstract word by its paired opposite.
skillTips.detail: For what full reading gives that a summary can't, gather the list after 'you watch how the author builds it': where evidence is strong, where it 'leans on assumption,' what 'made you pause.'

passages.title: Why Sleep Finishes Your Reading
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: When you finish a chapter, your brain is not done with it. During sleep, especially deep slow-wave sleep, the hippocampus replays the day's experiences and gradually transfers the durable parts to the cortex for long-term storage. This is why a passage that felt slippery at night can seem clearer the next morning: the material was reorganized while you rested. The practical lesson is that cramming fights your own biology. Reading a little each day, with sleep between sessions, lets each night's consolidation lock in what the previous day's effort began. Spacing your practice is not a sign of weak commitment; it is the schedule the memory system was built to use.

questions.stem: According to the passage, what does the hippocampus do during deep sleep?
choices: Replays experiences and transfers durable parts to the cortex
questions.explanation: The passage states the hippocampus "replays the day's experiences and gradually transfers the durable parts to the cortex."

questions.stem: What practical advice follows from the science described?
choices: Cram all your reading into one long session
Read a little each day with sleep between sessions
Reading does not benefit from rest
questions.explanation: Because consolidation happens during sleep, spaced daily reading "lets each night's consolidation lock in" the prior day's effort.

questions.stem: "Consolidation," as used here, most nearly means:
choices: the strengthening and storing of memories
questions.explanation: The passage uses "consolidation" for the process by which the brain stabilizes and stores what was learned.

lesson.strategy: A science explainer that moves from mechanism to advice. First track what the brain does during sleep: it replays experiences, then transfers the durable parts. Then find the two payoff sentences that turn that biology into a study rule. Let the causal words carry you.

signals.phrase: your brain is not done with it
signals.means: Opening claim: reading continues after you stop. Everything after explains this.

signals.phrase: This is why
signals.means: Causal hinge: links the sleep mechanism to the everyday result of a passage feeling clearer.

signals.phrase: cramming fights your own biology
signals.means: The turning point: the science is now converted into a rule about how to study.

signals.phrase: not a sign of weak commitment
signals.means: Corrects a wrong assumption: spacing looks lazy but is what memory needs.

vocab.inContext: figurative: a passage that felt hard to grasp or hold onto in your mind at night
the brain's overnight work of locking new material into lasting memory
the lasting, worth-keeping parts of the day the brain moves to long-term storage

skillTips.main-idea: Don't stop at the sleep science. The point arrives late: "cramming fights your own biology" and the closing line on spacing. Read the mechanism as setup for that rule.
skillTips.inference: The passage never says "sleep before you review." Infer it from "felt slippery at night can seem clearer the next morning": the gain came from rest, not extra effort.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "slippery," don't picture something wet. It sits next to "felt" and contrasts with "clearer," so it means a passage that was hard to hold in your mind.
skillTips.detail: Follow the order: during deep slow-wave sleep the hippocampus "replays the day's experiences," then "transfers the durable parts to the cortex." Replay first, transfer second.

passages.title: Reading the River
passages.source: Mark Twain, Life on the Mississippi (1883)
passages.attribution: Mark Twain, Life on the Mississippi (1883). Public domain.
passages.text: The face of the water, in time, became a wonderful book — a book that was a dead language to the uneducated passenger, but which told its mind to me without reserve, delivering its most cherished secrets as clearly as if it uttered them with a voice. And it was not a book to be read once and thrown aside, for it had a new story to tell every day. Throughout the long twelve hundred miles there was never a page that was void of interest, never one that you could leave unread without loss, never one that you would want to skip, thinking you could find higher enjoyment in some other thing. There never was so wonderful a book written by man; never one whose interest was so absorbing, so unflagging, so sparklingly renewed with every reperusal.

questions.stem: What does Twain mean by calling the river a "book"?
choices: He literally read books while on the river.
The river's surface held meaning that a trained eye could interpret.
The river was as boring as a textbook.
Passengers wrote about the river in journals.
questions.explanation: Twain treats the water's "face" as a text whose "secrets" he, unlike the untrained passenger, could read.

questions.stem: Why is the river "a dead language to the uneducated passenger"?
choices: Because passengers could not see the water
Because only trained skill lets one read its signs
Because the river had no features
Because passengers spoke a foreign language
questions.explanation: Like a language one hasn't learned, the river's meaning is inaccessible without the training Twain acquired.

questions.stem: "Unflagging" as used here most nearly means:
questions.explanation: Twain praises the river's "unflagging" interest — interest that never tires or diminishes.

lesson.strategy: One metaphor runs the whole passage: the river's surface is "a wonderful book." Hold that the whole way — "a new story to tell every day" and "never a page" describe reading water, not paper. Watch the "but" that splits two readers and the "never... never... never" pile-up.

signals.phrase: a wonderful book
signals.means: The controlling metaphor: the river surface is a book. Every later line describes reading water, not paper.

signals.phrase: but which told its mind to me
signals.means: The 'but' turn splits two readers: the passenger sees nothing, Twain reads everything. The contrast is the point.

signals.phrase: never a page that was void of interest, never one
signals.means: Anaphora: the stacked 'never' clauses prove the river-book is endlessly worth reading.

signals.phrase: so absorbing, so unflagging, so sparklingly renewed
signals.means: Piled superlatives close the argument, ranking this 'book' above any written by man.

vocab.inContext: holding back; the river told 'its mind to me without reserve,' hiding nothing.
empty of; a page 'void of interest' is a dull one, and Twain says there was none.
never tiring or weakening; the river-book's interest never fades on rereading.

skillTips.main-idea: The passage makes one claim through the book metaphor: the river was endlessly readable to Twain. Translate 'wonderful book' back to 'the river,' then say what reading it gave him.
skillTips.inference: Twain never says he trained for years, but the 'uneducated passenger' sees 'a dead language' while the river tells its secrets 'to me.' Infer his skill from what the passenger misses.
skillTips.vocabulary: Hard words sit next to their clue. 'Without reserve' is followed by 'delivering its most cherished secrets... clearly,' so 'reserve' means holding back. Read the neighbor phrase first.
skillTips.detail: The 'never a page' sentence packs three claims (void of interest / leave unread without loss / want to skip). For a detail question, match the exact one, not the general idea.

passages.title: A Truth Universally Acknowledged
passages.attribution: Jane Austen, Pride and Prejudice (1813). Public domain.
passages.text: It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife. However little known the feelings or views of such a man may be on his first entering a neighbourhood, this truth is so well fixed in the minds of the surrounding families, that he is considered the rightful property of some one or other of their daughters. "My dear Mr. Bennet," said his lady to him one day, "have you heard that Netherfield Park is let at last?" Mr. Bennet replied that he had not. "But it is," returned she; "for Mrs. Long has just been here, and she told me all about it." Mr. Bennet made no answer. "Do you not want to know who has taken it?" cried his wife impatiently. "You want to tell me, and I have no objection to hearing it." This was invitation enough.

questions.stem: The famous opening sentence is best understood as:
choices: Gentle irony about how families view wealthy bachelors
A legal rule of the time
questions.explanation: Austen's tone is ironic: the "truth" reflects the matchmaking assumptions of "surrounding families," not an actual law of nature.

questions.stem: What does the exchange reveal about Mr. Bennet?
choices: He is dryly amused and teases his wife.
He is angry at his wife.
He has no interest in his family.
questions.explanation: His reply — "You want to tell me, and I have no objection to hearing it" — shows a dry, teasing wit.

questions.stem: What news is Mrs. Bennet eager to share?
choices: That Netherfield Park has been rented
That the family has lost its fortune
questions.explanation: She asks whether he has heard "that Netherfield Park is let at last."

lesson.strategy: Read the opening "truth" as a joke, not a fact — the narrator mocks the marriage market. Then watch the Bennets act it out: Mrs. Bennet chases a rich new neighbor for her daughters while Mr. Bennet teases her by refusing to ask. Notice who really steers the talk.

signals.phrase: It is a truth universally acknowledged
signals.means: Mock-solemn opening; the narrator states a local prejudice as if it were a law of nature. Read the line as irony.

signals.phrase: rightful property of some one or other
signals.means: The joke's target: families treat a rich single man as prey to claim for a daughter, not a person with feelings.

signals.phrase: Mr. Bennet made no answer
signals.means: His silence is teasing, not indifference; he withholds to make his wife beg, showing the marriage he lives in.

signals.phrase: This was invitation enough
signals.means: Dry narrator wink: Mr. Bennet's tiny concession is all Mrs. Bennet needs — her eagerness does the rest.

vocab.inContext: here it means lack or need, not wish for — a rich man must be missing a wife
rented out to a tenant; Netherfield Park has finally been leased
wealth — a large amount of money and property

skillTips.main-idea: The point isn't the stated "truth"; it's that the narrator mocks how families scheme to marry daughters to rich men. Read the first sentence as irony, then watch the Bennets enact it.
skillTips.inference: The narrator never calls Mrs. Bennet scheming. Infer it from her rush to report "Netherfield Park is let" and her push to name who took it — she is sizing up a husband for a daughter.
skillTips.vocabulary: Old words shift meaning. "want of a wife" means lack, not desire; a house "let at last" means rented. Use the surrounding sentence to reset each word from its modern sense.
skillTips.detail: Track the back-and-forth: who speaks, who stays silent. "made no answer" and "cried his wife impatiently" show Mr. Bennet stalling and Mrs. Bennet losing patience.

passages.title: Two Kinds of Reading
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Not all reading is the same. Shallow reading scans for a single fact: a price, a time, a yes or no. It is fast and useful, and we do it dozens of times a day. Deep reading is different. It asks you to build a model in your head of what the writer is saying, to test it against what you already know, and to carry an idea from one paragraph into the next. Both kinds matter, but only one of them grows with practice. You can scan your whole life and never get better at scanning. Deep reading, by contrast, rewards effort: the more demanding texts you finish, the more easily you finish the next one. A good daily habit is small but deep — one short passage read with full attention beats an hour of skimming.

questions.stem: What is the main point of the passage?
choices: Deep reading is a skill that improves with practice, unlike scanning.
Skimming for an hour is the best habit.
questions.explanation: The passage contrasts the two and stresses that "only one of them grows with practice" — deep reading rewards effort.

questions.stem: According to the passage, what does deep reading ask you to do?
choices: Scan for a single fact quickly
Build a mental model, test it, and carry ideas across paragraphs
questions.explanation: Deep reading asks you to "build a model," "test it," and "carry an idea from one paragraph into the next."

questions.stem: What daily habit does the author recommend?
choices: One short passage read with full attention
questions.explanation: The closing advice: "one short passage read with full attention beats an hour of skimming."

lesson.strategy: This essay compares two kinds of reading, then picks one. Read the first part as shallow, the second as deep, and hunt for the sentence that says how they differ. Words like 'different,' 'but,' and 'by contrast' mark the turn where the writer makes the point.

signals.phrase: Deep reading is different
signals.means: Writer leaves shallow reading and turns to the other kind — compare the two side by side from here.

signals.phrase: but only one of them grows with practice
signals.means: The 'but' carries the main point: the two kinds are equal except in one key way the essay then explains.

signals.phrase: by contrast
signals.means: Flags the sentence naming deep reading's edge — it rewards effort while scanning never improves.

signals.phrase: beats an hour of skimming
signals.means: The writer's final verdict: short, deep reading is worth more than long, shallow reading.

vocab.inContext: Surface reading that grabs one fact, like a price or a time.
Texts that ask hard effort; the harder ones make the next read easier.
Reading fast over the surface — the same idea as scanning, not deep reading.

skillTips.main-idea: The essay argues one point by comparing. Find 'Both kinds matter, but only one of them grows with practice' — the main idea is that conclusion, not a fact about either kind alone.
skillTips.inference: 'You can scan your whole life and never get better at scanning' states a fact but leaves the 'so what' unsaid. What it implies about which reading is worth practicing is the inference.
skillTips.vocabulary: For a hard word like 'demanding,' read what sits next to it. The text pairs it with 'rewards effort' and 'the more easily you finish the next one,' which restate the meaning.
skillTips.detail: When a question asks what deep reading does, go to its list: 'build a model in your head,' 'test it against what you already know,' 'carry an idea from one paragraph into the next.'

passages.title: Learning to Read
passages.source: Frederick Douglass, Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass (1845)
passages.attribution: Frederick Douglass, Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave (1845). Public domain.
passages.text: Very soon after I went to live with Mr. and Mrs. Auld, she very kindly commenced to teach me the A, B, C. After I had learned this, she assisted me in learning to spell words of three or four letters. Just at this point of my progress, Mr. Auld found out what was going on, and at once forbade Mrs. Auld to instruct me further, telling her, among other things, that it was unlawful, as well as unsafe, to teach a slave to read. To use his own words, further, he said, 'If you give a slave an inch, he will take an ell. A slave should know nothing but to obey his master — to do as he is told to do. Learning would spoil the best slave in the world.' These words sank deep into my heart, stirred up sentiments within that lay slumbering, and called into existence an entirely new train of thought. It was a new and special revelation, explaining dark and mysterious things, with which my youthful understanding had struggled, but struggled in vain. I now understood what had been to me a most perplexing difficulty — namely, the white man's power to enslave the black man. From that moment, I understood the pathway from slavery to freedom. Though conscious of the difficulty of learning without a teacher, I set out with high hope, and a fixed purpose, at whatever cost of trouble, to learn how to read.

questions.stem: Why did Mr. Auld forbid his wife from teaching Douglass to read?
choices: He thought Douglass was too young to learn
He believed literacy would make an enslaved person harder to control
He wanted to teach Douglass himself
He thought reading was a waste of time
questions.explanation: Auld said learning would "spoil" a slave and that one taught an inch would "take an ell" — he feared literacy would undermine his control.

questions.stem: What realization did Auld's words spark in Douglass?
choices: That his mistress had been unkind
That reading was the pathway from slavery to freedom
That he should obey his master
That spelling was easier than reading
questions.explanation: Douglass writes, "From that moment, I understood the pathway from slavery to freedom" — the very thing Auld feared revealed reading's power to him.

questions.stem: In "give him an inch, he will take an ell," an "ell" (an old unit of length) is used to mean:
questions.explanation: An ell is far longer than an inch; the saying means a small concession will be stretched into a large one.

lesson.strategy: A first-person memoir, so read for the reversal: the master forbids the lessons, and that ban becomes Douglass's real lesson. Track the line from Auld's warning to Douglass turning it into his own plan. The argument ends not in a feeling but in a decision to act.

signals.phrase: forbade Mrs. Auld to instruct me further
signals.means: The turning point. The lessons stop here, but this ban is what ends up teaching Douglass the deeper lesson.

signals.phrase: These words sank deep into my heart
signals.means: Marks the shift from what Auld said to what Douglass understood. His new insight starts in this sentence.

signals.phrase: the pathway from slavery to freedom
signals.means: The point he arrives at: reading is the road out. This is why the whole memoir matters.

signals.phrase: a fixed purpose
signals.means: His resolve at the close. Notice the passage ends in action, not just realization.

vocab.inContext: a length far longer than an inch; 'give an inch, take an ell' means small becomes large.
a sudden clear truth that made hidden, 'dark and mysterious things' finally make sense.
sleeping; his feelings lay asleep until Auld's words woke them.

skillTips.main-idea: The main idea isn't Auld's warning itself. Follow the line from 'forbade' to 'the pathway from slavery to freedom': the ban is what showed Douglass why reading matters.
skillTips.inference: Auld says reading would 'spoil the best slave' — the opposite of freeing him. Read his fear against his intent: his warning reveals the very thing he wants kept hidden.
skillTips.vocabulary: For 'ell', use its paired word 'inch' in the same proverb. One is small, one is large, so 'give an inch, take an ell' means a small gift leads to a big taking.
skillTips.detail: For what changed in Douglass, look right after 'sank deep into my heart': he lists the effects — stirred sentiments, a new 'train of thought', seeing the 'white man's power'.

passages.title: The Allegory of the Cave
passages.source: Plato, The Republic, Book VII (c. 375 BC)
passages.attribution: Plato, The Republic, trans. Benjamin Jowett (1871). Public domain.
passages.text: And now let me show in a figure how far our nature is enlightened or unenlightened. Behold human beings living in an underground den; here they have been from their childhood, and have their legs and necks chained so that they cannot move, and can only see before them, being prevented by the chains from turning round their heads. Above and behind them a fire is blazing at a distance, and between the fire and the prisoners there is a raised way; and you will see, if you look, a low wall built along the way, like the screen which marionette players have in front of them, over which they show the puppets. And do you see men passing along the wall carrying vessels, and statues and figures of animals made of wood and stone? To the prisoners, the truth would be literally nothing but the shadows of these images cast upon the wall before them. And when one of them is liberated and compelled suddenly to stand up and turn his neck and walk and look towards the light, he will suffer sharp pains; the glare will distress him, and he will be unable to see the realities of which in his former state he had seen only the shadows. He will need to grow accustomed to the sight of the upper world, and only by degrees will he come to see the things themselves.

questions.stem: What do the shadows on the wall represent for the prisoners?
choices: Frightening illusions they wish to escape
The only reality they have ever known
questions.explanation: Having seen nothing else, the prisoners take the shadows to be "literally nothing but" the truth — the shadows are their entire reality.

questions.stem: What happens when a prisoner is first turned toward the light?
choices: He suffers pain and cannot yet see the real objects
He returns to his chains at once
He becomes invisible to the others
questions.explanation: The text says he "will suffer sharp pains," the glare distresses him, and he is at first "unable to see the realities."

questions.stem: The allegory is best understood as an image of:
choices: The painful, gradual passage from illusion to genuine knowledge
Why prisoners should not be freed
The superiority of firelight to sunlight
questions.explanation: Plato uses the freed prisoner's difficult adjustment to the light as a figure for how the mind moves, slowly and uncomfortably, from appearances to truth.

lesson.strategy: Read this as one extended metaphor, not a story about a real cave. When Socrates says "show in a figure," each object stands for an idea: the chains, the fire, the shadows. Track the one turn — a prisoner is "liberated" — and note the escape is painful and slow.

signals.phrase: show in a figure
signals.means: Flags the whole scene as a metaphor: read each object as standing for an idea, not a literal thing.

signals.phrase: the truth would be literally nothing
signals.means: The core claim: prisoners mistake shadows for reality because shadows are all they have ever seen.

signals.phrase: And when one of them is liberated
signals.means: The pivot. The passage turns from the trap to the painful, forced climb toward the light.

signals.phrase: only by degrees will he come to see
signals.means: Real understanding comes slowly and by stages, not in one sudden leap.

vocab.inContext: A metaphor or picture that stands for an idea — not a number or shape here.
Forced; the freed prisoner does not choose to rise, he is made to.
Steps or stages — "by degrees" means gradually, a little at a time.

skillTips.main-idea: Look past the cave scene to what it argues about knowledge. Ask what "nothing but the shadows" says about people who never leave the den — the figure claims we mistake appearance for truth.
skillTips.inference: The text never says shadows mean false beliefs; you infer it. Match each image to a meaning: the chained prisoners accept what is before them; the "glare" shows how hard new truth is.
skillTips.vocabulary: This old translation gives common words odd senses: "figure" means a metaphor, "compelled" means forced. Test a guess by swapping your meaning into the sentence to see if it fits.
skillTips.detail: The details are the trap's mechanics: chains stop the prisoners turning "round their heads," the wall works "like the screen" puppet players use. That is why only shadows reach them.

passages.title: The Lost Art of Finishing
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
There is a particular satisfaction that comes only from finishing something difficult, and it is disappearing from our lives one notification at a time. Consider what reading a long, demanding chapter actually requires. You must hold the beginning in mind as you reach the end. You must resist the dozen small urges to check something, to look away, to do anything other than stay with the single thread of an argument as it slowly tightens. None of this is comfortable, and that discomfort is precisely the point. The mind, like the body, grows only against resistance.

Modern tools are engineered to remove resistance. They deliver the answer before you have formed the question, summarize the book before you have opened it, and interrupt any sustained effort with something brighter and easier. Each individual convenience is harmless. Taken together, over years, they quietly retrain us to expect that understanding should arrive without struggle — and understanding that arrives without struggle rarely stays.

The cure is not to abandon these tools, which are genuinely useful, but to deliberately reserve some part of each day for the slow work they cannot do for you. Read one hard thing to the end. Sit with a paragraph you did not grasp the first time, and read it again. Notice the moment you want to quit, and continue just past it. Finishing is a skill, and like every skill it is built by finishing — not by intending to, not by summarizing, but by actually crossing the last line of something that asked more of you than you wanted to give.

questions.stem: What is the passage's central argument?
choices: Modern tools should be abandoned entirely.
Effortful reading builds an understanding that easy, summarized consumption cannot.
Finishing books is no longer necessary.
Comfort is the goal of reading.
questions.explanation: The author argues that the discomfort of sustained reading is "precisely the point," and that understanding without struggle "rarely stays."

questions.stem: Why does the author say "that discomfort is precisely the point"?
choices: Because the mind grows only against resistance, like a muscle
Because difficult books are better written
Because discomfort means you are reading too fast
questions.explanation: The next line makes the analogy explicit: "The mind, like the body, grows only against resistance."

questions.stem: What does the author recommend doing about modern tools?
choices: Abandon them, since they are harmful
Keep using them, but reserve daily time for the slow reading they can't do for you
Only use them to summarize books
questions.explanation: The cure "is not to abandon these tools" but "to deliberately reserve some part of each day for the slow work they cannot do for you."

lesson.strategy: An argument essay with a clear turn. First find the claim: struggle is what builds understanding. Watch the middle blame 'Modern tools' for removing that struggle, then 'The cure is not... but...' flips to what you should do. The mind/body line is the argument in miniature.

signals.phrase: that discomfort is precisely the point
signals.means: The thesis: the hard, uncomfortable part IS the value, not a flaw to remove.

signals.phrase: Modern tools are engineered to remove resistance
signals.means: Names the antagonist. What follows lists how tools do it and why that harms you.

signals.phrase: The cure is not to abandon
signals.means: The turn. A not/but structure: reject the easy fix, then state what to actually do.

signals.phrase: grows only against resistance
signals.means: The mind/body analogy that carries the argument — effort, not comfort, is what makes you grow.

vocab.inContext: friction or effort you must push against; here, the hard work the mind needs to grow.
kept going over time without stopping — effort held on one thing.
on purpose, by choice — setting aside slow work rather than letting it happen.

skillTips.main-idea: The claim opens and closes: finishing is 'disappearing from our lives,' and 'Finishing is a skill... built by finishing.' Read those two together; the middle explains why.
skillTips.inference: The author never says stop. He calls tools 'genuinely useful' and the cure 'not to abandon' them. His real view is balance, not rejection — read what he keeps and what he warns against.
skillTips.vocabulary: Hard words are decoded by the mind/body comparison. For 'resistance,' see 'like the body, grows only against resistance' — a muscle needs weight, so the mind needs difficulty.
skillTips.detail: When a question asks what to do, go to the last paragraph. It gives three steps: 'Read one hard thing to the end,' 'Sit with a paragraph you did not grasp,' and 'continue just past it.'

passages.title: The Last Bus
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Mara watched the bus pull away from the stop, its red lights shrinking into the dark. She had been three steps too slow. For a moment she just stood there, her bag heavy on her shoulder, the rain starting again. Then she laughed, a small surprised sound. Tomorrow she would have walked the long way home and arrived tired and grumpy. Now she had a reason to call her sister, who lived two blocks away and always kept the kettle warm. She turned up her collar and headed down the side street, almost glad the bus was gone.

questions.stem: How does Mara feel by the end of the passage?
choices: Quietly pleased about a change of plans
questions.explanation: She laughs and is 'almost glad the bus was gone' because missing it gives her a reason to visit her sister.

questions.stem: Why does Mara decide to go down the side street?
choices: To wait for the next bus
To visit her sister who lives nearby
To get out of the rain at a shop
questions.explanation: The text says her sister lives two blocks away and she heads off to call her, turning toward the side street.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'grumpy' most nearly means
questions.explanation: She imagines arriving 'tired and grumpy,' meaning bad-tempered or irritable.

lesson.strategy: This is a short story about one small moment. Mara misses the last bus, which feels bad at first. Then her feelings flip. Read to watch her mood change from disappointed to "almost glad," and notice the reason that flips it.

signals.phrase: three steps too slow
signals.means: Shows she just missed the bus. This is the bad moment the story starts from.

signals.phrase: Then she laughed
signals.means: The turn. Her feeling starts to change from upset to something lighter.

signals.phrase: a reason to call her sister
signals.means: The good thing she finds. This is why missing the bus stops feeling bad.

signals.phrase: almost glad the bus was gone
signals.means: The ending mood. She now feels okay, even happy, about what went wrong.

vocab.inContext: in a bad mood, cross and unhappy; how she would have felt after the long walk
the folded part of her coat near the neck; she turns it up against the rain
a pot for boiling water to make tea; her sister keeps it warm for visitors

skillTips.main-idea: The story is not really about a bus. Ask: what changes inside Mara from the first line to the last? She goes from missing the bus to feeling 'almost glad.' That change is the point.
skillTips.inference: The words never say 'sad' or 'happy.' Guess her feelings from what she does: standing still with a heavy bag in the rain feels low; laughing and planning to see her sister feels bright.
skillTips.vocabulary: Use the words nearby. 'Grumpy' sits right next to 'tired,' so both describe a bad way to feel after the long walk home. Let the neighbor word point you.
skillTips.detail: Watch the small facts that explain her change. Her sister 'lived two blocks away' and 'always kept the kettle warm' — these are why the missed bus turns into a good plan.

passages.title: The Spare Key
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Dev kept the spare key under the third flowerpot, the cracked one, because no one would think to look there. His neighbor, old Mr. Okafor, knew about it. So when Dev's phone buzzed at work with a photo of his front door standing open, he did not panic. The message under the photo read: Your cat got out. I put her back. Locked up. Key returned. Dev set down his sandwich and smiled at the screen. He had never told Mr. Okafor he was grateful for these small rescues. He decided that tonight he would knock on the old man's door and finally say it.

questions.stem: Why does Dev NOT panic when he sees the photo of his open door?
choices: He knew the door was broken
He trusts Mr. Okafor, who has the key
He does not own a cat
He was already on his way home
questions.explanation: Mr. Okafor knows where the spare key is, and the message shows he handled the problem, so Dev stays calm.

questions.stem: What does Dev's plan to knock on the door suggest about him?
choices: He wants to scold his neighbor
He feels he has overlooked thanking someone kind
He no longer trusts Mr. Okafor
questions.explanation: He realizes he 'never told' the old man he was grateful and decides to finally say it.

questions.stem: Here, 'rescues' refers to
choices: helpful acts that fix small problems
questions.explanation: The 'small rescues' are Mr. Okafor's quiet acts of help, like returning the cat and locking up.

lesson.strategy: Fiction, and short. A photo of an open door sounds like trouble, but Dev "did not panic." Track his feelings, not the danger: watch how the story moves from that photo to his smile to what he decides to do at the very end.

signals.phrase: he did not panic
signals.means: The scary photo is not a threat. This tells you Dev already trusts who sent it.

signals.phrase: smiled at the screen
signals.means: Mood shift: relief and warmth, not fear. The message is good news, not an alarm.

signals.phrase: grateful for these small rescues
signals.means: Names Dev's real feeling and hints he has kept it quiet until now.

signals.phrase: finally say it
signals.means: The turn. Dev decides to act on a thank-you he never gave before.

vocab.inContext: an extra key kept in case it is needed
small helpful acts that save you from a problem
feeling thankful for what someone did

skillTips.main-idea: The story builds to one choice at the end. Ask what changed in Dev from the photo to "finally say it." The point is about thanking a neighbor he never thanked.
skillTips.inference: Dev sees an open door but "did not panic." Ask why. The next lines tell you: Mr. Okafor knows the key spot and sent the fix. Read on to close the gap.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "small rescues," look at what Mr. Okafor actually did: put the cat back, locked up, returned the key. A rescue here is a small helpful save, not a big dramatic one.
skillTips.detail: Facts are exact. Where the key hides ("the third flowerpot, the cracked one"), who knows about it, and each line of the message. Reread the message to answer any "what happened" question.

passages.title: The Tide Pool
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: The boy crouched at the edge of the tide pool, holding his breath as if his breathing might scare the water. A small crab sidled out from under a rock, lifted one claw, and froze. His grandmother stood behind him, saying nothing. She had learned long ago that the surest way to make a child lose interest in a thing was to explain it to death. So she folded her hands and let the silence do its slow work. The boy leaned closer, then closer still, until his nose nearly touched the surface. A minute passed, then two. After a while he looked up, his eyes wide and his voice hushed with wonder, and whispered, "It's watching me back." His grandmother nodded slowly. That, she thought, was the whole lesson, and he had managed to teach it to himself without a single word from her. She would remember this small triumph longer than he would. The waves came in then, sliding up over the rocks and filling the pool to its brim, and the crab folded its claw and slipped quietly away into the deeper green.

questions.stem: Why does the grandmother stay quiet?
choices: She does not know anything about crabs
She is angry at the boy
She believes explaining too much would spoil his discovery
She cannot see what he is looking at
questions.explanation: The text says she learned that explaining everything makes a child lose interest, so she lets silence work.

questions.stem: What is the central idea of this passage?
choices: Discovery means more when a person finds it themselves
Grandmothers should always teach lessons aloud
questions.explanation: The grandmother sees the boy teach himself 'the whole lesson,' showing self-discovery as the point.

questions.stem: The word 'sidled' suggests the crab moved
questions.explanation: 'Sidled' means to move sideways in a wary, careful way, matching the crab freezing with a lifted claw.

lesson.strategy: Two people are here, but the story hides whose it is. Watch the boy find the crab, then see the grandmother's silence is a choice, not emptiness. At the end ask: who won? "Small triumph" and the last line point at her, not him.

signals.phrase: explain it to death
signals.means: Her silence is a teaching choice — she thinks talking would kill the boy's interest in the crab.

signals.phrase: let the silence do its slow work
signals.means: Her plan is working through waiting; the quiet is doing something on purpose, not just empty.

signals.phrase: It's watching me back
signals.means: The boy's turn — his hushed wonder shows he found that the pool feels alive on his own.

signals.phrase: this small triumph
signals.means: Reveals whose story this is: the win belongs to the grandmother, who taught without a word.

vocab.inContext: moved slowly sideways, the way a crab scuttles out from under a rock
lowered and soft, like a whisper full of wonder
a quiet victory — here, the grandmother's success at staying silent

skillTips.main-idea: The main idea is not the crab. Track why the grandmother stays silent and her thought at the end: 'that was the whole lesson.' A child learns best when nobody explains it to death.
skillTips.inference: Her thoughts are told; the boy's are shown. When he whispers 'It's watching me back' with eyes wide, infer what he felt — awe, wonder — from his actions, since the story never names it.
skillTips.vocabulary: Use the action around a hard word to unlock it. 'sidled out from under a rock' plus 'lifted one claw' shows a slow sideways crawl, so sidled must mean moved sideways.
skillTips.detail: Notice the frame: the crab appears at the start and 'slipped quietly away' at the end, while 'a minute passed, then two' and the rising waves mark how much time went by.

passages.title: The Understudy
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Nina had memorized every line of the play, though no one had asked her to. She was the understudy, the name printed in small type at the bottom of the program, the one who watched from the wings night after night while another actor took the bows. She told herself she did not mind, and most evenings she almost believed it. Then, an hour before curtain on a cold Thursday, the lead actor twisted her ankle on the stairs behind the dressing rooms. Word spread fast through the cramped backstage halls. The director went looking for Nina and found her already in costume, standing before the empty mirror, mouthing the opening speech under her breath. He stopped in the doorway. "You knew," he said, half a question. Nina only shrugged, but her hands were perfectly steady as she fixed her collar. She had been ready for months, ready in a way she had never let herself say out loud. The waiting, she realized as she stepped toward the stage, had not been wasted after all. All those silent nights in the wings had not been a punishment. They had been practice.

questions.stem: What does Nina mean by realizing the waiting 'had not been wasted'?
choices: She wishes she had quit the play
Her years of preparation made her ready for the chance
The lead actor planned to get hurt
questions.explanation: She had memorized every line and was already in costume; the waiting served as quiet preparation for this moment.

questions.stem: Why is the director's 'You knew' described as 'half a question'?
choices: He is unsure whether she heard him
He suspects she was secretly prepared but isn't certain
He is asking what time it is
He doubts the actor is really hurt
questions.explanation: He finds her already in costume rehearsing, so he half-suspects she was ready, but isn't fully sure.

questions.stem: In the theater, 'the wings' are
choices: the areas at the sides of the stage out of view
questions.explanation: Nina watches 'from the wings,' the offstage side areas where an understudy waits unseen.

lesson.strategy: Fiction: track the gap between what Nina shows and what she secretly does. She acts like the waiting does not bother her, but her actions say she prepared all along. Watch how the last line renames 'waiting' as 'practice' — let that final word carry the point.

signals.phrase: though no one had asked her to
signals.means: She prepares in secret, on her own drive. First clue she wants the role more than she admits.

signals.phrase: She told herself she did not mind
signals.means: A feeling she is talking herself into, not a fact. Trust her actions over this claim.

signals.phrase: her hands were perfectly steady
signals.means: She shrugs to hide it, but steady hands reveal she is ready and calm — the truth leaking out.

signals.phrase: They had been practice.
signals.means: The last line flips 'waiting' into 'practice.' The whole point sits in this reframe.

vocab.inContext: The backup actor who learns a role in case the lead can't perform — Nina's job here.
The hidden sides of the stage; where Nina watches, unseen by the audience, night after night.
The start of the show — 'an hour before curtain' means one hour before the play begins.

skillTips.main-idea: The point is not that Nina performs. Compare her view of the waiting at the start — a punishment she pretends not to mind — with the last two lines: the waiting became practice.
skillTips.inference: Nina never says she wants the lead. Read her actions: she memorized every line, and the director found her 'already in costume,' 'mouthing the opening speech.' Ready before she said so.
skillTips.vocabulary: For theater words, use the action nearby. She 'watched from the wings night after night' while 'another actor took the bows' — so the wings are offstage, out of the spotlight.
skillTips.detail: For a fact question, find the moment things turn: 'an hour before curtain on a cold Thursday, the lead actor twisted her ankle on the stairs.' Note the when, who, and how.

passages.title: Snow Day
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: The school called at six in the morning: closed, on account of snow. From under his blankets Tomas heard his father groan in the next room, already dialing work to say he could not come in, his voice tight with the particular irritation of a plan gone wrong. A whole day rearranged, the father muttered, just like that. Tomas pulled the covers higher and went back to sleep. But by nine, when the worst of the storm had passed and the street lay buried and silent, Tomas wandered downstairs and found the kitchen empty. Through the frosted window he saw his father outside in boots and an old coat, building a lopsided fort in the front yard, snow stuck to his eyebrows and a grin on his face. "I haven't done this in thirty years," his father admitted, patting another crooked wall into place. Tomas pulled on his own boots and knelt beside him without a word. They worked together until their fingers ached and their cheeks burned red, saying very little, and somewhere in the quiet of it Tomas understood something. The inconvenience his father had grumbled about at six that morning had, by nine, quietly turned into the best part of the entire week.

questions.stem: How does the father's attitude change over the course of the day?
choices: From annoyed at the disruption to enjoying it
From tired to even more tired
questions.explanation: He groans about missing work in the morning, but by nine he is happily building a fort, calling it the best part of the week.

questions.stem: What does the passage mainly suggest?
choices: An unwelcome interruption can become a gift
Work is more important than play
questions.explanation: The closed school first reads as an inconvenience but becomes a cherished shared moment.

questions.stem: Here, 'lopsided' means
choices: uneven or tilted to one side
questions.explanation: A 'lopsided' fort is built unevenly, leaning to one side, fitting the playful, imperfect effort.

lesson.strategy: This is a mood-flip story. The father starts angry about the snow day, then ends up loving it. Watch the clock: how he feels at "six" versus "nine." The change in the father is the whole point, not the snow itself.

signals.phrase: his voice tight with the particular irritation
signals.means: The father begins annoyed. This sets up a feeling that will change later.

signals.phrase: But by nine
signals.means: The turn word. The story pivots here from the father's complaint to him playing.

signals.phrase: a grin on his face
signals.means: Shows the mood has flipped. The morning irritation is gone; now he is happy.

signals.phrase: the best part of the entire week
signals.means: The payoff. States plainly what the annoying day turned into.

vocab.inContext: leaning to one side, not straight or even; the fort is crooked but built with joy
something that ruins your plan; here, the closed school that changed the father's day
said something a bit embarrassing out loud, like not having played in thirty years

skillTips.main-idea: The whole passage tracks one change: how the father feels about the snow day from six to nine. Follow that change from irritation to joy, not the storm. That arc is the main idea.
skillTips.inference: The father and Tomas say "very little," yet Tomas "understood something." Read the actions, kneeling beside him, working till fingers ache, to feel the closeness the words never state.
skillTips.vocabulary: Use the nearby words for hard ones. "lopsided" sits right by "crooked wall," so both mean not straight or uneven. Let one word explain the other.
skillTips.detail: The times anchor the story: "six" and "nine." Match each feeling to its time, grumbling at six, grinning at nine, to keep the before and after straight.

passages.title: The Lighthouse Keeper
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: For nineteen years Halloran had kept the light burning on the rock at the harbor mouth, and in all that time not a single ship had wrecked on his watch. The town considered this proof of nothing. Ships did not wreck, they said, because the sea here was calm; the light was a courtesy, a habit, an old man's company in the dark. Halloran did not argue. He climbed the spiral stairs each evening, trimmed the wick, and polished the great lens until it threw its beam clean across the water. He knew what the townsfolk could not: that the absence of disaster is the hardest kind of work to see. No one thanks the wall that holds back the flood until the day it falls. So he kept it standing, asking nothing, and slept well, because he understood the value of a thing that never has to prove itself by failing.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of this passage?
choices: Quiet, preventive work often goes unnoticed because nothing goes wrong
The townsfolk are cruel to Halloran
questions.explanation: The passage argues that 'the absence of disaster is the hardest kind of work to see,' praising unseen prevention.

questions.stem: Why does Halloran not argue with the townsfolk?
choices: He agrees the light is useless
He is too shy to speak
He is content knowing his work's true worth even unrecognized
He plans to leave the town soon
questions.explanation: He 'asks nothing' and 'sleeps well,' content in his private understanding of the light's value.

questions.stem: What does the image of 'the wall that holds back the flood' illustrate?
choices: That floods are common in the town
That protective work is only appreciated after it fails
That walls are stronger than lighthouses
That Halloran fears a flood is coming
questions.explanation: The line says no one thanks the wall 'until the day it falls,' mirroring the unseen value of the light.
