This pattern is known as the tragedy of the commons, and it appears wherever a shared resource has no limits on its use: fisheries emptied of fish, air thickened with smoke, roads choked with traffic.

The lesson is not that people are wicked. The herders are not villains; they are responding sensibly to the incentives in front of them. The failure lies in the structure. When the benefits of use are private but the costs are shared, individual good sense produces collective harm. Solutions usually require changing that structure, by assigning ownership, setting enforceable limits, or charging for use, so that the cost of taking more lands on the one who takes it.

questions.stem: What is the central point of the passage?
choices: Herders are greedy and should be punished
Shared resources with no limits get overused because costs are spread while gains are private
Meadows are the best place to raise cattle
People always act against their own interests
questions.explanation: The passage defines the tragedy of the commons: private gains plus shared costs lead to overuse and collective harm.

questions.stem: Based on the passage, why do the proposed solutions focus on structure rather than character?
choices: Because changing laws is cheaper than educating people
Because the harm comes from incentives, not from people being wicked
Because herders cannot understand the problem
Because shared resources can never be saved
questions.explanation: The text stresses that herders are not villains; the failure is in the structure of incentives, so fixes target structure.

questions.stem: Which example of the tragedy does the passage give?
questions.explanation: The passage lists 'fisheries emptied of fish' as an example of a shared resource being overused.

lesson.strategy: Read this as an argument, not a story. The meadow just sets it up; the real claim lands at "The failure lies in the structure." Track how "private" gain and "shared" cost drive each herder, then see every fix change that structure.

signals.phrase: The trouble is
signals.means: The turn: reasonable individual logic becomes collective disaster once everyone reasons the same way.

signals.phrase: The lesson is not
signals.means: The writer blocks a wrong reading (people are wicked) before naming the real cause.

signals.phrase: The failure lies in the structure
signals.means: The thesis: blame the incentive setup, not the herders. Everything else supports this line.

signals.phrase: Solutions usually require
signals.means: Shift to the fix; each remedy works by changing the private-benefit, shared-cost structure.

vocab.inContext: a resource open to everyone with no limits on use, like the "free of charge" meadow
the rewards and costs "in front of" a person that steer their choices
destructive; grazing sensible for one "turns out to be ruinous for all"

skillTips.main-idea: Don't stop at the meadow. Find where it names the cause: "The failure lies in the structure" and "individual good sense produces collective harm." The point is structure, not grass.
skillTips.inference: Herders are "not villains," so the setup causes overgrazing. Each herder gets "clear private gain" while the cost is "spread across the whole village" — gains fully, pays a sliver.
skillTips.vocabulary: "Commons" is never defined directly. The opening line, "a meadow open to every herder... free of charge," shows it means a resource anyone can use with no limits.
skillTips.detail: The pattern isn't only grass. For concrete cases, scan the list: "fisheries emptied of fish, air thickened with smoke, roads choked with traffic."

passages.title: The Loop Behind a Habit
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
A habit is a behavior the brain has learned to run on autopilot. It usually forms around a simple loop with three parts: a cue, a routine, and a reward. The cue is a trigger, such as a time of day or a feeling. The routine is the action itself. The reward is the payoff that tells the brain the loop is worth remembering.

When the same loop repeats often enough, the brain stops deliberating and lets the cue pull the routine along almost automatically. This saves effort, which is the whole point: habits free attention for harder tasks. But the same machinery that builds helpful habits also builds harmful ones, because the brain does not judge whether a reward is good for you, only whether it feels rewarding.

This is why willpower alone rarely changes a habit. A more effective approach keeps the cue and the reward but swaps the routine in between for a better one. The trigger still fires, the payoff still arrives, but the action that connects them is replaced.

questions.stem: The passage mainly explains
choices: how habits form through a cue, routine, and reward loop
which rewards are good for health
how the brain rests during sleep
questions.explanation: The passage centers on the three-part habit loop and how it operates.

questions.stem: Why does the passage suggest keeping the cue and reward while changing the routine?
choices: Because cues and rewards cannot be removed
Because the loop still triggers and pays off, but with a better action between
Because routines are the easiest part to ignore
questions.explanation: The text says the trigger still fires and the payoff still arrives, but the routine in between is replaced.

questions.stem: According to the passage, what does the reward do?
choices: It signals to the brain that the loop is worth remembering
questions.explanation: The text states the reward is 'the payoff that tells the brain the loop is worth remembering.'

lesson.strategy: A psychology explainer built on one model: the three-part habit loop. Pin down cue, routine, and reward first, then follow the two turns — "But the same machinery" (the same process builds bad habits too) and "This is why willpower alone rarely" (so swap the routine, keep the other two).

signals.phrase: a cue, a routine, and a reward
signals.means: The three parts of the loop; the next three sentences define each one in order — this is the backbone.

signals.phrase: But the same machinery
signals.means: The turn: the same process that builds helpful habits also builds harmful ones.

signals.phrase: This is why willpower alone rarely
signals.means: The payoff line — it uses the loop to explain why willpower fails and points to the real fix.

signals.phrase: keeps the cue and the reward but swaps the routine
signals.means: The solution stated exactly: change the routine, keep the other two parts.

vocab.inContext: a trigger that starts the loop, such as a time of day or a feeling
thinking a choice over; the brain stops doing this once a loop repeats enough
the brain's habit-building process, pictured as a machine that runs good or bad the same way

skillTips.main-idea: The first sentence defines a habit; the last paragraph says what to do about one. The passage circles one model — the cue-routine-reward loop and how to change it.
skillTips.inference: The brain "does not judge whether a reward is good for you, only whether it feels rewarding." Chain that to the loop: a bad routine that feels rewarding still gets remembered.
skillTips.vocabulary: For cue, routine, and reward, read the sentence right after each is named — "The cue is a trigger...", "The routine is the action...", "The reward is the payoff...".
skillTips.detail: Details come in threes and in what-stays-vs-what-changes. Track which two the fix keeps (cue, reward) and which one it swaps (routine). Match a question to the exact part it names.

passages.title: Where Price Comes From
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
Prices are not set by a single person. They emerge from a tug-of-war between two forces: how much of a thing is available and how much people want it. These forces are called supply and demand.

When many people want a product but little of it exists, the price rises. Buyers compete, and sellers can ask for more. When a product is everywhere but few people want it, the price falls, because sellers must lower it to attract anyone at all. Most of the time the two forces pull toward a balance, a price where the amount offered roughly matches the amount wanted.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: How supply and demand together shape prices
questions.explanation: The passage explains that price emerges from the interaction of supply and demand.

questions.stem: Based on the passage, what would likely happen to the price of umbrellas during a long drought?
choices: It would likely fall, since fewer people want them
It would stay exactly the same
It would be set by the government
questions.explanation: Lower demand pushes price down, as the passage says sellers must lower prices when few people want a product.

questions.stem: In the passage, 'emerge' most nearly means
questions.explanation: Prices 'emerge from a tug-of-war,' meaning they arise out of those forces.

lesson.strategy: This explains where a price comes from. The first two sentences give the answer: two forces, supply and demand. The middle two are mirror cases — price rises, then falls. The last sentence lands on balance. Read it as answer, two examples, then the middle point.

signals.phrase: tug-of-war between two forces
signals.means: The core image: price is a pull between what's available (supply) and what people want (demand).

signals.phrase: the price rises
signals.means: Case one — lots of want, little supply. Buyers compete, so sellers can charge more.

signals.phrase: the price falls
signals.means: Case two — lots of supply, little want. Sellers drop the price to attract buyers. Mirror of case one.

signals.phrase: pull toward a balance
signals.means: The landing point: the two forces usually settle where amount offered matches amount wanted.

vocab.inContext: come out on their own — prices are not set by one person, they come from the two forces
pull buyers in — sellers lower the price to attract anyone at all
an even middle point where the amount offered roughly matches the amount wanted

skillTips.main-idea: The answer is in the first two sentences: two forces, supply and demand. To state the main idea, join those forces to the ending 'balance', not just one example.
skillTips.inference: The two 'When...' sentences show a pattern, not one fact. Much want + little supply pushes price up; much supply + little want pushes it down. Infer the rule from both cases.
skillTips.vocabulary: Hard words sit next to their own clue. 'emerge from a tug-of-war' means come out of it; 'lower it to attract anyone' means pull buyers in. Read the words around the hard word.
skillTips.detail: The passage gives two opposite cases. For a detail question, match the exact condition first — 'few people want it' goes with 'the price falls', not rises. Pick the case that fits.

passages.title: The Price of Your Attention
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
Many of the apps and sites people use every day cost nothing to open. This generosity has a hidden logic. The companies behind them do not sell a product to you; they sell you, or rather your attention, to advertisers. The longer you stay and the more often you return, the more there is to sell.

This arrangement quietly reshapes the design of the things we use. A tool built mainly to be useful would help you finish a task and leave. A tool built to capture attention does the opposite: it is engineered to keep you scrolling. Endless feeds, autoplaying videos, and notifications timed to pull you back are not accidents. They are the natural result of measuring success by time spent rather than tasks completed.

None of this requires bad intentions. Designers are simply responding to what they are rewarded for. If a small change makes people linger a few seconds longer, it spreads, while a change that helps people leave satisfied may be quietly discarded. Over millions of users, those tiny choices accumulate into systems that are very good at holding us and not always good for us. Understanding the incentive does not switch it off, but it does let you watch the machinery work and decide, more deliberately, how much of yourself to feed it.

questions.stem: The central idea of the passage is that
choices: free apps are a gift to users with no catch
when attention is the product, apps are designed to hold us rather than help us finish
advertisers dislike apps that are free
designers intend to harm their users
questions.explanation: The passage argues that selling attention to advertisers shapes design toward holding users, not serving them.

questions.stem: Why does the passage say a feature that helps people 'leave satisfied' might be discarded?
choices: It is too expensive to build
Success is measured by time spent, so features that reduce time spread less
questions.explanation: Because success is measured by time spent, changes that make people linger spread, while ones that help people leave are discarded.

questions.stem: As used in the passage, 'accumulate' most nearly means
choices: build up into a larger whole
questions.explanation: Tiny choices 'accumulate into systems,' meaning they build up into something larger.

lesson.strategy: This is an argument. The first sentence is bait, not the thesis. Find the real claim about who the product is, then track how design follows the money. Watch the turn at 'None of this requires bad intentions' — blame shifts from people to the reward system. End on what you can actually do.

signals.phrase: sell you, or rather your attention, to advertisers
signals.means: The thesis: you are the product being sold, not the buyer. Everything after explains this.

signals.phrase: not accidents
signals.means: Feeds and notifications are deliberate results of the incentive, not sloppy or accidental design.

signals.phrase: None of this requires bad intentions
signals.means: The pivot. Blame shifts from villains to a reward system that produces the outcome on its own.

signals.phrase: does not switch it off
signals.means: The qualified conclusion: awareness cannot stop the machinery, only let you choose how much to feed it.

vocab.inContext: The reward driving design — keeping you longer, since that is what advertisers pay for.
Tiny per-user choices build up over millions into powerful attention-holding systems.
Making a conscious choice about your attention instead of being pulled in automatically.

skillTips.main-idea: The main idea is not the first sentence. Combine 'they sell you... your attention, to advertisers' with the ending: free apps are shaped by what advertisers reward, not what helps you.
skillTips.inference: The text calls features 'not accidents' and says designers respond 'to what they are rewarded for.' Infer the stance: no villain is needed — the reward system produces the harm by itself.
skillTips.vocabulary: For 'incentive,' read the sentence around it: 'responding to what they are rewarded for' defines it. Let the cause-and-effect language nearby pin the word's meaning.
skillTips.detail: Separate claim from example. 'Endless feeds, autoplaying videos, and notifications' are concrete cases of the claim that tools are 'engineered to keep you scrolling.' Tie each to its claim.

passages.title: Why the Crowd Attracts a Crowd
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
Some products become more valuable the more people use them. A single telephone is useless; there is no one to call. A million telephones form a system worth far more than the sum of its parts. This pattern is called a network effect.

The pull is powerful. Once a service has many users, newcomers join because that is where everyone already is, and each new member makes it more attractive to the next. The same force can trap people, too. A product may keep its lead not because it is best but because everyone is already there, and no one wants to be first to leave. This is why strong networks are hard to challenge: a rival must offer not just a better tool, but a reason for the whole crowd to move at once.

questions.stem: What is the main idea?
choices: Some products grow more valuable as more people use them, which both attracts and traps users
The best product always wins the market
questions.explanation: The passage defines network effects and shows how they attract newcomers and lock users in.

questions.stem: Why is the strongest network hard for a rival to defeat?
choices: A rival must convince the whole crowd to move at once, not just build a better tool
questions.explanation: The text says a rival must offer a reason for the whole crowd to move together, not merely a better tool.

questions.stem: According to the passage, why is a single telephone useless?
choices: There is no one to call
It cannot connect to the internet
questions.explanation: The passage states 'A single telephone is useless; there is no one to call.'

lesson.strategy: An explainer that defines one idea, then complicates it. Read the telephone example to grab the core claim, then watch the turn at 'The same force can trap people, too' — the same pull that helps also locks people in. Hold both sides together.

signals.phrase: This pattern is called a network effect.
signals.means: The passage names its central idea. Everything before is the example; everything after explores this term.

signals.phrase: The same force can trap people, too.
signals.means: The turn. The writer flips from the good side (pull) to the bad side (lock-in) using the same cause.

signals.phrase: not because it is best
signals.means: The key contrast: a product can lead on crowd size, not quality. Watch the 'not X but Y' shape here.

signals.phrase: The pull is powerful.
signals.means: A short line that shifts the passage from defining the effect to showing what it does to people.

vocab.inContext: When a product gets more valuable as more people use it, like phones needing others to call.
To keep people stuck in a product because everyone else is there, not because it is best.
A competing product trying to win users away from the one already in the lead.

skillTips.main-idea: The main idea is not the telephone; the phones are proof. Ask what they prove: a product can gain value from having many users. Add the twist — that same pull can also trap people.
skillTips.inference: Why are strong networks 'hard to challenge'? Link two lines: people stay 'because everyone is already there,' and a rival needs 'the whole crowd to move at once.' The crowd is the wall.
skillTips.vocabulary: For 'network effect,' don't guess from the words 'network' and 'effect.' The passage defines it right after the telephone example: value that grows as more people use the product.
skillTips.detail: Details here come in pairs. 'A single telephone is useless' sits against 'A million telephones form a system.' When asked about a number or example, find its matching opposite nearby.

passages.title: The Tired Chooser
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
Making decisions feels weightless, but it draws on a limited supply of mental energy. After a long string of choices, that supply runs low, and the quality of our judgment quietly slips. Researchers call this decision fatigue.

When worn down, the mind looks for shortcuts. It may grab the first acceptable option, or avoid deciding at all and simply keep things as they are. Both are the mind conserving what little fuel remains. This explains why some people deliberately shrink the number of small decisions they face, by eating the same breakfast or laying out clothes the night before. Each trivial choice removed leaves a little more energy for the choices that truly matter.

questions.stem: The passage mainly argues that
choices: making many choices drains mental energy and lowers later judgment
breakfast is the most important meal
questions.explanation: The passage is about decision fatigue: choices draw on limited energy, weakening later judgment.

questions.stem: Why might someone wear the same outfit every day, according to the passage?
choices: To remove small decisions and save energy for important ones
questions.explanation: The text says removing trivial choices leaves more energy for choices that truly matter.

questions.stem: In the passage, 'conserving' most nearly means
questions.explanation: The mind takes shortcuts to conserve fuel, meaning to save what energy remains.

lesson.strategy: A short psychology explainer with one claim: choosing drains mental energy. Read it in two halves. First the claim and its name ("decision fatigue"); then the real-life proof after "This explains why." Ask at each example how it saves energy.

signals.phrase: feels weightless, but it draws on
signals.means: The "but" flips a false belief. The real claim: choosing feels free but spends a limited energy supply.

signals.phrase: the quality of our judgment quietly slips
signals.means: Names the cost. "Quietly" is the key word — the drop is real but you don't feel it happen.

signals.phrase: This explains why
signals.means: The turn from theory to proof. Everything after it is a real habit that the claim is meant to explain.

signals.phrase: Both are the mind conserving
signals.means: Labels the two shortcuts as one motive: grabbing the first option and not deciding both just save fuel.

vocab.inContext: seeming to cost nothing, no effort — though the passage says it secretly drains energy
small and unimportant; the everyday choices worth removing to save energy
saving or protecting the little energy the tired mind has left

skillTips.main-idea: One claim runs the passage: choosing spends limited mental energy, and running low hurts judgment. Say it yourself, then check the breakfast and clothes examples exist only to prove it.
skillTips.inference: The passage never says willpower drains like a battery — it shows it. Tie "conserving what little fuel remains" to the habits after it: cutting small choices leaves fuel for the big ones.
skillTips.vocabulary: The energy idea rides on metaphors, not plain words: "weightless," "fuel," "conserving," "runs low." When a word sounds physical here, read it as mental energy and the sentence opens up.
skillTips.detail: The two habits are exact: "eating the same breakfast" and "laying out clothes the night before." For a detail question, go back to these named examples, not your memory of the general idea.

passages.title: Memory as Rebuilding
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
We tend to imagine memory as a recording, a faithful video stored away and replayed on demand. The truth is stranger. Each time we remember something, we are not pressing play; we are rebuilding the event from scattered fragments, filling the gaps with assumptions about how things usually go.

This reconstruction is useful. It lets us recall the gist of an experience without storing every detail, which would be impossibly costly. But it also means memory is editable. A leading question, a later conversation, even our own retelling can slip new details into the rebuilt scene, and we cannot feel the seam where invention meets fact. The altered memory seems just as vivid and certain as a true one.

This has unsettling consequences. Eyewitnesses can sincerely describe events that never happened, not because they lie but because their minds rebuilt the past around suggestions planted afterward. Confidence, it turns out, is a poor measure of accuracy. What feels like replay is really a fresh act of construction, shaped by everything we have learned since.

questions.stem: Which statement best captures the main idea?
choices: Memory works like a perfect video recording
Remembering is an act of rebuilding that can introduce errors we cannot detect
Eyewitnesses always lie about what they saw
questions.explanation: The passage argues memory is reconstructed each time, making it editable and sometimes inaccurate without our awareness.

questions.stem: Why does the passage say confidence is 'a poor measure of accuracy'?
choices: People are usually lying when confident
Reconstructed false memories can feel just as vivid and certain as true ones
Accuracy cannot be measured at all
questions.explanation: The text notes altered memories seem as vivid and certain as true ones, so feeling sure does not prove correctness.

questions.stem: As used here, 'seam' most nearly refers to
choices: the join where invented details meet real ones
questions.explanation: We cannot feel 'the seam where invention meets fact,' meaning the join between false and true details.

lesson.strategy: Read this as an argument that flips a common belief. Sentence one states the belief (memory as a recording); "The truth is stranger" flips it. Follow one idea—reconstruction—as it turns from "useful" to "editable" to its eyewitness cost. The thesis lands last, not first.

signals.phrase: The truth is stranger
signals.means: Signals a flip: the everyday belief just stated is wrong; the author's real claim comes next.

signals.phrase: But it also means memory is editable
signals.means: The turn. The same trait that helps (recall the gist) is what makes memory changeable. Track both sides.

signals.phrase: we cannot feel the seam where invention meets fact
signals.means: The mechanism: an invented detail feels identical to a true one, which is why we trust false memories.

signals.phrase: Confidence, it turns out, is a poor measure
signals.means: The payoff claim the whole piece builds toward. This is the point, not the opening line.

vocab.inContext: Rebuilding a memory from scattered fragments each time, not replaying a stored copy.
Able to be changed after the fact; new details can slip into a remembered scene.
The join line between real memory and invented detail—which we cannot sense.

skillTips.main-idea: The main claim is the flipped belief, not the opening one. The author rejects memory as "a faithful video" and lands the real point last: confidence "is a poor measure of accuracy."
skillTips.inference: Ask what follows if memory is rebuilt each time. Eyewitnesses describe false events "sincerely," not by lying—the cause the author names is suggestions "planted afterward."
skillTips.vocabulary: For abstract words, use the nearby image. "where invention meets fact" tells you a "seam" is the join between made-up and real; "editable" shows as details that "slip" in.
skillTips.detail: See which details back which claim. The "leading question," "later conversation," and "own retelling" are three examples of one point—memory is editable—not separate facts.

passages.title: The Pull of a Favor
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
Across nearly every culture, people feel an urge to return a favor. When someone gives us something, even something small and unrequested, a quiet pressure builds to give something back. This rule of reciprocity helped early communities cooperate and share, and it runs so deep that we often obey it without noticing.

Because the urge is automatic, it can be used as a tool of persuasion. A free sample, an unexpected gift, a small concession in an argument: each can plant a sense of debt that makes us more willing to say yes afterward. The favor need not be large, and we need not even have wanted it. The discomfort of feeling indebted is enough to nudge our behavior.

Knowing this does not make the urge vanish, but it offers a defense. When a gift seems designed to obligate you, you are free to receive it as what it is, a sales tactic rather than a true kindness, and to feel no debt at all.

questions.stem: The passage is mainly about
choices: how the urge to return favors shapes behavior and can be used to persuade
questions.explanation: The passage explains reciprocity and how it can be used as a persuasion tool.

questions.stem: What defense against this tactic does the passage suggest?
choices: Recognizing a manipulative gift as a tactic and feeling no debt
questions.explanation: The text says you can receive such a gift as a sales tactic rather than kindness and feel no debt.

questions.stem: According to the passage, must a favor be large to create a sense of debt?
choices: No, even a small, unwanted favor can create the feeling
Only favors we asked for count
questions.explanation: The passage says the favor need not be large and we need not even have wanted it.

lesson.strategy: This essay runs in three moves: it names a rule ("rule of reciprocity"), shows how that rule becomes "a tool of persuasion," then hands you "a defense." Read for the turns, not the opening. The payoff is the last line, where relabeling the favor cancels the debt.

signals.phrase: rule of reciprocity
signals.means: The essay's core claim: a deep, automatic urge to return a favor. Every later point builds on this one idea.

signals.phrase: Because the urge is automatic
signals.means: The first turn. The essay pivots from describing the rule to showing how persuaders exploit it against you.

signals.phrase: but it offers a defense
signals.means: The second turn and the payoff. Knowing the trick won't kill the urge, but it lets you refuse the debt.

signals.phrase: a sales tactic rather than a true kindness
signals.means: The move that frees you: relabel the gift, and the pressure to give something back disappears.

vocab.inContext: the social rule that a favor received creates a pull to give something back
feeling you owe someone because they gave you something first
a point you give up in an argument, used here as a small favor that plants debt

skillTips.main-idea: Don't lock the main idea to paragraph one's rule. The essay finishes its point only at the end: reciprocity is automatic, persuaders use it, but naming the gift 'a sales tactic' frees you.
skillTips.inference: The passage never says 'distrust free samples.' Read the loaded words: 'a gift seems designed to obligate you' implies some gifts are strategic. The clue is that word 'designed.'
skillTips.vocabulary: This essay defines its hard words in place. 'Reciprocity' follows 'an urge to return a favor'; 'indebted' is glossed by 'a sense of debt.' Read the neighboring sentence, don't guess.
skillTips.detail: Separate the claim from its examples. The claim: reciprocity runs deep, automatic. Its proof is 'A free sample, an unexpected gift, a small concession.' On a detail question, find that list.

passages.title: The Cost You Cannot See
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
Every choice quietly closes a door. When you spend an afternoon on one activity, you give up everything else you might have done with that time. When you spend a dollar on one thing, you forgo whatever else that dollar could have bought. Economists call this hidden price the opportunity cost: the value of the best option you did not take.

What makes opportunity cost slippery is that it never appears on a receipt. The money you paid is visible and easy to count. The trip you did not take, the skill you did not learn, the rest you did not get, leave no trace, so the mind tends to ignore them. Yet they are the true measure of what a decision costs. A purchase that seems cheap may be expensive once you account for the better use of the same resources, and a free activity is never truly free if it consumes time you valued more elsewhere.

Thinking in terms of opportunity cost changes the question we ask. Instead of wondering whether something is worth its sticker price, we ask whether it is worth more than the next best thing we could do instead. This is a harder question, because it forces us to name what we are giving up. But it is also a more honest one. Resources are limited, and the real cost of having anything is the most valuable thing we surrender to get it. Decisions made without this lens often feel like bargains while quietly draining what matters most: our time, our energy, and our attention.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: The true cost of a choice is the best alternative you give up, which is easy to overlook
Money is the only real cost worth counting
Free activities have no cost at all
questions.explanation: The passage defines opportunity cost as the value of the best forgone option and stresses that it is hidden but real.

questions.stem: Why does the passage say opportunity cost is 'slippery'?
choices: It never appears on a receipt, so the mind tends to ignore it
questions.explanation: The text explains the cost leaves no trace and never appears on a receipt, so people overlook it.

questions.stem: How does opportunity cost change the question we should ask about a choice?
choices: Whether it costs less than last year
Whether it is worth more than the next best alternative
Whether other people approve of it
Whether it is the cheapest option available
questions.explanation: The passage says we should ask whether something is worth more than the next best thing we could do instead.

lesson.strategy: This essay defines one idea, opportunity cost, then defends it. Read in three beats: the definition ("the value of the best option you did not take"), why it hides ("never appears on a receipt"), and the reframe it forces ("changes the question we ask"). Track each "yet" or "but" turn.

signals.phrase: Economists call this hidden price
signals.means: Names the term being defined; the definition follows the colon, and the whole essay rests on it.

signals.phrase: What makes opportunity cost slippery
signals.means: The turn from defining the idea to explaining why it stays hidden from us.

signals.phrase: Yet they are the true measure
signals.means: 'Yet' flips the invisible costs from ignorable to the real point of a decision.

signals.phrase: changes the question we ask
signals.means: The pivot: from 'worth its price?' to 'worth more than the next best thing?' — the payoff.

vocab.inContext: to give up something else you could have had instead
hard to pin down or keep in mind; easy to overlook
to give up something valued in order to get another thing

skillTips.main-idea: Stated twice. Combine the definition, 'the value of the best option you did not take,' with the close, 'the real cost of having anything is the most valuable thing we surrender.'
skillTips.inference: Apply the rule to a case not named. The essay says 'a free activity is never truly free if it consumes time you valued more elsewhere.' What about a 'bargain' that drains your time?
skillTips.vocabulary: For hard words, use the nearby restatement. 'forgo' sits beside 'give up everything else you might have done'; 'slippery' is explained by the next line, 'it never appears on a receipt.'
skillTips.detail: The invisible costs are listed as examples: 'The trip you did not take, the skill you did not learn, the rest you did not get.' They share one form: things NOT done. That is the point.

passages.title: The First Number Wins
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
When we judge an unknown quantity, the mind reaches for a starting point and adjusts from there. The trouble is that the starting point need not be relevant, and the adjustment is usually too small. The first figure we encounter, even an arbitrary one, tugs our estimate toward itself. Psychologists call this the anchoring effect.

The pull is surprisingly strong. In studies, people asked whether a quantity is higher or lower than some random number, perhaps drawn from a spin of a wheel, will later give estimates that drift toward that meaningless number. They do not believe the wheel knows anything, and yet their guesses bend toward it. The anchor works even when we are told to ignore it.

This quietly shapes the marketplace. A high original price printed beside a sale price makes the sale feel generous, because the original anchors our sense of what the item is worth. A first offer in a negotiation frames every counteroffer that follows. Even an absurd opening demand can drag the final agreement in its direction, which is why the person who names the first number often holds an unseen advantage.

The defense is not to feel immune, since no one is. It is to notice when a number has been handed to you and to ask, deliberately, what the thing would be worth if you had never seen that figure at all. Generating your own estimate first, before any anchor appears, is one of the few reliable ways to keep the anchor from setting the terms of your judgment.

questions.stem: The passage is mainly about
choices: how an initial number, even an irrelevant one, biases our later estimates
why sale prices are always fair
how wheels are used in psychology labs
questions.explanation: The passage defines and illustrates the anchoring effect: a first number pulls our estimates toward it.

questions.stem: Why does naming the first number give a negotiator an advantage?
choices: It sets an anchor that drags later counteroffers toward it
It proves the negotiator is honest
It lowers the final price for everyone
questions.explanation: The text says a first offer frames every counteroffer and even an absurd demand drags the final agreement toward it.

questions.stem: What does the passage recommend as a defense against anchoring?
choices: Believing you are immune to it
Forming your own estimate before any anchor appears
questions.explanation: The passage says generating your own estimate first, before an anchor appears, is a reliable defense.

lesson.strategy: A four-move psychology essay: para 1 defines the anchoring effect, para 2 proves the pull is real, para 3 shows it in the marketplace, para 4 gives the defense. Read for the mechanism ("tugs our estimate toward itself"), not the examples.

signals.phrase: The trouble is that
signals.means: Flags the flaw in how the mind estimates: the starting point may be irrelevant and the adjustment too small.

signals.phrase: even when we are told to ignore it
signals.means: The strongest proof of the effect. Knowing about the anchor does not free you from its pull.

signals.phrase: which is why the person who names the first number
signals.means: A causal payoff. The marketplace lesson drawn from the studies: naming the first number is quiet power.

signals.phrase: The defense is not to feel immune
signals.means: The turn from problem to remedy. The rest of the paragraph tells you what to do instead.

vocab.inContext: chosen at random, with no real link to the true answer; still it tugs the estimate
protected, unaffected; here, the false belief that you cannot be swayed by an anchor
on purpose, as a careful conscious step, not by reflex or instinct

skillTips.main-idea: The whole claim sits in para 1: the mind grabs a first number and adjusts too little. Studies, prices, and negotiation all apply that one idea. Name the mechanism, not an example.
skillTips.inference: The passage never says 'let the other side open first.' Combine 'the person who names the first number often holds an unseen advantage' with the defense to infer the smart move.
skillTips.vocabulary: For 'anchor,' skip the boat meaning. The passage defines it by action, the first figure 'tugs our estimate toward itself.' Let the verbs (tugs, drift, bend, drag) fix the sense.
skillTips.detail: The study detail is the point: a quantity judged against 'some random number' from 'a spin of a wheel.' Random and meaningless, yet it still moves the guess. Note that, not the numbers.

passages.title: Why the Sea Rises and Falls
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Twice a day, the ocean creeps up the beach and then slides back down again. These changes are called tides, and they are caused mostly by the Moon. The Moon's gravity pulls on the water of the ocean. The side of Earth facing the Moon is tugged hardest, so the water there bulges outward. A matching bulge forms on the opposite side of the planet. As Earth spins, different coasts pass through these bulges, which is why high tide and low tide arrive at regular times. The Sun adds a smaller pull of its own. When the Sun and Moon line up, their pulls combine to make especially large tides.

questions.stem: What is this passage mainly about?
choices: How fish survive in the sea
questions.explanation: The passage explains that tides are the rise and fall of the sea, caused mostly by the Moon's gravity.

questions.stem: According to the text, how many bulges of water form on Earth?
questions.explanation: The text says water bulges on the side facing the Moon and 'a matching bulge forms on the opposite side,' which is two.

questions.stem: In this passage, the word 'bulges' means areas that
questions.explanation: The water is 'tugged' and 'bulges outward,' so a bulge swells outward.

lesson.strategy: This passage explains WHY tides happen, one step at a time. Read it as a cause-and-effect chain, not a list of facts: the Moon pulls, the water bulges, Earth spins, tides arrive on time. Follow the linking words like 'so' and 'which is why' from the Moon to the beach.

signals.phrase: they are caused mostly by the Moon
signals.means: The main claim. Every sentence after this explains HOW the Moon causes tides.

signals.phrase: is tugged hardest, so the water there bulges
signals.means: A cause-effect link. 'So' means the bulge is the result of the hardest pull.

signals.phrase: which is why high tide and low tide
signals.means: 'Which is why' points back: the spinning Earth explains the regular timing.

signals.phrase: When the Sun and Moon line up
signals.means: A condition word. This 'when' marks the special case that makes extra-large tides.

vocab.inContext: the daily rise and fall of the ocean up and down the beach
the invisible pull the Moon has on Earth's water
places where the ocean swells outward into a raised mound

skillTips.main-idea: One question drives this passage: why does the sea rise and fall? The second sentence answers it, 'caused mostly by the Moon.' The main idea is that cause, not the bulges or the Sun.
skillTips.inference: The text says Earth spins and coasts 'pass through these bulges.' It never says a coast stays in one bulge. Put those together to see why a beach gets high tide, then low tide later.
skillTips.vocabulary: For 'bulges', use the words right beside it: 'the water there bulges outward.' 'Outward' tells you the water swells up and out, like a bump, not that it sinks down.
skillTips.detail: Facts here come in a chain, so trace the link before and after each one. For the biggest tides, find where the Sun enters ('the Sun adds a smaller pull') and what its lining up does.

passages.title: The Dance of the Honeybee
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: When a honeybee discovers a rich patch of flowers, it does not simply fly home and rest. Instead, it returns to the hive and performs a curious movement that scientists call the waggle dance. The bee walks in a straight line while shaking its body, then loops back to repeat the pattern. The direction of the straight run, measured against the position of the Sun, tells the other bees which way to fly. The length of the waggle hints at how far away the food lies. Nearby flowers earn a short, quick dance, while distant ones produce a long, vigorous one. Through this silent language, a single scout can send dozens of workers straight to a meal they have never seen.

questions.stem: The passage is mostly about how honeybees
questions.explanation: The whole passage describes the waggle dance as a way bees tell others where food is.

questions.stem: What does the length of the waggle indicate?
choices: How far away the food is
questions.explanation: The text states the length of the waggle 'hints at how far away the food lies.'

questions.stem: Based on the passage, a very long, vigorous dance most likely means the flowers are
questions.explanation: The text contrasts short dances for nearby flowers with long, vigorous dances for distant ones.

lesson.strategy: This explains one thing: how a honeybee's "waggle dance" tells other bees where food is. Read it as a code with two parts: the direction of the straight run maps to a real direction, and the length of the waggle maps to distance. Watch the "Instead" and "while" turns.

signals.phrase: call the waggle dance
signals.means: The named term the whole passage exists to explain. Every later detail describes a piece of this dance.

signals.phrase: The direction of the straight run
signals.means: One coded variable: which way to fly. Pair it with 'The length of the waggle' — two signals, two meanings.

signals.phrase: Nearby flowers earn a short, quick dance
signals.means: A 'while' contrast: near = short dance, far = long dance. The comparison is the point, not either half alone.

signals.phrase: silent language
signals.means: The author's label for the dance. When a science text names its subject like this, it states the main idea.

vocab.inContext: the fast side-to-side body shake the bee makes during the straight run
strong and full of energy; here, a long forceful dance meaning the food is far
the single bee that finds the flowers and returns to report their location

skillTips.main-idea: The passage answers one question: how does a bee tell others where food is? To state the main idea, say what the waggle dance does — not just that bees dance.
skillTips.inference: The text never says bees 'talk,' yet a scout can send workers 'straight to a meal they have never seen.' Infer the dance carries exact information, not a vague signal.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Vigorous' sits beside 'long' and opposite 'short, quick,' so it means strong and energetic. Use the near/far contrast to unlock words you don't know here.
skillTips.detail: The dance codes two facts: the direction ('measured against the position of the Sun') and the distance ('how far away the food lies'). Ask which one a question wants.

passages.title: How Rock Becomes Rock Again
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Rock may look permanent, but over long stretches of time it changes from one form into another. Deep below the surface, intense heat melts rock into a glowing liquid called magma. When magma cools, either underground or after erupting from a volcano, it hardens into igneous rock. Wind and water then break that rock into tiny grains. The grains settle in layers at the bottom of lakes and seas, and over millions of years the weight above presses them into sedimentary rock. If sedimentary rock is buried deeper still, heat and pressure can squeeze and bake it into metamorphic rock without fully melting it. Should that rock sink far enough to melt, it becomes magma again. Geologists call this endless loop the rock cycle, and it has been turning for billions of years. Because each step takes so long, a single grain of sand may carry the history of several different rocks within it, a record patient observers can learn to read.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'magma' refers to rock that is
questions.explanation: The text defines magma as rock that intense heat 'melts ... into a glowing liquid.'

questions.stem: According to the text, sedimentary rock forms when
choices: grains settle and are pressed in layers
questions.explanation: The passage says grains settle in layers and the weight above presses them into sedimentary rock.

questions.stem: The best title for the central idea of this passage is
questions.explanation: The passage describes the rock cycle, the endless loop in which rock changes form.

lesson.strategy: This traces one loop: magma to igneous to sedimentary to metamorphic and back to magma. Read it as a chain, not a list. At each rock name, ask what force made it (heat, wind, weight, pressure) and what comes next. The "then / over millions of years / again" words carry the sequence.

signals.phrase: it changes from one form into another
signals.means: The whole passage's claim: rock is not fixed. Every detail after this shows one step of that change.

signals.phrase: Wind and water then break that rock
signals.means: "then" marks a step in the cycle. Track each "then/when/should" to keep the order of rock types straight.

signals.phrase: endless loop the rock cycle
signals.means: Names the structure: the steps form a circle, not a line. That is why it ends back at magma, where it began.

signals.phrase: Because each step takes so long
signals.means: A cause-effect turn. It explains why one grain of sand can hold several rocks' history.

vocab.inContext: Rock melted into a glowing liquid by intense heat deep below the surface.
Rock made when tiny grains settle in layers and weight presses them together.
Rock made when heat and pressure bake buried rock without fully melting it.

skillTips.main-idea: The first sentence states it: rock "changes from one form into another." Everything after is proof. The passage is about rock changing shape over time, not about volcanoes.
skillTips.inference: A grain of sand "may carry the history of several different rocks." To infer why, connect that to "each step takes so long" and the loop that keeps turning. Chain the stated facts.
skillTips.vocabulary: Each rock name is defined by the sentence around it. "igneous" follows "When magma cools... it hardens into." Read the how-it-forms clause next to the word.
skillTips.detail: Details here are ordered steps. To find what makes sedimentary rock, locate the name, then read its force clause: grains "settle in layers," "weight above presses them."

passages.title: The Toughest Animal You Can't See
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Among the most resilient creatures on Earth is one barely visible to the naked eye. The tardigrade, sometimes called a water bear, is a chubby, eight-legged animal smaller than a grain of sand. It lives in mossy patches, ocean sediments, and the films of water that cling to soil. What makes the tardigrade remarkable is its response to harsh conditions. When its surroundings dry out, the animal expels nearly all the water from its body and pulls in its legs, shriveling into a dormant ball called a tun. In this suspended state its metabolism almost halts, and it can endure temperatures near absolute zero, the vacuum of space, and doses of radiation that would kill most life many times over. Years later, a single drop of water can revive it, and the tiny animal lumbers off as though nothing had happened. Scientists study tardigrades to understand how life might survive on other worlds, and how delicate biological structures can be preserved through extreme stress.

questions.stem: As used in the passage, 'dormant' most nearly means
questions.explanation: The tun is a state where 'metabolism almost halts,' so dormant means inactive or resting.

questions.stem: Why do scientists study tardigrades in connection with other worlds?
choices: Their survival hints at how life might endure harsh, alien conditions
They can only live in vacuums
questions.explanation: The passage says scientists study them to understand how life might survive on other worlds because they endure extreme conditions.

questions.stem: What does a tardigrade do when its surroundings dry out?
choices: It expels water and curls into a tun
questions.explanation: The text states it 'expels nearly all the water from its body and pulls in its legs,' forming a tun.

lesson.strategy: A science profile built on one claim: the tardigrade survives almost anything. Read the opening as the claim, follow the drying-out steps in order (dry out → tun → revive), and treat the extremes it endures as proof. The last sentence says why it matters.

signals.phrase: What makes the tardigrade remarkable
signals.means: The thesis marker: the sentence right after it states the whole point everything else backs up.

signals.phrase: When its surroundings dry out
signals.means: Starts the survival process in time order; the tun forms only in response to this trigger.

signals.phrase: In this suspended state
signals.means: Signals a result: the extremes it endures come from the tun, not as separate facts.

signals.phrase: Scientists study tardigrades
signals.means: The so-what close; read on for why the survival matters, not just what happens.

vocab.inContext: able to survive and bounce back from harsh conditions that would kill most life
in a paused, inactive state; alive but with body processes nearly stopped
the shriveled dormant ball a tardigrade becomes when it expels its water

skillTips.main-idea: The claim sits right after "What makes the tardigrade remarkable": its response to harsh conditions. The tun, the extremes, and the revival are all evidence for that one idea.
skillTips.inference: The passage never says outright why this is useful. The last sentence gives the purpose: to learn how life might survive on other worlds. Infer the value from that.
skillTips.vocabulary: Hard words are defined beside them. "a dormant ball called a tun" names the tun; "suspended state" restates dormant. When a word is new, check the same sentence.
skillTips.detail: For "what can it survive," find the list after "endure": absolute zero, the vacuum of space, radiation. For "how," track the order: expels water → pulls in legs → tun → revives.

passages.title: Eating Sunlight
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Animals must find food to get energy, but green plants make their own. Inside their leaves are tiny green parts that capture sunlight. The plant uses that light energy to combine water from the soil with a gas called carbon dioxide from the air. This process turns those simple ingredients into sugar, which feeds the plant and helps it grow. The process is called photosynthesis. As a bonus, the plant releases oxygen, the gas that people and animals need to breathe. So when you stand near a leafy tree on a sunny day, the tree is quietly building food and freshening the air at the same time.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of this passage?
choices: Plants use sunlight to make their own food
Oxygen is a kind of sugar
questions.explanation: The passage centers on photosynthesis, how plants use sunlight to make food.

questions.stem: According to the text, what gas do plants release during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: The text says the plant 'releases oxygen' as a bonus.

questions.stem: In the passage, 'capture' most nearly means
questions.explanation: The green parts 'capture sunlight,' meaning they take it in to use its energy.

lesson.strategy: This explains how a plant makes its own food. Read it like a recipe: track what goes in (sunlight, water, carbon dioxide) and what comes out (sugar, oxygen). The "but" in line one splits animals from plants. The whole process gets one name: photosynthesis.

signals.phrase: but green plants make their own
signals.means: The 'but' splits the passage in two: animals must hunt for food, plants make it. This contrast is the setup.

signals.phrase: a gas called carbon dioxide
signals.means: The ingredients going in. Mark water, carbon dioxide, and sunlight as what the plant uses to make food.

signals.phrase: This process turns those simple ingredients into sugar
signals.means: The result. 'turns...into' is a cause word: ingredients in, sugar out. Sugar is the food the plant makes.

signals.phrase: As a bonus, the plant releases oxygen
signals.means: 'As a bonus' flags an extra result: the plant feeds itself and gives off oxygen we breathe.

vocab.inContext: The name for the whole process: using sunlight to turn water and carbon dioxide into sugar.
A gas in the air the plant takes in and uses to make sugar.
The gas the plant gives off, which people and animals need to breathe.

skillTips.main-idea: The passage answers one question: how do plants feed themselves? 'The process is called photosynthesis' names the answer. Hold the question and that name together for the main idea.
skillTips.inference: Link 'releases oxygen, the gas that people and animals need to breathe' with 'freshening the air' to infer why a leafy tree matters to you, not just to itself.
skillTips.vocabulary: This passage defines hard words right beside them: 'a gas called carbon dioxide' and 'oxygen, the gas that people and animals need to breathe'. Read the words just after a term.
skillTips.detail: Read the middle like a recipe. To answer what the plant uses or makes, scan 'combine water from the soil' and 'turns those simple ingredients into sugar' for the exact ins and outs.

passages.title: Messages in the Brain
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Every thought, memory, and movement begins as a tiny electrical signal traveling through the brain. The brain contains roughly eighty-six billion cells called neurons, each one shaped like a tree with many branching arms. A neuron receives signals through its branches, and when enough of them arrive, the cell fires a pulse of electricity down a long fiber toward its neighbors. But neurons do not actually touch. Between them lies a microscopic gap called a synapse. To cross it, the electrical signal triggers the release of chemical messengers that drift across the gap and pass the message along. This blend of electricity and chemistry lets billions of neurons coordinate in fractions of a second. What we experience as a single smooth thought is really a vast storm of these signals racing through an intricate web of connections, many of them strengthened or weakened by what we learn.

questions.stem: What is the small gap between two neurons called?
questions.explanation: The text says 'Between them lies a microscopic gap called a synapse.'

questions.stem: The passage suggests that a single thought is
choices: the result of many neurons signaling together
questions.explanation: The text says a smooth thought is 'really a vast storm of these signals' across billions of neurons.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'intricate' most nearly means
questions.explanation: An 'intricate web of connections' refers to a complex, detailed network.

lesson.strategy: Read this as a process with a twist. It opens with a claim, then walks a signal through a neuron step by step, then turns at "But neurons do not actually touch." Track the sequence and the switch from electricity to chemistry; the last sentence reframes it all.

signals.phrase: cells called neurons
signals.means: The 'called X' pattern is the author naming a key term right where it first appears.

signals.phrase: But neurons do not actually touch.
signals.means: The turn: a surprising fact that sets up the gap-and-chemistry mechanism the rest explains.

signals.phrase: To cross it, the electrical signal triggers
signals.means: Cause-and-effect: how the signal jumps the gap, switching from electricity to chemistry.

signals.phrase: a single smooth thought is really
signals.means: Appearance-vs-reality reframe: the passage's main point landing at the end.

vocab.inContext: The microscopic gap between two neurons that the signal must cross.
Brain cells shaped like branching trees that receive and fire signals.
Complex and finely detailed, with countless connected parts.

skillTips.main-idea: Read the first and last sentences together: 'begins as a tiny electrical signal' and 'really a vast storm of these signals' - one smooth thought is really many signals.
skillTips.inference: Follow the causal chain to what is unstated. From 'strengthened or weakened by what we learn,' infer that learning physically changes the connections, though the text never says so.
skillTips.vocabulary: This author names its hard words with 'called': 'cells called neurons', 'a gap called a synapse.' When a term is new, look right before or after it for that naming phrase.
skillTips.detail: It is an ordered process: a neuron receives, then fires, then chemical messengers cross the synapse. Use the order words ('when', 'To cross it', 'along') to keep each step in place.

passages.title: Cities Beneath the Waves
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: A coral reef looks like a colorful rocky garden, but it is actually built by living animals. Each coral is a colony of tiny soft-bodied creatures called polyps. Over many years, the polyps draw minerals from seawater to build hard limestone cups around themselves, and as old polyps die and new ones grow on top, the reef slowly rises. Inside the polyps live even smaller partners: microscopic algae that make food from sunlight and share it with the coral. In return, the algae get shelter. This partnership is why reefs grow only in clear, sunlit waters. When the water grows too warm, the coral expels its algae and turns pale, an event known as bleaching. Without its partners, the coral can starve. That is why scientists watch ocean temperatures so closely, since even a small, lasting rise can leave miles of reef pale and lifeless within a single season.

questions.stem: The passage is mainly about
choices: how living coral builds reefs and depends on algae
questions.explanation: The passage explains that reefs are built by coral polyps that partner with algae.

questions.stem: What do the algae give the coral in their partnership?
questions.explanation: The text says the algae 'make food from sunlight and share it with the coral.'

questions.stem: Based on the passage, bleaching is harmful to coral because
choices: it makes the coral too colorful
the coral loses the algae that feed it
it cools the water too much
questions.explanation: Bleaching is when coral expels its algae; without those partners 'the coral can starve.'

lesson.strategy: This explains how a reef works. Read for the deal between coral and algae, and the chain from warm water to dead reef. Catch the "but it is actually" flip in sentence one, track the "In return" trade, and note that "bleaching" is defined just before it is named.

signals.phrase: but it is actually built by living animals
signals.means: The 'but... actually' flips your first guess; the real claim is that the reef is alive, not rock.

signals.phrase: In return, the algae get shelter
signals.means: Marks a two-way trade. Track what each partner gives and gets: coral gets food, algae get shelter.

signals.phrase: an event known as bleaching
signals.means: A term is named right after it is described, so the meaning of bleaching sits in the words just before it.

signals.phrase: since even a small, lasting rise
signals.means: A cause-and-effect link: warm water leads to expelled algae, starving coral, and dead reef.

vocab.inContext: tiny soft-bodied creatures that each coral is made of; they build the reef's limestone cups
pushes out; the coral forces out its algae when the water gets too warm
when coral loses its algae and turns pale, which can lead it to starve

skillTips.main-idea: The text keeps returning to one relationship: coral and algae need each other. For the main idea, follow the 'partnership' thread throughout, not the opening 'colorful rocky garden.'
skillTips.inference: The last line says a 'small, lasting rise' can leave 'miles of reef pale and lifeless.' To infer, chain the stated causes: warm water, then coral expels algae, then no food, then starve.
skillTips.vocabulary: For hard words, the sentence defines them. 'polyps' comes right after 'tiny soft-bodied creatures called,' and 'expels' pairs with 'turns pale' to show the coral pushing its algae out.
skillTips.detail: Details sit inside the process steps. To find how the reef grows, look for: polyps 'draw minerals from seawater to build hard limestone cups,' then 'old polyps die and new ones grow on top.'

passages.title: The Endless Journey of Water
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: The water in a puddle today may have floated in a cloud last week and rushed down a river last year. Water travels through the world in a repeating path called the water cycle. The Sun heats oceans, lakes, and rivers, turning some of the water into an invisible gas called vapor that rises into the sky. High up, where the air is cooler, the vapor turns back into tiny droplets that gather to form clouds. When the droplets grow heavy enough, they fall as rain or snow. That water flows back into rivers and seas, and the journey starts over. No new water is made and none is lost; the same water has been moving in this circle for a very long time.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'vapor' is best described as
choices: water in the form of gas
questions.explanation: The text calls vapor 'an invisible gas' that water turns into when heated.

questions.stem: According to the text, what makes water rise into the sky?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'The Sun heats oceans, lakes, and rivers,' turning water into vapor that rises.

questions.stem: The passage mainly explains that water
questions.explanation: The whole passage describes the water cycle as a repeating path, and notes no water is made or lost.

lesson.strategy: This traces water through one loop. Read it as a chain where each step causes the next: watch the change-verbs "heats," "rises," "turns back," "gather," "grow heavy," "fall," "flows back." Save the last line for the real point.

signals.phrase: the water cycle
signals.means: Names the one repeating path the whole passage follows. Expect a loop that ends where it began.

signals.phrase: The Sun heats
signals.means: The cause that starts every round. Heat is what turns liquid water into vapor and lifts it up.

signals.phrase: turns back into tiny droplets
signals.means: The reverse step: cool air changes vapor back to water. This is where clouds get made.

signals.phrase: No new water is made and none is lost
signals.means: The main claim. All the steps before it prove the same water just keeps circling.

vocab.inContext: The invisible gas water becomes when the Sun heats it; it rises into the sky.
Tiny bits of water that form high up and gather together to make clouds.
A path that repeats over and over, so the journey keeps starting again.

skillTips.main-idea: The last sentence is the point: "No new water is made and none is lost." The Sun-to-rain steps are proof for it. Read the ending, then see how the steps support it.
skillTips.inference: One puddle's water sits in a cloud last week and a river last year. The text never says it moved in that order, but the cycle lets you conclude the same water traveled the whole path.
skillTips.vocabulary: Hard words are defined right in the sentence. "an invisible gas called vapor" tells you vapor is that gas. Look for the word "called" to grab the meaning without guessing.
skillTips.detail: Facts sit inside the order of steps. To find what happens where, follow the chain: Sun heats, vapor rises, turns to droplets, form clouds, fall as rain or snow, flows back.

passages.title: Glowing in the Dark Ocean
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Sunlight reaches only the upper layers of the ocean. Below about a thousand meters lies a realm of permanent darkness, crushing pressure, and frigid cold. Yet this seemingly empty zone teems with life, and much of it makes its own light. The ability of an organism to produce light through a chemical reaction is called bioluminescence, and in the deep sea it is the rule rather than the exception. Animals there use their glow for many purposes. Some anglerfish dangle a luminous lure to draw curious prey within reach of their jaws. Certain squid release a cloud of glowing fluid to confuse a predator, much as a shallow-water octopus releases ink. Others flash patterns to find a mate in the vast blackness. Because the deep ocean offers almost no places to hide, light has become both a weapon and a language. A sudden burst can startle an attacker, while a steady glow can betray the swimmer that produces it, so each animal must balance the risk of being seen against the benefit of being noticed. Some species even tune the color and timing of their flashes so precisely that they amount to private codes, recognized only by their own kind. Studying these creatures is difficult, since the delicate animals often die when hauled up through warmer, lower-pressure water, so researchers increasingly send cameras and robotic vehicles down to observe them where they live. Each expedition tends to return with footage of species no one has ever catalogued, a reminder of how much of our own planet remains genuinely unexplored.

questions.stem: As used in the passage, 'bioluminescence' is
choices: an organism producing light by a chemical reaction
a kind of cold ocean current
the lack of sunlight in the deep
questions.explanation: The text defines it as 'the ability of an organism to produce light through a chemical reaction.'

questions.stem: How does an anglerfish use its light, according to the passage?
choices: To dangle a lure that attracts prey
To find its way to the surface
questions.explanation: The text says anglerfish 'dangle a luminous lure to draw curious prey within reach of their jaws.'

questions.stem: The central idea of this passage is that
choices: the deep sea is completely lifeless
many deep-sea animals make and use light to survive
anglerfish are the only glowing animals
questions.explanation: The passage describes how bioluminescence is widespread in the deep sea and serves many survival purposes.

lesson.strategy: A nature explainer built on one claim. Read the first three sentences for the claim: glow is normal down deep. Then treat each animal as evidence for it. Track the frame 'weapon and a language' and the cause words 'because' and 'so.' End on the zoom-out about what stays unexplored.

signals.phrase: Yet this seemingly empty zone teems
signals.means: The turn word Yet flips the setup: the dark, cold, crushing deep is not dead but full of life.

signals.phrase: the rule rather than the exception
signals.means: The thesis. Glow in the deep sea is normal, not rare, so read the animal examples as proof of it.

signals.phrase: both a weapon and a language
signals.means: The organizing frame. Sort each example into attack/defense or communication.

signals.phrase: so each animal must balance
signals.means: The so marks a cause-effect conclusion: being seen is both a risk and a benefit, a tradeoff.

vocab.inContext: an organism making its own light through a chemical reaction; the sentence defines it
glowing, giving off light, like the anglerfish lure that draws prey within reach
to give away or expose; a steady glow reveals where the swimmer is to a predator

skillTips.main-idea: One idea holds the passage: deep-sea glow is 'the rule rather than the exception.' The anglerfish, squid, and mate-flashers support it. Name the shared claim, not one fish.
skillTips.inference: The passage only hints the ocean is barely known. Combine 'species no one has ever catalogued' with 'genuinely unexplored' to infer discovery is still ongoing.
skillTips.vocabulary: Hard words are explained nearby. 'bioluminescence' is defined by the clause before 'is called'; 'luminous' sits by 'lure.' Read the surrounding sentence first.
skillTips.detail: Each example pairs one animal with one use: anglerfish lure prey, squid release a glowing cloud to confuse, others flash to find a mate. Match the exact animal to its exact purpose.

passages.title: The Blanket of Air
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Earth stays warm enough for life because of a delicate balance played out in its atmosphere. Energy arrives from the Sun mostly as visible light, which passes easily through the air and warms the planet's surface. The warmed surface then radiates that energy back upward, but it does so as infrared heat, a longer wavelength that behaves differently. Certain gases in the atmosphere, including carbon dioxide, methane, and water vapor, are nearly transparent to incoming sunlight yet absorb outgoing infrared. They re-radiate some of that heat back toward the ground, slowing its escape into space. This natural trapping is the greenhouse effect, and without it the planet would freeze. The concern today is one of degree rather than direction. By burning fossil fuels, humans have raised the concentration of these heat-absorbing gases, thickening the blanket and pushing the balance toward a warmer state. Because the climate system responds slowly and unevenly, the full consequences of a change unfold over decades, long after the gases are released. The oceans, in particular, absorb a vast share of the extra heat and release it only gradually, acting like a sluggish flywheel that keeps turning after the engine is switched off. Understanding this lag is essential, for it means the warming already set in motion will continue even if emissions were to stop tomorrow. It also explains why the effects of choices made today, whether harmful or helpful, may not become fully visible until the people who made them are gone, a feature that makes the problem unusually hard for societies to confront.

questions.stem: Why does the passage say the warming would continue even if emissions stopped tomorrow?
choices: Because the Sun is getting hotter
Because the climate system responds slowly, with a lag
Because sunlight cannot escape at all
questions.explanation: The text explains the climate 'responds slowly and unevenly,' so warming already set in motion continues.

questions.stem: In this passage, gases described as 'transparent to incoming sunlight' means sunlight
questions.explanation: Transparent to sunlight means light passes easily through, which the text contrasts with absorbing outgoing infrared.

questions.stem: The passage is mainly concerned with
choices: how the greenhouse effect works and how humans are intensifying it
questions.explanation: The passage explains the greenhouse effect and how burning fossil fuels thickens the blanket toward a warmer state.

lesson.strategy: It explains a mechanism, then turns. First the natural process: light in, infrared out, gases trap it. At 'one of degree rather than direction' it pivots to the human problem. Track the mechanism, then follow the lag to the ending claim about delayed, invisible consequences.

signals.phrase: yet absorb outgoing infrared
signals.means: The pivot 'yet': gases let sunlight in but block heat out — that gap is the whole effect.

signals.phrase: one of degree rather than direction
signals.means: The turn from the natural effect to the human worry: not whether it happens, but how much.

signals.phrase: acting like a sluggish flywheel
signals.means: The analogy for lag: the ocean keeps warming after emissions stop, like a wheel spinning past the engine.

signals.phrase: even if emissions were to stop tomorrow
signals.means: A hypothetical proving the point: past warming is locked in, so the consequence outlasts the cause.

vocab.inContext: invisible heat of a longer wavelength; what the warm surface sends back up
the delay between releasing gases and seeing the full warming
slow to respond; the ocean absorbs and releases heat gradually, not at once

skillTips.main-idea: The point isn't how the greenhouse effect works — that's setup. It arrives after 'The concern today' and lands on lag: warming 'set in motion will continue.' Ask what the mechanism explains.
skillTips.inference: The flywheel image and 'stop tomorrow' aren't decoration. They imply today's choices show up 'until the people who made them are gone' — cause and effect split in time.
skillTips.vocabulary: Hard terms are defined beside them. 'Infrared heat' is followed by 'a longer wavelength that behaves differently'; 'greenhouse effect' is named after the trapping. Read the next phrase.
skillTips.detail: Keep the energy path in order: sunlight 'passes easily through,' the surface 'radiates that energy back upward,' gases 'absorb outgoing infrared' and 're-radiate' it. In, up, back down.

passages.title: The Strength of Spider Silk
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: For its weight, spider silk is one of the strongest materials known. A strand far thinner than a human hair can stretch without snapping and absorb the impact of a flying insect many times the spider's size. Engineers admire silk because it combines two qualities that usually oppose each other: it is both strong and stretchy. Steel is strong but brittle, while rubber stretches but tears easily. Silk manages both, which is why a web can catch prey without shattering. A single spider can produce several kinds of silk from different glands, each tuned for a task. One sort forms the sturdy frame of the web, another the sticky spiral that traps insects, and yet another wraps eggs in a protective case. Scientists have long hoped to manufacture silk in factories for uses from medical stitches to lightweight armor, but reproducing the spider's recipe has proven stubbornly hard.

questions.stem: Why do engineers find spider silk especially impressive?
choices: It is both strong and stretchy, qualities that usually oppose each other
questions.explanation: The text says silk combines strength and stretchiness, two qualities that 'usually oppose each other.'

questions.stem: According to the passage, what is one kind of silk used for?
questions.explanation: The text says one sort of silk 'forms the sturdy frame of the web.'

questions.stem: In this passage, 'brittle' most nearly means
questions.explanation: Steel is called 'strong but brittle,' contrasted with stretchy, so brittle means likely to break.

lesson.strategy: This science explainer turns on one paradox: silk is "both strong and stretchy," traits that "usually oppose each other." Track the opening claim, the steel-and-rubber contrast, the "which is why" payoff, and the closing "but" for what humans still can't do.

signals.phrase: For its weight
signals.means: A qualifier scoping the claim: silk is strongest for its weight, not in raw terms.

signals.phrase: usually oppose each other
signals.means: Names the paradox the passage exists to explain: strong plus stretchy in one material.

signals.phrase: which is why
signals.means: A causation link: strong-and-stretchy is the reason a web catches prey without shattering.

signals.phrase: but reproducing
signals.means: The closing turn: admiration flips to the unsolved problem of copying silk in factories.

vocab.inContext: Strong but breaks or snaps easily instead of bending; the opposite of stretchy.
Organs inside the spider that produce silk, each making a different kind.
In a way that resists all effort; the recipe stays hard however hard they try.

skillTips.main-idea: The point is not "silk is strong" alone. It is the paradox: silk joins strong and stretchy, traits that "usually oppose each other," and copying it is "stubbornly hard." Hold both halves.
skillTips.inference: The steel-and-rubber sentence is never called the lesson, but it proves silk is special: each rival has one trait only, so "Silk manages both" is what makes it rare.
skillTips.vocabulary: Let the built-in contrasts define hard words. "Strong but brittle" pairs brittle against strong; "stretches but tears easily" pairs with stretchy. Read the opposite word beside it.
