skillTips.detail: For the kinds of silk, anchor on "each tuned for a task" and count the three: the "sturdy frame," the "sticky spiral," and the wrap that protects eggs. Match each silk to its job.

passages.title: The Lives of Stars
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: A star is not eternal; it is born, lives, and dies over spans that dwarf human history. Stars begin within vast clouds of gas and dust drifting through space. Gravity slowly pulls a clump of this material inward, and as the clump shrinks it grows denser and hotter. When the core becomes hot enough, hydrogen atoms begin fusing into helium, releasing enormous energy and igniting the star. For most of its life a star maintains a steady balance: the outward push of this fusion energy resists the inward crush of gravity. When the hydrogen fuel runs low, that balance breaks, and the star's fate depends on its mass. A modest star like the Sun swells into a red giant, sheds its outer layers, and settles into a slowly cooling ember. A far more massive star ends violently, collapsing and then exploding as a supernova that briefly outshines an entire galaxy. The debris of such explosions scatters heavy elements across space. Gathered by gravity over billions of years, that scattered debris becomes the raw material for new clouds, new stars, and the planets that circle them. Remarkably, the carbon in living bodies and the iron in blood were forged inside ancient stars, making every person, in a literal sense, the remnant of stellar death. The very atoms that let you read this sentence were once at the searing heart of a sun that perished long before the Earth existed, a continuity that links the smallest cell to the grandest events in the sky.

questions.stem: Which factor decides whether a star ends as a cooling ember or a supernova?
questions.explanation: The text states that when hydrogen runs low, 'the star's fate depends on its mass.'

questions.stem: What keeps a star in a steady balance for most of its life?
choices: Fusion energy pushing out against gravity pulling in
questions.explanation: The passage says the 'outward push of this fusion energy resists the inward crush of gravity.'

questions.stem: As used in the passage, 'forged' most nearly means
questions.explanation: Heavy elements like carbon and iron were 'forged inside ancient stars,' meaning created or formed there.

lesson.strategy: Read this as a life story with a physics engine. Follow "born, lives, and dies," and watch the force-balance—fusion pushing out, gravity crushing in—hold a star steady until "the hydrogen fuel runs low." At "depends on its mass" the path forks; the last lines turn it personal.

signals.phrase: born, lives, and dies
signals.means: Frames the passage as a life cycle. Read the stages in order; each paragraph moves one step along it.

signals.phrase: outward push of this fusion energy
signals.means: The core mechanism: this push resists gravity's inward crush. The plot is that balance holding, then breaking.

signals.phrase: the star's fate depends on its mass
signals.means: The fork. Two endings branch from here—Sun-sized vs. massive. Read the next sentences as a contrast pair.

signals.phrase: in a literal sense
signals.means: Flags that 'you are made of dead stars' is a factual claim, not a poetic metaphor. Take it at face value.

vocab.inContext: atoms joining into a heavier atom (hydrogen into helium), releasing energy that lights the star
the violent exploding death of a massive star, briefly brighter than a whole galaxy
made or created under heat—here, carbon and iron built inside ancient stars

skillTips.main-idea: The point is bigger than how stars work. It pulls to 'the carbon in living bodies... were forged inside ancient stars.' Ask why the author ends on your body, not the supernova.
skillTips.inference: The text never says the Sun will die, but it files it under 'a modest star like the Sun.' Treat the two mass-paths as a sorting rule, then place the Sun in its branch to infer its ending.
skillTips.vocabulary: Hard science words are defined by nearby words. 'fusing into helium, releasing enormous energy' tells you fusing means joining atoms plus energy. Read the nearby clause instead of guessing.
skillTips.detail: Time and cause words are the spine: 'When the core becomes hot enough,' 'then exploding.' For order or cause questions, find the when/then word for that step, not a keyword match.

passages.title: The First Day
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Nadia arrived at the bakery an hour before it opened. Her hands were cold and her stomach felt tight. It was her first day, and she did not know where anything was. The baker, a tall man named Cole, handed her an apron. "Ask me anything," he said. "Everyone was new once." Nadia felt a little better. She watched how Cole shaped the dough. She wrote down each step in a small notebook. When she made a mistake and dropped a tray, no one shouted. Cole just showed her how to hold it. By noon, the shelves were full of warm bread. Nadia was tired, but she was proud. She had asked many questions, and now she knew a hundred small things she had not known that morning. Tomorrow would be easier.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: A woman's first day at a new job, and how asking questions helped her learn
How to bake many kinds of bread
A fight between two workers at a bakery
Why the bakery opened an hour late
questions.explanation: The passage follows Nadia through her first day and shows her learning by asking and watching.

questions.stem: What did Nadia write in her small notebook?
choices: Each step for shaping the dough
questions.explanation: The text says she watched how Cole shaped the dough and wrote down each step.

questions.stem: Why did Nadia feel "a little better" after Cole spoke?
choices: His kind words told her it was okay to be new and to ask
She had finished all her work
She was about to go home
questions.explanation: Cole said "Ask me anything" and "Everyone was new once," which reassured her.

questions.stem: In this passage, her stomach felt "tight" most nearly means
questions.explanation: On her first day, with cold hands and not knowing anything, "tight" points to nervous, not full or calm.

lesson.strategy: This is a short story about a first day at work. Track Nadia's feelings, not the bread. She starts cold and nervous, hears one kind line from Cole, and ends tired but proud. Watch how each small win changes her mood.

signals.phrase: her first day
signals.means: Sets the situation. Everything that follows is Nadia learning something new for the first time.

signals.phrase: Ask me anything
signals.means: Cole's kind offer. It tells you the bakery is a safe place to be new and to ask for help.

signals.phrase: no one shouted
signals.means: The mistake is not a disaster. This calm reaction shows she is allowed to get things wrong while learning.

signals.phrase: tired, but she was proud
signals.means: The turn in her mood. The hard morning ends in a good feeling, and that is the point.

vocab.inContext: a cloth you tie on to keep your clothes clean while you cook or bake
the soft mix of flour and water that becomes bread when baked
the good feeling you get after doing something hard well

skillTips.main-idea: The story is not about bread. Follow Nadia from "her first day" to "tired, but she was proud." The main idea is what she learned by asking, not how a bakery works.
skillTips.inference: The text never says Nadia is brave. You see it: she asks many questions and writes each step down. Judge her by what she does.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "tight," use the clues around it: cold hands, a first day, not knowing where things are. These point to nervous, not full or calm.
skillTips.detail: Small facts sit in order. Who gave the apron, what she wrote, what she dropped. Reread the middle to find the exact thing asked.

passages.title: Pack Light
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: A heavy bag makes a trip harder. You carry it through stations, up stairs, and onto buses. So many travelers learn one simple rule: pack light. Start by laying out everything you think you need. Then take away half of it. Most people bring more clothes than they will ever wear. Choose a few items in colors that match, so you can mix them. Wear your heaviest shoes on the plane instead of packing them. Roll your clothes to save space. Keep what is important close: your papers, your money, and your phone. A light bag is easy to carry and quick to pack. You spend less time waiting and more time seeing the place you came to see. The best trips often fit in one small bag.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Why and how to travel with a lighter bag
How to choose a good hotel
Which shoes are best for flying
How to save money on flights
questions.explanation: The whole passage gives reasons and steps for packing a lighter bag.

questions.stem: What does the passage say to do after laying out everything you need?
questions.explanation: The text says: "Then take away half of it."

questions.stem: Why does the passage say to wear your heaviest shoes on the plane?
choices: So they do not take up weight and space in the bag
Because shoes are not allowed in bags
Because new shoes hurt your feet
questions.explanation: Wearing the shoes keeps them out of the bag, which fits the goal of a light bag.

questions.stem: In this passage, "items" most nearly means
questions.explanation: "A few items in colors that match" refers to things like clothes; "items" means objects.

lesson.strategy: This gives advice on how to travel with less. Read it as a list of steps: lay out, cut in half, match colors, wear heavy shoes, roll clothes, keep valuables close. The last lines say why a light bag is worth it.

signals.phrase: pack light
signals.means: The main advice, named early. Every step after this serves the same goal: carry less.

signals.phrase: take away half of it
signals.means: The strongest tip. It tells you most of what you plan to bring is not needed.

signals.phrase: Roll your clothes to save space
signals.means: A concrete how-to. Rolling, not folding, fits more into a small bag.

signals.phrase: The best trips often fit in one small bag
signals.means: The closing point. Less to carry means more time to enjoy the trip.

vocab.inContext: people who take trips from one place to another
single things, like a shirt, a book, or a pair of shoes
go well together, so clothes can be worn in different pairs

skillTips.main-idea: The point is not any single tip. Tie the steps together under "pack light" and the closing line about a small bag.
skillTips.inference: The passage never says a heavy bag ruins a trip in plain words. Infer it from "You spend less time waiting and more time seeing."
skillTips.vocabulary: For "items," look at the examples near it: clothes, shoes, papers, money, phone. "Items" is the general word for all these things.
skillTips.detail: The steps come in order. To answer a "what to do" question, find the matching step: after laying out, you "take away half of it."

passages.title: A Cheap, Good Meal
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Dried beans cost little and keep for a long time. A bag of them can sit in your cupboard for months without going bad. When you are ready, you soak the beans in water overnight. The next day they are soft enough to cook. Beans and rice together make a full meal. Beans give your body protein, the same thing that meat gives. Rice fills you up and costs very little. People all over the world eat this pair, from Mexico to India to West Africa. You can change the taste each time with different spices, so you never grow tired of it. A large pot feeds a whole family and costs less than a single fast-food lunch. Simple food, made at home, can be both cheap and good for you.

questions.stem: What is the main point of the passage?
choices: Beans and rice make a cheap, healthy, and filling meal
Meat is always better than beans
Rice is very hard to cook well
Spices are too expensive for most people
questions.explanation: The passage gives reason after reason that beans and rice are cheap and good for you.

questions.stem: According to the text, what do you do to dried beans before cooking?
questions.explanation: The text says: "you soak the beans in water overnight."

questions.stem: Why does the passage mention Mexico, India, and West Africa?
choices: To show that people in many places rely on this cheap, healthy pair
To prove beans grow only in those places
To say those foods all taste the same
To explain how rice is farmed
questions.explanation: Naming many far-apart places supports the idea that this meal works well for people everywhere.

questions.stem: In this passage, "protein" is best understood as
choices: a part of food your body needs, also found in meat
questions.explanation: The text explains protein as "the same thing that meat gives" to your body.

lesson.strategy: This explains why beans and rice make a smart meal. Read for the reasons: cheap, keeps a long time, full of protein, eaten worldwide. Each sentence adds one more reason to the same claim in the last line.

signals.phrase: cost little and keep for a long time
signals.means: Two early reasons beans are smart: they are cheap and they do not go bad fast.

signals.phrase: make a full meal
signals.means: The core claim. Together, beans and rice give you what one meal needs.

signals.phrase: People all over the world eat this pair
signals.means: Support by example. If many places rely on it, the meal must work well.

signals.phrase: both cheap and good for you
signals.means: The point, stated at the end. Cheap and healthy do not have to be opposites.

vocab.inContext: a closed shelf in a kitchen where you store food
leave in water for a long time so the beans grow soft
a part of food the body needs to grow and stay strong, also in meat

skillTips.main-idea: Do not stop at one fact like cost. Collect all the reasons and match them to the last line: the meal is "both cheap and good for you."
skillTips.inference: The list of countries is not just decoration. Ask why the writer names them. It hints the meal is trusted in many places, not just one.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "protein," read the clue beside it: "the same thing that meat gives." That tells you what protein does without a hard science word.
skillTips.detail: Steps and facts are spread across the passage. To find what you do before cooking, scan for the action: you "soak the beans in water overnight."

passages.title: A Little at a Time
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Saving money feels hard when you do not earn much. But you do not need a large amount to start. You need a habit. Put away a small sum each week, even if it is only a few dollars. Keep it in a place you cannot easily reach, like a separate account. Do not wait until the end of the month to see what is left, because often nothing is left. Instead, save first, then spend the rest. Over a year, small weekly savings add up to a real sum. More important, you build the habit of saving. Once saving feels normal, you keep doing it as you earn more. A person who saves a little for many years often ends up safer than one who earns a lot and saves nothing.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: How saving a small amount often, as a habit, builds up over time
How to earn a bigger salary
Why banks pay interest on savings
How to make a monthly food budget
questions.explanation: The passage centers on saving small amounts regularly and building the habit.

questions.stem: According to the text, when should you save?
choices: At the end of the month
First, before you spend the rest
Only when you earn a lot
questions.explanation: The text says: "save first, then spend the rest."

questions.stem: Why does the passage say to keep savings "in a place you cannot easily reach"?
choices: So you are not tempted to spend the money you set aside
Because cash is not safe at home
Because the money grows faster there
questions.explanation: Money that is hard to reach is harder to spend on a whim, which protects your savings.

questions.stem: In this passage, "habit" most nearly means
choices: something you do again and again until it feels normal
a strict rule from a bank
questions.explanation: The text ties "habit" to saving each week until it "feels normal."

lesson.strategy: This gives advice on saving money. Read for the one main move: save a small amount first, and do it every week. The passage repeats that the habit matters more than the size. The last line compares two people to prove the point.

signals.phrase: You need a habit
signals.means: The real message. Not a big sum, but a repeated action is what makes saving work.

signals.phrase: save first, then spend the rest
signals.means: The key rule. Set money aside before spending, or nothing will be left.

signals.phrase: small weekly savings add up to a real sum
signals.means: The payoff. Tiny amounts, repeated over a year, grow into something that matters.

signals.phrase: often ends up safer
signals.means: The closing claim. Steady saving beats high earning with no saving.

vocab.inContext: an amount of money, small or large
a place at a bank where you keep money apart from your cash
an action you repeat so often it becomes automatic

skillTips.main-idea: The point is the habit, not the dollars. Connect "You need a habit" with the last line about who ends up "safer."
skillTips.inference: The text says keep savings where you "cannot easily reach." Ask why. The reason is left unsaid: hard-to-reach money is harder to spend.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "habit," use the words around it: "each week," "keep doing it," "feels normal." These show a habit is a repeated action.
skillTips.detail: The advice is exact. On when to save, the text says "save first, then spend the rest," not at the end of the month.

passages.title: The Simple Walk
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: You do not need a gym or special gear to care for your body. You only need to walk. A walk each day is one of the easiest ways to stay well. It costs nothing. When you walk, your heart beats faster and grows stronger. Your legs and back feel less stiff. Many people also notice that a walk clears the mind. A worry that felt heavy at your desk can feel lighter after twenty minutes outside. You do not have to walk fast or far. A slow walk still helps. Try to make it a daily habit: after lunch, or before bed, or on the way to the store. Leave the car and use your feet when you can. Small, steady movement, done often, keeps the body and mind in better shape.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: A daily walk is a simple, free way to help both body and mind
Gyms are a waste of money
Walking fast is the only good exercise
You should never drive a car
questions.explanation: The passage praises a daily walk as an easy, free help for both body and mind.

questions.stem: According to the text, what happens to your heart when you walk?
choices: It beats faster and grows stronger
questions.explanation: The text says: "your heart beats faster and grows stronger."

questions.stem: Why does the passage say a heavy worry can "feel lighter after twenty minutes outside"?
choices: Because problems solve themselves in time
Because walking can calm the mind, not only exercise the body
Because walking makes you forget on purpose
Because fresh air removes all stress fully
questions.explanation: The line about a worry feeling lighter shows walking helps the mind, not just the body.

questions.stem: In this passage, "stiff" most nearly means
choices: hard to bend or move easily
questions.explanation: Legs and back "feel less stiff" after walking; stiff means hard to bend or move.

lesson.strategy: This is health advice with one clear message: walk every day. Read for the two kinds of good it names, body and mind, and the reasons under each. The end tells you how to fit walking into normal days.

signals.phrase: You only need to walk
signals.means: The whole message in one line. No gym, no gear, just walking.

signals.phrase: your heart beats faster and grows stronger
signals.means: The body benefit. This is the physical reason walking helps.

signals.phrase: a walk clears the mind
signals.means: The second benefit, for the mind. Walking helps how you feel, not just your body.

signals.phrase: make it a daily habit
signals.means: The practical step. The good comes from doing it often, not once.

vocab.inContext: special equipment or tools, like the kind used in a gym
hard to bend; a body that has not moved feels this way
even and regular, done again and again without stopping

skillTips.main-idea: The passage praises two gains: body and mind. Join "heart... grows stronger" and "clears the mind" to state the full main idea.
skillTips.inference: The line about a worry feeling "lighter" is not about the body. Infer that walking helps the mind, since the writer moves from muscles to worries.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "stiff," use the body clue: legs and back that "feel less stiff" after walking. Stiff means hard to move, the opposite of loose.
skillTips.detail: The body facts are stated plainly. On the heart, the text says it "beats faster and grows stronger." Find the exact line first.

passages.title: The Dawn Chorus
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Before the sun is fully up, many birds begin to sing. If you wake early, you can hear them: one call, then another, then many at once. People call this the dawn chorus. But why do birds sing so much at dawn? In the early morning, the air is often still and cool. Sound travels farther when the air is calm, so a bird's song can reach much farther than it would at noon. It is also too dark to hunt for food, so the birds use the time to call instead. A male bird sings to say two things: this is my space, and I am strong and healthy. Females listen and choose. By the time the sun is high, the singing slows down. The birds go quiet and get to work finding food.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Why many birds sing loudly at dawn
Which birds sing the best songs
Why birds fly south in winter
questions.explanation: The passage asks and answers why birds sing so much at dawn.

questions.stem: According to the text, why does sound travel farther at dawn?
choices: The birds sing much louder then
The air is often still and cool
The sun makes sound move faster
questions.explanation: The text says "the air is often still and cool" and "Sound travels farther when the air is calm."

questions.stem: Why do birds sing at dawn instead of hunting?
choices: They are not hungry in the morning
It is too dark to find food, so they use the time to call
Singing gives them energy to hunt
questions.explanation: The text says it is "too dark to hunt for food, so the birds use the time to call instead."

questions.stem: In this passage, "chorus" most nearly means
choices: many voices singing together at once
questions.explanation: The opening picture, "one call, then another, then many at once," shows a chorus is many voices together.

lesson.strategy: This asks and answers one question: why do birds sing at dawn? Read for the question mark in the middle, then collect the reasons that follow. Each reason is a separate cause of the same behavior.

signals.phrase: the dawn chorus
signals.means: Names the thing the passage explains: the wave of bird song at sunrise.

signals.phrase: why do birds sing so much at dawn?
signals.means: The question the whole passage answers. Every line after this is a reason.

signals.phrase: Sound travels farther when the air is calm
signals.means: Reason one. Still morning air carries a song a long way.

signals.phrase: this is my space, and I am strong and healthy
signals.means: The message in the song. Birds sing to claim ground and attract a mate.

vocab.inContext: the time when the sun first begins to rise
many voices or songs heard together at the same time
still and quiet, with little or no wind

skillTips.main-idea: The middle question, "why do birds sing so much at dawn?", is your guide. The main idea is the set of answers, not the sound itself.
skillTips.inference: The text says it is "too dark to hunt." You infer why that matters: with no hunting to do, the birds have free time to sing.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "chorus," use the picture in the first lines: "one call, then another, then many at once." A chorus is many voices together.
skillTips.detail: Reasons are listed one by one. On why sound carries, the text says the air is "still and cool." Point to that line, not a guess.

passages.title: The Food Cart
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Every day at noon, a line forms at Sami's food cart. He parks it on the same corner, near the tall office buildings. He sells warm rice bowls, and he knows many of his buyers by name. "The usual?" he asks a woman in a gray coat. She nods and smiles. Sami works fast, but he never rushes a person who is not sure. He lets them look and ask. His prices are fair, and the food is fresh, cooked that morning. Some days it rains, and the line is short. Sami still comes. He says the people who show up in the rain are his best customers, and he wants them to find him there. Over the years, the corner has become a small meeting place. People wait, they talk, and they leave full.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: A food cart owner who builds a loyal following through fair prices and care
How to cook rice bowls at home
Why office jobs are so stressful
The best corner in town to park a cart
questions.explanation: The passage shows how Sami treats people, which builds a loyal following.

questions.stem: Where does Sami park his cart?
choices: On the same corner, near the tall office buildings
In a different spot each day
questions.explanation: The text says he "parks it on the same corner, near the tall office buildings."

questions.stem: Why does Sami come even when it rains and the line is short?
choices: He has nowhere else to sell
He values the loyal customers who still show up
Rice sells better in the rain
He does not notice the weather
questions.explanation: He calls the rainy-day crowd "his best customers" and wants them to find him there.

questions.stem: In this passage, "customers" most nearly means
choices: the people who buy from him
questions.explanation: The customers are the buyers who "wait, they talk, and they leave full."

lesson.strategy: Short fiction about a food seller. Do not read it as a menu. Track how Sami treats people: he learns names, he does not rush, he shows up in the rain. Those choices, not the food, are the point.

signals.phrase: he knows many of his buyers by name
signals.means: First sign of who Sami is. He treats buyers as people, not just sales.

signals.phrase: he never rushes a person who is not sure
signals.means: A choice about care. Patience matters to him more than speed.

signals.phrase: his best customers
signals.means: Sami's own view. The loyal rainy-day crowd is who he values most.

signals.phrase: a small meeting place
signals.means: The result of his care. The corner became more than a place to buy lunch.

vocab.inContext: a small movable stand on wheels used to sell food
honest and reasonable, not too high
people who buy something from a seller

skillTips.main-idea: The food is background. Follow how Sami acts toward people, from knowing names to coming in the rain. That behavior is the main idea.
skillTips.inference: Coming in the rain for a short line makes no money sense. Infer his reason from "his best customers" -- he shows up for loyal people.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "customers," look at what they do: they buy, they wait, they leave full. Customers are the people who buy from the seller.
skillTips.detail: Small facts are exact. Where he parks, what he sells, what he asks the woman. Reread the lines to match the detail.

passages.title: Asking the Way
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Marcus stood on a busy street in a city he did not know. His map made no sense. The street signs were in a language he could not read. He needed to find the train station, and his train left in an hour. For a while he was too shy to ask anyone. Then he stopped an older woman with kind eyes. He did not share her language, so he simply said the word "station" and held up his ticket. The woman understood at once. She pointed down the street, held up two fingers, and made a left turn with her hand. Marcus thanked her and followed her signs. Two streets later, he turned left, and there it was: the station. He had almost missed his train because he was afraid to ask. A few simple words and a kind stranger had saved him.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: A traveler learns that asking for help, even without a shared language, can save him
How to read a map in a foreign language
Why trains are so often late
The best way to learn a new language
questions.explanation: The story shows Marcus overcoming his fear to ask, and being saved by a stranger's help.

questions.stem: How did Marcus tell the woman what he needed?
choices: He drew a picture of a train
He said the word "station" and held up his ticket
He showed her a photo on his phone
questions.explanation: The text says he "said the word 'station' and held up his ticket."

questions.stem: Why did Marcus almost miss his train?
choices: He was too shy to ask for help sooner
The woman gave him wrong directions
questions.explanation: He was "too shy to ask anyone" "for a while," and that delay nearly cost him the train.

questions.stem: In this passage, "stranger" most nearly means
choices: a person you do not know
questions.explanation: The woman is someone Marcus has never met, so "stranger" means a person you do not know.

lesson.strategy: Short fiction about being lost in a strange city. Follow Marcus's problem and how he solves it: a map he cannot read, a fear of asking, then one brave choice. The last line names the lesson.

signals.phrase: too shy to ask anyone
signals.means: Marcus's block. His fear, not the map, is the real problem to overcome.

signals.phrase: held up his ticket
signals.means: How he crosses the language gap. One word and a gesture instead of full speech.

signals.phrase: understood at once
signals.means: The turn. Even without a shared language, the stranger gets his meaning.

signals.phrase: afraid to ask
signals.means: The lesson, named near the end. His fear nearly cost him the train.

vocab.inContext: nervous about speaking to people you do not know
a person you have never met before
hand movements used to show meaning without words

skillTips.main-idea: The map is a side detail. The story is about Marcus choosing to ask despite fear. Tie "too shy to ask" to "afraid to ask" at the end.
skillTips.inference: The text says he was "too shy to ask anyone" "for a while." Infer that this delay is why he "almost missed his train."
skillTips.vocabulary: For "stranger," use the story: the woman is someone Marcus has never met. A stranger is a person you do not know.
skillTips.detail: The steps are exact. How he asked (one word, his ticket), what the woman did (pointed, two fingers, left turn). Reread to match.

passages.title: A Free Place to Learn
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: In almost every town there is a place where you can sit, read, and learn without paying a cent. It is the public library. With a library card, which is free, you can borrow books and take them home. You can read the news, use a computer, or find a quiet corner to study. Many people do not know how much a library offers. Some hold classes to help adults learn to read or use a computer. Some lend tools, games, or even musical instruments. A library is warm in winter and cool in summer, open to anyone who walks in. You do not have to buy anything. You do not have to be rich or young. For a person who wants to learn but has little money, the library may be the most useful place in the whole town.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: The public library is a free place where anyone can learn and borrow
Books cost too much to buy
Every town needs a bigger library
questions.explanation: The passage lists the free services a public library offers to anyone.

questions.stem: According to the text, what do you need to borrow books and take them home?
choices: A library card, which is free
questions.explanation: The text says: "With a library card, which is free, you can borrow books."

questions.stem: Why does the passage say the library may be "the most useful place" for someone with little money?
choices: It is the warmest building in town
It offers learning and resources without any cost
It is the only place with computers
questions.explanation: The library's services are free, so a person with little money gains the most from them.

questions.stem: In this passage, "borrow" most nearly means
choices: take for a while and then return
questions.explanation: You "take them home" from a library and, by its nature, return them; borrow means take for a while.

lesson.strategy: This praises one thing: the free public library. Read for the list of what it offers and the one idea tying them together, that all of it is free. The last line says who benefits most.

signals.phrase: without paying a cent
signals.means: The key fact, stated up front. Everything the library gives costs nothing.

signals.phrase: a library card, which is free
signals.means: The one thing you need, and even that is free. It opens all the rest.

signals.phrase: Many people do not know how much a library offers
signals.means: A turn to the surprise: the library does far more than lend books.

signals.phrase: the most useful place in the whole town
signals.means: The closing claim, aimed at people with little money.

vocab.inContext: open to everyone, paid for by the town, not by you
take something for a while and give it back later
gives or makes available to people who want it

skillTips.main-idea: The passage is one long list of free services. Sum them under the idea that a library gives learning "without paying a cent."
skillTips.inference: The last line calls the library "most useful" for people with little money. Infer why: it gives learning and tools that others must pay for.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "borrow," note you "take them home" and, by a library's nature, return them. Borrow means take for a while, then give back.
skillTips.detail: Facts are stated plainly. To borrow books you need "a library card, which is free." Find the exact line before choosing.

passages.title: Rest Your Eyes
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Many people now spend hours looking at a screen. After a long day of it, the eyes can feel dry, tired, and sore. This happens partly because we blink less when we stare at a screen. We also hold our eyes at one short distance for too long. There is a simple way to help, sometimes called the 20-20-20 rule. Every twenty minutes, look at something far away, about twenty feet, for twenty seconds. This lets the eye relax and change its focus. Blinking on purpose also helps, since it wets the eye again. It helps to sit a little farther from the screen and to keep the room softly lit, not too bright or too dark. Your eyes were made to look near and far all day. A few short breaks give them the change they need.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Simple habits can rest tired eyes after long screen use
Screens are bad and should be avoided
The eye cannot be helped once it is tired
Bright rooms are always best for reading
questions.explanation: The passage explains why screens tire the eyes and gives simple habits to help.

questions.stem: What does the 20-20-20 rule tell you to do every twenty minutes?
choices: Close your eyes for twenty minutes
Look at something far away for twenty seconds
Walk twenty steps away from the desk
Blink twenty times as fast as you can
questions.explanation: The rule is: every twenty minutes, look far away, about twenty feet, for twenty seconds.

questions.stem: Why do the eyes feel dry after staring at a screen?
choices: Because we blink less when we stare
Because the room is too dark
Because the eyes grow too strong
questions.explanation: The text ties dry eyes to the fact that "we blink less when we stare at a screen."

questions.stem: In this passage, "focus" most nearly means
choices: how the eye sets itself to see clearly at a distance
questions.explanation: The eye must "change its focus" from near to far, so focus is how it sets itself to see clearly.

lesson.strategy: This explains why screens tire the eyes and how to help. Read for two parts: the causes of tired eyes, then the fixes. The 20-20-20 rule is the main tip, so hold its three numbers clearly.

signals.phrase: we blink less when we stare at a screen
signals.means: A cause of tired eyes. Less blinking means a drier eye.

signals.phrase: the 20-20-20 rule
signals.means: The main fix, given a name so it is easy to remember.

signals.phrase: look at something far away
signals.means: The core action of the rule. Changing distance lets the eye relax.

signals.phrase: A few short breaks give them the change they need
signals.means: The closing point. Small pauses, not big changes, do the job.

vocab.inContext: aching or painful in a dull way
the way the eye adjusts to see clearly near or far
close and open the eyes quickly, which wets them

skillTips.main-idea: The passage has causes and fixes, but its message is hopeful: simple breaks help. Tie the fixes to "the change they need."
skillTips.inference: The text pairs "dry" eyes with "we blink less." Infer the link: less blinking leaves the eye dry, so blinking on purpose helps.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "focus," use the action near it: the eye must "change its focus" from near to far. Focus is how the eye sets itself to see clearly.
skillTips.detail: The rule has exact numbers. Every twenty minutes, look about twenty feet away for twenty seconds. Match all three before choosing.

passages.title: The Night Shift
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: While the city sleeps, Priya is already at work. She is a baker, and her day begins at two in the morning, long before the sun. The streets are empty and quiet as she walks to the shop. Inside, the ovens are cold, and she must bring them to life. She mixes the dough she prepared the night before and shapes each loaf by hand. The work is steady and warm, and she likes the peace of these dark hours. There is no one to serve yet, only bread to make. By the time the first customers arrive at six, the shelves are full and the whole shop smells of fresh bread. Priya rarely sees the busy afternoon; by then she is home and asleep. Most people would find her hours strange. But she has come to love the quiet start, when the city is still and the day belongs only to her.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: A night baker who finds quiet joy in working while the city sleeps
How to bake bread at home
Why bakeries open so early in the morning
The best time of day to buy fresh bread
questions.explanation: The passage centers on Priya's night work and how she has come to love its quiet.

questions.stem: What time does Priya's day begin?
questions.explanation: The text says "her day begins at two in the morning."

questions.stem: Why does Priya "rarely see the busy afternoon"?
choices: By then she is home asleep after her early shift
She works a second job in the day
questions.explanation: She starts at two in the morning and is "home and asleep" by afternoon.

questions.stem: In this passage, "steady" most nearly means
choices: even and calm, without sudden change
questions.explanation: "The work is steady and warm" is tied to peace and calm, so steady means even and calm.

lesson.strategy: Short fiction about an unusual work life. Notice the contrast: the city sleeps while Priya works. Track her feelings about the strange hours. Most would dislike them, but the last lines show she loves the quiet.

signals.phrase: While the city sleeps, Priya is already at work
signals.means: Sets the contrast: her day starts when everyone else's has not begun.

signals.phrase: she likes the peace of these dark hours
signals.means: Her true feeling. The strange schedule brings her calm, not misery.

signals.phrase: Most people would find her hours strange
signals.means: Names the common view, so the writer can push against it next.

signals.phrase: the day belongs only to her
signals.means: The closing image. The lonely hours feel like a gift, not a burden.

vocab.inContext: the soft flour mix that becomes bread after baking
even and calm, moving at one smooth pace
a whole shaped piece of bread before it is cut

skillTips.main-idea: Two feelings could fit: hardship or peace. The writer lands on peace. Read past "strange" to "the day belongs only to her."
skillTips.inference: The text says she is "home and asleep" by afternoon. Infer why she misses the busy hours: her body follows a night schedule.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "steady," use the pair beside it: "steady and warm," tied to peace and calm. Steady means even, not rushed or unsure.
skillTips.detail: Times are exact. Her day begins "at two in the morning"; customers come "at six." Match the exact time the question names.

passages.title: The Long Train
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: The train would take nine hours to cross the country, and Elena was glad of it. She had chosen the train over the plane on purpose. A plane would have been faster, but from a plane you see nothing but clouds. From the train window, the whole land unrolled before her. First came the flat farms, green and wide, split into neat squares. Then low hills rose, dotted with sheep. In the afternoon the train climbed into mountains, and the air grew thin and cold. Elena did not read her book. She simply watched, as one town after another slid past, each with its own small station and its own name. She thought about the people who lived in these places she would never visit. By the time the lights of the far city appeared, she felt she had truly traveled, not just arrived. The slow way, she decided, had shown her more.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: A traveler who chooses the slow train to truly see the land, not just arrive
Why trains cost less than planes
How to pack for a very long trip
Why mountains are hard to cross by train
questions.explanation: The passage shows Elena choosing the train so she can watch the land and feel she has traveled.

questions.stem: How long would the train take to cross the country?
questions.explanation: The text says "The train would take nine hours to cross the country."

questions.stem: Why did Elena choose the train over the plane?
choices: She wanted to see the land, which a plane would hide behind clouds
questions.explanation: She notes that "from a plane you see nothing but clouds," while the train shows the whole land.

questions.stem: In this passage, "unrolled" most nearly means
choices: opened up and spread out to view
questions.explanation: "The whole land unrolled before her" means it spread open into view like a mat unrolling.

lesson.strategy: Short fiction about choosing a slow journey. The key is Elena's choice: train, not plane, on purpose. Follow the land she watches change, hour by hour, and the reason the slow way meant more to her.

signals.phrase: she had chosen the train over the plane on purpose
signals.means: The central choice. She picked slow over fast for a reason.

signals.phrase: from a plane you see nothing but clouds
signals.means: Her reason. The plane hides the land; the train shows it.

signals.phrase: the whole land unrolled before her
signals.means: The reward of the choice. She gets to watch the country change.

signals.phrase: she felt she had truly traveled, not just arrived
signals.means: The point. The journey itself, not the ending, was the value.

vocab.inContext: spread open into view, the way a rolled mat opens flat
less full; high in the mountains the air has less to breathe
moved by smoothly, as seen from the moving train

skillTips.main-idea: The changing scenery is support, not the point. The main idea is Elena's belief that "the slow way... had shown her more."
skillTips.inference: She never says she dislikes planes. Infer her reason from "from a plane you see nothing but clouds" set against the rich train view.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "unrolled," picture the land opening like a mat: "the whole land unrolled before her." It means spread out into view.
skillTips.detail: The trip has exact steps: nine hours, flat farms, low hills, then mountains. Follow that order to place any detail the question asks.

passages.title: Why We Sleep
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: We spend about a third of our lives asleep, yet many people treat sleep as wasted time. In truth, the body and brain are hard at work while we rest. During sleep, the body repairs muscles and heals small hurts from the day. The brain does something just as important: it sorts through what we learned and stores the parts worth keeping. This is one reason students who sleep well remember more than those who stay up all night to study. Sleep also clears waste from the brain that builds up while we are awake. When we do not get enough, we feel it. We think slowly, forget easily, and grow short of temper. A single bad night is not serious, but weeks of poor sleep harm both health and mood. Sleep is not lost time. It is the quiet work that makes the waking hours possible.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Sleep is active, useful time when the body and brain do vital work
Students should never study at night
The brain shuts fully off during sleep
Everyone must sleep exactly eight hours
questions.explanation: The passage argues that sleep is not wasted time but active, useful work.

questions.stem: According to the text, what does the brain do during sleep?
choices: It sorts what we learned and stores what is worth keeping
It heals cuts on the skin
questions.explanation: The text says the brain "sorts through what we learned and stores the parts worth keeping."

questions.stem: Why do students who sleep well remember more than those who stay up all night?
choices: Staying awake erases memory on purpose
Sleep helps the brain store what was learned
The brain learns only while asleep
questions.explanation: Since sleep "stores the parts worth keeping," missing sleep loses some of what was learned.

questions.stem: In this passage, "repairs" most nearly means
questions.explanation: The body "repairs muscles" and "heals small hurts," so repairs means fixes what was damaged.

lesson.strategy: This argues against a common idea: that sleep is wasted time. Read for the jobs sleep does, one for the body and one for the brain, then what happens when we miss it. The last line answers the false idea.

signals.phrase: many people treat sleep as wasted time
signals.means: The wrong idea the passage sets out to correct.

signals.phrase: the body and brain are hard at work while we rest
signals.means: The main claim. Sleep is active, not idle.

signals.phrase: stores the parts worth keeping
signals.means: The brain's key job. Sleep saves what you learned, which is why it helps memory.

signals.phrase: Sleep is not lost time
signals.means: The answer to the opening idea, stated plainly at the end.

vocab.inContext: fixes or mends what was worn or hurt during the day
goes through things and separates what to keep from what to drop
your mood; "short of temper" means easily upset

skillTips.main-idea: The passage opens with a wrong idea and closes by fixing it. Tie "wasted time" to "Sleep is not lost time" for the main idea.
skillTips.inference: The text does not spell out why sleepy students forget. Infer it from the brain's job: sleep "stores" learning, so missing sleep loses it.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "repairs," note what it acts on: "muscles" and "small hurts." To repair is to fix what was damaged.
skillTips.detail: Two systems do two jobs. The body "repairs muscles"; the brain "sorts" and "stores." Match the job to the right one.

passages.title: Money for a Rainy Day
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Life brings surprises, and many of them cost money. A car breaks down. A tooth needs fixing. A job ends without warning. People who have no savings must borrow when these things happen, often at a high cost. That is why many advisers suggest keeping an emergency fund. This is a pot of money set aside for hard times and nothing else. A common goal is to save enough to cover three months of basic costs, such as rent and food. That amount is not easy to reach, and it may take years. The trick is to build it slowly and to leave it alone. It is not for a holiday or a new phone. It is only for a true emergency. People with such a fund sleep better, because a sudden bill does not become a crisis. The fund turns a disaster into a mere problem.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: An emergency fund: savings set aside only for hard times
How to get a loan quickly
Why cars break down so often
The best way to spend a bonus
questions.explanation: The passage explains what an emergency fund is, what it is for, and why it helps.

questions.stem: According to the text, how much is a common goal for an emergency fund?
choices: Enough to cover three months of basic costs
The price of a new car
questions.explanation: The text says a common goal is "to save enough to cover three months of basic costs."

questions.stem: Why do people with an emergency fund "sleep better"?
choices: They no longer have any bills
A sudden bill does not turn into a crisis for them
The fund earns them extra money
questions.explanation: The text says a sudden bill "does not become a crisis" for people who have a fund.

questions.stem: In this passage, "emergency" most nearly means
choices: a sudden serious event that needs quick action
questions.explanation: The examples -- a broken car, a lost job, a sudden bill -- show an emergency is a sudden serious event.

lesson.strategy: This explains a money idea: the emergency fund. Read for what it is, what it is for, and what it is not for. The examples at the start show the kind of surprise it guards against. The last two lines say the payoff.

signals.phrase: an emergency fund
signals.means: Names the idea the whole passage explains: money kept for hard times.

signals.phrase: three months of basic costs
signals.means: The common goal. It tells you how big the fund should be.

signals.phrase: to build it slowly and to leave it alone
signals.means: The method. Add to it over time and do not touch it for wants.

signals.phrase: turns a disaster into a mere problem
signals.means: The payoff. The fund shrinks a crisis down to a manageable size.

vocab.inContext: people who give guidance, here about money
a sudden serious event that needs money and action right away
a very bad moment that is hard to handle

skillTips.main-idea: The examples of car and tooth are support. The main idea is the fund itself: money "set aside for hard times and nothing else."
skillTips.inference: The text says the fund lets people "sleep better." Infer why from the next line: a sudden bill "does not become a crisis."
skillTips.vocabulary: For "emergency," use the examples: a broken car, a lost job, a sudden bill. An emergency is a sudden serious event, not a want.
skillTips.detail: The size goal is exact: "three months of basic costs, such as rent and food." Match that figure, not a rounder guess.

passages.title: Otters at Play
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: River otters seem to treat life as a game. Along quiet rivers and lakes, they slide down muddy or snowy banks again and again, one after another, as if for the pure fun of it. But this play has a purpose. Through it, young otters build the strength and skill they will need to hunt. An otter's body is made for water. Its fur is thick and traps a layer of air that keeps the skin dry and warm, even in cold rivers. Webbed feet and a strong tail push it through the water with ease. Otters hunt fish, frogs, and other small animals, diving and turning faster than their prey can flee. They live in family groups and seem to enjoy each other's company. To watch an otter is to see an animal that is very good at staying alive and, at the same time, appears to be having a wonderful time.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: River otters are playful animals whose play also builds hunting skill
Otters are the fastest animals in any water
How rivers form muddy banks over time
Why otters are so hard to find
questions.explanation: The passage describes the otter's playful nature and how that play and its body fit it for survival.

questions.stem: According to the text, what keeps an otter's skin dry and warm?
choices: Thick fur that traps a layer of air
questions.explanation: The text says the fur "traps a layer of air that keeps the skin dry and warm."

questions.stem: Why is the otters' play more than just fun?
choices: It keeps them warm in the cold
It helps young otters build strength and skill for hunting
It helps them build their homes
questions.explanation: The text says the play "has a purpose" and builds "strength and skill... to hunt."

questions.stem: In this passage, "prey" most nearly means
choices: the animals an otter hunts for food
questions.explanation: Otters hunt fish and frogs "faster than their prey can flee," so prey means the hunted animals.

lesson.strategy: This describes the river otter. Notice the twist: the play looks like fun but has a purpose. Read for how the otter's body fits water, and for the mix of skill and joy the writer points out at the end.

signals.phrase: seem to treat life as a game
signals.means: The opening image. Otters look playful, which the passage will both show and explain.

signals.phrase: this play has a purpose
signals.means: The turn. The fun is not only fun; it trains young otters to hunt.

signals.phrase: An otter's body is made for water
signals.means: Shifts to how the animal is built. The fur, feet, and tail all fit water life.

signals.phrase: very good at staying alive
signals.means: The closing balance: the otter is both skilled at survival and full of joy.

vocab.inContext: the sloping sides of a river or lake, here muddy or snowy
with skin between the toes, which helps push through water
the smaller animals a hunter chases and eats

skillTips.main-idea: Do not stop at "otters play." The passage pairs play with purpose and survival. The main idea joins the fun to the skill it builds.
skillTips.inference: The text says play "has a purpose" and builds "strength and skill... to hunt." Infer that the games are really practice for survival.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "prey," look at what the otter does: it hunts "fish, frogs, and other small animals." Those hunted animals are its prey.
skillTips.detail: Body facts are specific. Warmth comes from fur that "traps a layer of air," not from fat. Match the exact cause the text gives.

passages.title: A World of Tea
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: After water, tea is the most common drink in the world. It is made in a simple way: pour hot water over the dried leaves of the tea plant, and wait. Yet from this one plant, people have built countless customs. In Japan, a tea ceremony can last for hours and follows careful, quiet steps. In England, tea often comes with milk in the afternoon. In Morocco, green tea is poured with mint and plenty of sugar, from high above the glass to make a foam on top. In many places, offering tea to a guest is a sign of welcome, and to refuse it can seem rude. The tea plant grows best on cool, wet hillsides, and its leaves are still picked by hand in many countries. One plant, one hot pot of water, and yet a thousand ways to share a warm drink with another person.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: One tea plant gives rise to many different customs around the world
How to grow tea on a hillside
Why tea is better than coffee
The one correct way to make green tea
questions.explanation: The passage shows how one simple plant leads to many tea customs across countries.

questions.stem: According to the text, how is tea served in Morocco?
choices: Green tea poured with mint and sugar to make a foam
Cold, with no sugar at all
questions.explanation: The text says in Morocco green tea is poured "with mint and plenty of sugar... to make a foam on top."

questions.stem: Why might refusing tea seem rude in many places?
choices: Tea is very expensive to make
Offering tea is a sign of welcome, so refusing rejects that welcome
The host will have nothing else to drink
The tea will simply go to waste
questions.explanation: Since offering tea is "a sign of welcome," turning it down rejects the welcome itself.

questions.stem: In this passage, "customs" most nearly means
choices: the usual ways a group of people does things
questions.explanation: The examples -- ceremonies, milk, mint and foam -- are a group's usual ways, so customs means usual ways.

lesson.strategy: This shows how one simple thing, tea, becomes many customs. Read the middle as a set of examples from different countries, each a different way to serve tea. The first and last lines frame them: one plant, many customs.

signals.phrase: the most common drink in the world
signals.means: Opens with tea's reach. Sets up why its many customs matter.

signals.phrase: people have built countless customs
signals.means: The main idea in one phrase. One plant, many ways to use it.

signals.phrase: a sign of welcome
signals.means: Lifts tea above taste. In many places it carries meaning, not just flavor.

signals.phrase: a thousand ways to share a warm drink
signals.means: The closing image. Restates the theme: one plant, endless customs.

vocab.inContext: the usual, accepted ways that a group of people do something
a set of formal, careful steps done in a certain order
say no to something that is offered

skillTips.main-idea: The country examples are support, not the point. The main idea frames them: from one plant come "countless customs."
skillTips.inference: The text says offering tea is "a sign of welcome." Infer why refusing seems rude: it turns down the welcome itself.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "customs," the examples explain it: ceremonies, milk in the afternoon, mint and foam. Customs are a group's usual ways.
skillTips.detail: Each country gets its own way. Morocco means mint, sugar, and foam; England means milk. Match the country to its exact custom.

passages.title: The Case for Used
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: A new phone, a new car, a new sofa: the moment you buy them, they lose value. Drive a new car off the lot, and it may be worth thousands less the same day. This is why buying used can be a wise choice. Someone else has already paid for that first drop in price. A used item, checked with care, can serve you just as well for far less money. The key is to check with care. Look closely, ask questions, and test the thing before you pay. For a car, a trusted mechanic can spot hidden trouble. For furniture, look for strong joints and clean lines. Not everything should be bought used; some items, like a mattress or safety gear, are better new. But for many things, used is not a step down. It is a way to get what you need and keep more of your money.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Buying used, when checked carefully, can save money without giving up quality
New things are always better than used ones
Cars lose value too fast to be worth owning
How to sell your old furniture
questions.explanation: The passage argues that used items, checked with care, save money and still serve well.

questions.stem: According to the text, who can spot hidden trouble in a used car?
questions.explanation: The text says "a trusted mechanic can spot hidden trouble."

questions.stem: Why does the passage say a used car's first owner has "already paid for that first drop in price"?
choices: Used cars never lose any value
The biggest loss of value happens right after a car is new
The first owner owes the buyer money
New cars are cheaper than used ones
questions.explanation: A new car loses the most value at once, so the first owner takes that loss, not the used buyer.

questions.stem: In this passage, "value" most nearly means
choices: what something is worth in money
questions.explanation: A car "worth thousands less" has lost value, so value is what a thing is worth in money.

lesson.strategy: This argues for buying used. Read for the reason (new things lose value fast) and the one big condition (check with care). Notice where the writer draws a limit: some things are better new. Balance the advice.

signals.phrase: they lose value
signals.means: The core reason. New things drop in worth fast, so buying used avoids that loss.

signals.phrase: buying used can be a wise choice
signals.means: The main claim, stated plainly early.

signals.phrase: check with care
signals.means: The condition. The advice only holds if you inspect before you buy.

signals.phrase: some items, like a mattress or safety gear, are better new
signals.means: The limit. The writer admits used is not right for everything.

vocab.inContext: how much something is worth, usually in money
already owned by someone else before you, not brand new
the places where parts of furniture are joined together

skillTips.main-idea: The passage argues for used but adds a limit. State both: used is wise "checked with care," though some things are "better new."
skillTips.inference: The text says the first owner paid for "that first drop in price." Infer the rule: a new car loses the most value right away.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "value," use the money clue: a car "worth thousands less" has lost value. Value is what a thing is worth in money.
skillTips.detail: Advice is specific to the item. A "mechanic" checks a car; "strong joints" matter for furniture. Match the check to the right thing.

passages.title: How the Cactus Survives
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: A desert can go months, even years, without rain. Under a burning sun, most plants would dry out and die. Yet the cactus not only survives there, it thrives. Over long ages, the cactus has changed its very shape to hold on to water. Its thick, rounded stem works like a tank. When rain finally comes, the stem swells and fills, storing water for the dry months ahead. A waxy skin covers the plant and seals that water in, so little escapes into the hot air. Most plants have wide, flat leaves, but wide leaves lose water fast. The cactus has traded its leaves for sharp spines. The spines lose almost no water, and they guard the juicy stem from thirsty animals. Shallow roots spread out wide, just under the surface, ready to drink in any rain before it sinks away. Every part of the cactus is built for one task: to save water.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: The cactus has changed its shape in many ways to save water in the desert
Deserts get far more rain than we think
All plants have spines to protect them
How a cactus grows its flowers
questions.explanation: Each feature of the cactus is shown to serve one goal: saving water.

questions.stem: According to the text, what does the cactus have instead of wide, flat leaves?
questions.explanation: The text says "the cactus has traded its leaves for sharp spines."

questions.stem: Why does the cactus have shallow roots that spread out wide?
choices: To hold the plant up in strong wind
To drink in rain quickly before it sinks away
To store extra water inside the roots
questions.explanation: The text says the roots are "ready to drink in any rain before it sinks away."

questions.stem: In this passage, "thrives" most nearly means
questions.explanation: The cactus "not only survives... it thrives," so thrives means does well, more than just survive.

lesson.strategy: This explains how the cactus lives where other plants die. Read it as a list of body parts, each solving the same problem: water. Stem, skin, spines, roots. The last line names the single task they all serve.

signals.phrase: changed its very shape to hold on to water
signals.means: The main idea. Every feature that follows serves this one goal.

signals.phrase: works like a tank
signals.means: The stem's job. It stores water for the long dry months.

signals.phrase: traded its leaves for sharp spines
signals.means: A key change. Spines lose little water and guard the stem, unlike wide leaves.

signals.phrase: built for one task: to save water
signals.means: The closing line. It ties every part back to the same purpose.

vocab.inContext: grows strong and does well, not just survives
grows larger as it fills, here with stored water
sharp points that replace leaves and lose little water

skillTips.main-idea: Each part, stem to roots, points one way. The main idea is the shared goal named at the end: the cactus is "built... to save water."
skillTips.inference: The text says roots are "shallow" and "spread out wide." Infer why: they catch rain fast, near the surface, before it sinks.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "thrives," note the contrast: other plants "die," but the cactus "not only survives... it thrives." Thrives means does well.
skillTips.detail: Each part has a job. Instead of leaves the cactus has "sharp spines"; the stem is the water tank. Match the feature to the part.

passages.title: The Home Office
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: For many jobs today, the office is wherever a laptop opens. Working from home was once rare, but now millions do it. Like most changes, it brings both gains and costs. The gains are real. There is no long drive to work, which saves time and money. A worker can start soon after waking and use the saved hours for family or rest. The home is quiet, without the noise of a busy office. But there are costs too. At home, work and rest share the same walls, and the line between them can blur. Some people find it hard to stop working when the desk is ten steps from the bed. Others feel lonely, missing the small talk and shared lunches of an office. The best home workers set clear limits: a fixed start, a fixed stop, and a space used only for work. Freedom, it turns out, needs a little structure to work well.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Working from home has real gains and real costs, and works best with limits
Offices are always better than homes
How to buy a good laptop for work
questions.explanation: The passage weighs the gains and costs of working from home and advises setting limits.

questions.stem: According to the text, what is one gain of working from home?
choices: More people to talk to each day
No long drive to work, which saves time and money
questions.explanation: The text lists "no long drive to work, which saves time and money" as a gain.

questions.stem: Why can "the line between them blur" at home?
choices: Home lights are too dim to work by
Work and rest happen in the same place, so it is hard to separate them
questions.explanation: The text says work and rest "share the same walls," so the two are hard to keep apart.

questions.stem: In this passage, "blur" most nearly means
choices: become unclear or hard to tell apart
questions.explanation: The border between work and rest "can blur," meaning it becomes unclear.

lesson.strategy: This weighs both sides of working from home. Read for the turn at "But there are costs too." List the gains before it and the costs after it. The last two lines give the writer's advice: set limits.

signals.phrase: both gains and costs
signals.means: The frame. The passage will give reasons for and against, in two groups.

signals.phrase: The gains are real
signals.means: Starts the first side: time saved, money saved, quiet.

signals.phrase: But there are costs too
signals.means: The turn. Everything after weighs against working from home.

signals.phrase: set clear limits
signals.means: The writer's advice. Freedom works only with some structure.

vocab.inContext: become unclear, so two things are hard to tell apart
a clear order or set of rules that shapes the day
fixed lines that mark where work stops and rest begins

skillTips.main-idea: Neither side alone is the point. The main idea holds both: gains and costs, plus the advice to "set clear limits."
skillTips.inference: The text says work and rest "share the same walls." Infer why the line "can blur": one space for both makes them hard to separate.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "blur," use the idea of a fading line: the border between work and rest "can blur." To blur is to make unclear.
skillTips.detail: Gains and costs are listed separately. "No long drive" is a gain; loneliness is a cost. Find the right side before you answer.

passages.title: Why Jet Lag Happens
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Fly across many time zones, and your body arrives before your inner clock does. This tired, foggy feeling is called jet lag. Inside every person is a kind of clock that tells the body when to sleep and when to wake. It is set mostly by light. For a long time this clock matched the sky where you lived. Then a fast plane drops you in a place where noon feels like midnight to your body. Your inner clock still runs on the old time and takes several days to catch up. That is why, in a new time zone, you may feel wide awake at three in the morning and sleepy at noon. Light is the best tool to fix it. Spend time outdoors in daylight, and your clock begins to reset to the new place. Going the slow way, by ship or by train, jet lag barely happens at all, because the body has time to adjust.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Jet lag happens because the body's inner clock lags behind a fast time-zone change
Planes make people tired from their noise
How to sleep well on a long plane
Why some cities are in different time zones
questions.explanation: The passage explains jet lag as the gap between the body clock and a new time zone.

questions.stem: According to the text, what mostly sets the body's inner clock?
questions.explanation: The text says the inner clock "is set mostly by light."

questions.stem: Why does jet lag "barely happen" when you travel by ship or train?
choices: Ships and trains cross no time zones
The slow trip gives the body's clock time to adjust
The body sleeps the whole way
Slow travel is less tiring for the legs
questions.explanation: The text says the slow way works "because the body has time to adjust."

questions.stem: In this passage, "reset" most nearly means
choices: set again to a new time
questions.explanation: The clock "begins to reset to the new place," so reset means set again to a new time.

lesson.strategy: This explains one problem: jet lag. Read for cause and fix. The cause is the gap between your inner clock and the new time zone. The fix is light. The last line adds a contrast with slow travel.

signals.phrase: your body arrives before your inner clock does
signals.means: The core cause, in one line. The clock lags behind the fast trip.

signals.phrase: It is set mostly by light
signals.means: The key fact. Because light sets the clock, light can also fix it.

signals.phrase: takes several days to catch up
signals.means: Why jet lag lasts. The clock resets slowly, not at once.

signals.phrase: Light is the best tool to fix it
signals.means: The solution. Daylight resets the clock to the new place.

vocab.inContext: wide bands of the world that keep the same clock time
set again, here the body clock adjusting to a new time
change slowly to fit new conditions

skillTips.main-idea: The main idea is cause plus effect: a fast trip leaves your "inner clock" behind, and that gap is jet lag. Hold both parts together.
skillTips.inference: The text says light "resets" the clock and slow travel gives "time to adjust." Infer that a slow trip lets the clock keep up.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "reset," use the clock idea: the body clock "begins to reset to the new place." Reset means set again to a new time.
skillTips.detail: The cause is exact: the clock "is set mostly by light," not by food or a phone. Point to the stated cause before choosing.

passages.title: Turning Scraps into Soil
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Every kitchen makes waste: apple cores, coffee grounds, old bread, potato peels. Most of it goes into the trash and then to a dump. But there is an older, better path for this waste. It can become rich soil, through a slow, natural process called composting. Composting is simple. You gather food scraps and yard waste, such as dead leaves, in a pile or a bin. Tiny living things, from worms to bugs to unseen microbes, feed on the waste and break it down. Air and a little water help them work. Over weeks or months, the pile shrinks and darkens, until the scraps are gone and dark, crumbly soil is left. Gardeners call this soil black gold, because it feeds plants so well. Composting turns a daily waste into something useful, keeps trash out of the dump, and gives back to the earth what came from it.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Composting turns food and yard waste into rich soil through a natural process
Gardens need far less water than we think
Worms are harmful to garden plants
How to grow apples at home
questions.explanation: The passage explains how composting changes waste into rich soil, and why it is good.

questions.stem: According to the text, what breaks down the waste in a compost pile?
choices: The heat of the sun alone
Tiny living things like worms, bugs, and microbes
The heavy weight of the pile
questions.explanation: The text says "worms to bugs to unseen microbes... feed on the waste and break it down."

questions.stem: Why do gardeners call the finished soil "black gold"?
choices: It is made of real gold
It is very valuable because it feeds plants so well
It is heavy and dark like metal
It is rare and hard to find
questions.explanation: The text ties "black gold" to soil that "feeds plants so well," so it is prized, like gold.

questions.stem: In this passage, "scraps" most nearly means
questions.explanation: The list -- "apple cores, coffee grounds, old bread, potato peels" -- shows scraps are food leftovers.

lesson.strategy: This explains composting step by step. Read for the change: waste in, rich soil out. Follow the process, gather, break down, wait, and note what does the work. The last line gives the reasons composting is good.

signals.phrase: an older, better path for this waste
signals.means: Sets up the whole idea: waste does not have to end in the trash.

signals.phrase: a slow, natural process called composting
signals.means: Names the process the passage explains.

signals.phrase: feed on the waste and break it down
signals.means: The heart of how it works. Living things turn scraps into soil.

signals.phrase: keeps trash out of the dump, and gives back to the earth
signals.means: The closing reasons composting is worth doing.

vocab.inContext: small leftover pieces of food, like peels and cores
living things too small to see that break down waste
loose and easy to break into small pieces, like good soil

skillTips.main-idea: The steps all serve one change: waste becomes soil. State that change, plus the reasons at the end, for the full main idea.
skillTips.inference: "Black gold" is not real gold. Infer its meaning from the line that this soil "feeds plants so well" -- it is prized, so it is called gold.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "scraps," use the list beside it: "apple cores, coffee grounds, old bread, potato peels." Scraps are small food leftovers.
skillTips.detail: The workers are named: "worms to bugs to unseen microbes," helped by air and water. Match that cause, not the sun or chemicals.

passages.title: Market Morning
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: On Saturday mornings, the town square fills with tables of food. Farmers drive in before dawn to set out what they picked the day before. There are red tomatoes still warm from the field, green beans, brown eggs, and jars of honey that catch the light. Grandmother Ines has come to this market for forty years. She moves slowly from table to table, feeling each fruit, smelling the herbs. She knows which farmer grows the sweetest melons and which one to trust for fish. The sellers know her too, and often slip an extra plum into her bag. Ines could buy the same food, cheaper, at the large store across town. But she comes here for more than food. She comes to see faces she knows, to hear how the crops did this year, and to feel part of the town. When her bag is full, she sits on a bench and watches, in no hurry to leave.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: An old woman who shops at the market for connection, not just cheaper food
How farmers grow the best tomatoes
Why markets open on Saturday mornings
The best food to buy in a small town
questions.explanation: The passage shows Ines choosing the market for the people and the sense of belonging.

questions.stem: According to the text, how long has Ines come to this market?
questions.explanation: The text says "Grandmother Ines has come to this market for forty years."

questions.stem: Why does Ines shop at the market instead of the cheaper store?
choices: The store is closed on Saturdays
She values knowing the sellers and feeling part of the town
The market food is always cheaper
questions.explanation: She comes "for more than food," to see known faces and "feel part of the town."

questions.stem: In this passage, "herbs" most nearly means
choices: leafy plants used to add flavor to food
questions.explanation: Ines is "smelling the herbs" among the food, so herbs are leafy plants used to flavor food.

lesson.strategy: Short fiction about a market. The food scene is rich, but watch Ines. The key line admits she could buy cheaper elsewhere, then explains why she does not. Her reasons are the point.

signals.phrase: come to this market for forty years
signals.means: Shows Ines is a regular. Her long habit hints the market means more than shopping.

signals.phrase: The sellers know her too
signals.means: The bond runs both ways. She and the farmers share a real connection.
