signals.phrase: cheaper, at the large store across town
signals.means: The turn. If the store is cheaper, why come here? The next lines answer.

signals.phrase: for more than food
signals.means: The heart of it. Ines comes for people and belonging, not only groceries.

vocab.inContext: an open public space in the center of a town
leafy plants added to food for their smell and taste
the plants farmers grow and gather for food

skillTips.main-idea: The market's color is background. The main idea is Ines's reason: she comes "for more than food," to feel part of the town.
skillTips.inference: She could shop "cheaper" elsewhere but does not. Infer her reason from what she seeks: known faces, news of the crops, belonging.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "herbs," note she is "smelling the herbs" among fruit and food. Herbs are leafy plants used to flavor food.
skillTips.detail: Small facts are exact: forty years, the extra plum, the food on the tables. Reread to match the precise detail asked.

passages.title: Move at Your Desk
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Sitting still for hours is hard on the body, even if it does not feel that way. When you sit hunched at a desk all day, your muscles grow tight, your back aches, and your blood moves more slowly. The body was built to move, not to hold one shape for eight hours. The good news is that the fix is easy and free. You do not need to leave your chair for long. Every half hour or so, stand up, roll your shoulders, and stretch your arms above your head. Walk to fill a glass of water. Look away from the screen and let your eyes rest. These small breaks keep the blood flowing and loosen tight muscles. They also refresh the mind, so you often work better after them, not worse. The trick is not one long stretch at the end of the day. It is many small moves, spread across it.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Frequent small movements at a desk protect the body and refresh the mind
Desk jobs should be avoided if possible
One long stretch at day's end is best
How to buy a better office chair
questions.explanation: The passage advises many small breaks through the day to help body and mind.

questions.stem: According to the text, how often should you stand and stretch?
choices: Once at the end of the day
questions.explanation: The text says "Every half hour or so, stand up, roll your shoulders, and stretch."

questions.stem: Why does the passage say you often "work better" after small breaks?
choices: The breaks make the day shorter
The breaks refresh the mind as well as the body
The work finishes itself during breaks
questions.explanation: The text says the breaks "refresh the mind," so you work better after them.

questions.stem: In this passage, "hunched" most nearly means
questions.explanation: You "sit hunched at a desk" and your back aches, so hunched means bent forward with rounded shoulders.

lesson.strategy: This gives simple health advice for desk workers. Read for the problem (sitting still hurts the body) and the fix (many small breaks). Note the contrast at the end: many short moves beat one long stretch.

signals.phrase: Sitting still for hours is hard on the body
signals.means: The problem, stated first. The rest of the passage answers it.

signals.phrase: the fix is easy and free
signals.means: Signals the turn to solutions. What follows is the how-to.

signals.phrase: many small moves, spread across it
signals.means: The core advice. Frequent short breaks, not one long one.

signals.phrase: you often work better after them
signals.means: An added reason. Breaks help the mind, not just the body.

vocab.inContext: bent forward with the shoulders rounded, as when leaning over a desk
make less tight, so stiff muscles can move again
make fresh again, giving new energy to the mind

skillTips.main-idea: The point is the pattern: "many small moves, spread across" the day. Tie the problem of sitting to that fix for the main idea.
skillTips.inference: The text says breaks let you "work better." Infer why from "refresh the mind": rest helps thinking, not just muscles.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "hunched," picture the pose: you "sit hunched at a desk" and your back aches. Hunched means bent forward with rounded shoulders.
skillTips.detail: Timing is exact: "Every half hour or so," not once a day. Match the stated interval before choosing a detail answer.

passages.title: The Geese in the V
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text: Each autumn, as the days grow short, great flocks of geese rise into the sky and fly south for the winter. Look up, and you may see them in a clear V shape, one long arm on each side. This shape is not for show. It saves the birds a great deal of energy. As each goose flaps, its wings push the air and leave a small rising current behind. The goose just behind and to the side rides on that current, so it does not have to work as hard. Only the lead goose, at the point of the V, gets no such help. That is why the geese take turns in front. When the leader grows tired, it drops back, and another moves up to take its place. In this way, no single bird carries the whole load, and the flock can fly many hundreds of miles together.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Geese fly in a V and take turns leading to save energy on a long trip
Geese are the fastest of all flying birds
Why winters are so cold in the north
How geese build and hide their nests
questions.explanation: The passage explains that the V shape and turn-taking save the geese energy.

questions.stem: According to the text, which goose gets no help from the current?
choices: The lead goose at the point of the V
Every goose on the left arm
questions.explanation: The text says "only the lead goose, at the point of the V, gets no such help."

questions.stem: Why do the geese take turns flying in front?
choices: The leader always knows the way best
The leader gets no help and tires, so they share the hardest spot
The front is the safest place to fly
The birds cannot see well from behind
questions.explanation: The leader "gets no such help" and tires, so "another moves up to take its place."

questions.stem: In this passage, "flocks" most nearly means
questions.explanation: "Great flocks of geese rise into the sky," so a flock is a large group of birds.

lesson.strategy: This explains a behavior: why geese fly in a V. Read for cause and effect. The shape saves energy, and the turn-taking shares the hardest spot. Follow how each part of the V helps the birds behind it.

signals.phrase: This shape is not for show
signals.means: Signals a real reason is coming. The V has a purpose beyond looks.

signals.phrase: It saves the birds a great deal of energy
signals.means: The main reason for the V, stated plainly.

signals.phrase: gets no such help
signals.means: The exception. The front bird works hardest, which sets up the turn-taking.

signals.phrase: the geese take turns in front
signals.means: The clever fix. No single bird stays in the hard spot for long.

vocab.inContext: large groups of birds moving together
a stream of moving air the birds ride on
the work or weight carried, here the effort of leading

skillTips.main-idea: Two ideas join: the V saves energy, and turns share the hard front. The main idea holds both, not just the shape.
skillTips.inference: The text says the leader "gets no such help." Infer why they take turns: the front is hardest, so they rotate the burden.
skillTips.vocabulary: For "flocks," note what rises into the sky: "great flocks of geese." A flock is a large group of birds.
skillTips.detail: Positions matter. Only "the lead goose, at the point of the V" gets no lift. Match the exact spot before you answer.

passages.title: The Library of Alexandria
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
In the ancient city of Alexandria, in Egypt, stood one of the most famous libraries the world has known. Its keepers had an unusual ambition: to gather a copy of every book in existence. To fill the shelves, the rulers of the city sent agents to buy scrolls in distant markets. According to one story, ships that docked in the harbor had to hand over any books on board so that scribes could copy them; the copy was returned to the owner, and the original was kept.

The library was more than a storehouse. Scholars from many lands came to read, argue, and write there, and the mixing of their ideas made it a center of learning. Work in mathematics, astronomy, and medicine was carried out under its roof.

Over the centuries the library declined, and much of what it held was lost. The exact way it ended is still debated by historians. Yet the idea behind it survived: that knowledge is worth collecting in one place, where anyone who seeks it can find it. Every public library today is, in a small way, an heir to that ambition.

questions.stem: The passage is mainly about
choices: how scrolls were made in ancient Egypt
an ancient library that tried to collect all books and shaped the idea of the public library
the wars fought by Egyptian rulers
how ships were built in the harbor
questions.explanation: The passage describes the library's ambition to collect every book and the lasting idea of a shared place for knowledge.

questions.stem: According to one story, what happened to books found on ships that docked?
choices: They were handed over to be copied, and the original was kept
They were burned on the shore
They were sold at the market
They were taxed by the rulers
questions.explanation: The text says scribes copied the books; the copy was returned to the owner and the original was kept.

questions.stem: The passage suggests the library mattered most because
choices: it had the tallest building in the city
it stood close to the sea
scholars from many lands met and mixed their ideas there
it was free for every citizen
questions.explanation: It says the mixing of scholars' ideas 'made it a center of learning' — the people and ideas, not the building.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'declined' most nearly means
choices: grew weaker and lost what it held
questions.explanation: The clause beside it, 'much of what it held was lost,' shows 'declined' means weakening and decay.

lesson.strategy: A history explainer built around one place and one idea. First pin the ambition: 'to gather a copy of every book in existence'. Then separate what the library did — store books and bring scholars together — from how it ended, which the passage marks as still debated. The last line states the legacy.

signals.phrase: to gather a copy of every book in existence
signals.means: The library's central ambition; the whole passage measures the place against this one goal.

signals.phrase: According to one story
signals.means: A signpost for a claim the author is not vouching for — read what follows as a tale, not settled fact.

signals.phrase: more than a storehouse
signals.means: The turn: the library was not just shelves but a meeting place where scholars mixed ideas.

signals.phrase: Yet the idea behind it survived
signals.means: The 'Yet' pivots from loss to legacy — the point the last lines land on.

vocab.inContext: a strong goal or aim; here, the wish to collect every book that exists
people whose job was to copy books by hand before printing existed
one who inherits something; today's libraries inherit the old library's aim

skillTips.main-idea: Two threads run through this: what the library tried to do and what it left behind. State both: the aim to collect all books and the idea of a shared place for knowledge, not just 'a big library'.
skillTips.inference: The passage never calls it a university, yet scholars 'came to read, argue, and write' and made it 'a center of learning'. Infer its value was people and ideas, not only scrolls.
skillTips.vocabulary: For 'declined', use the clause beside it: 'much of what it held was lost'. The loss tells you the word means weakening, not a polite refusal.
skillTips.detail: Details here split into story and fact. The ship-and-copy account opens with 'According to one story', so tie it to that frame rather than treating it as certain.

passages.title: How Railways Set the Clocks
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
For most of history, each town kept its own time. Noon was simply the moment the Sun stood highest overhead, so a clock in one city might read a few minutes different from a clock a short distance away. When people traveled by foot or horse, these small gaps caused no trouble.

The railway changed that. Trains ran on printed timetables, and a timetable makes no sense if every station measures the hour differently. A train might seem to arrive before it left, simply because the two clocks disagreed. Confusion of this kind could also be dangerous, since two trains sharing one track had to know exactly when each would pass.

To solve the problem, railway companies agreed to keep a single, shared time along their lines, ignoring the small differences from town to town. In time, whole nations adopted these standard hours, and the globe was divided into broad zones, each keeping one clock. What began as a convenience for trains became the way the modern world tells time. The Sun no longer sets the hour; a shared agreement does.

questions.stem: The passage mainly explains
choices: how the Sun moves across the sky
how to read a train timetable
how railways led towns to keep a single, shared time
questions.explanation: The passage traces how the railway's need for one time led to standard hours and zones.

questions.stem: Before railways, how did a town decide when it was noon?
choices: By agreement with the capital city
The moment the Sun stood highest overhead
questions.explanation: The text says noon was 'the moment the Sun stood highest overhead'.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that standard time was adopted mainly because it was
choices: more accurate to the Sun than local time
practical for running trains safely and on schedule
ordered by scientists studying the stars
questions.explanation: It calls standard time 'a convenience for trains' and stresses safety on shared tracks, not solar accuracy.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'adopted' most nearly means
choices: took up and began to use
questions.explanation: Nations 'adopted these standard hours' means they took them up and began using them.

lesson.strategy: A history explainer that traces one change from cause to result. Hold two states in mind: before the railway, 'each town kept its own time'; after, a single shared time. The turn is 'The railway changed that'. The last two lines state what the change finally meant.

signals.phrase: each town kept its own time
signals.means: The starting state; everything after measures how far the world moved from it.

signals.phrase: The railway changed that
signals.means: The turn that drives the whole passage — the cause of the shift to shared time.

signals.phrase: To solve the problem
signals.means: Marks the fix: railway companies agreeing on one time along their lines.

signals.phrase: a convenience for trains
signals.means: Names the true reason for standard time — practicality, not matching the Sun.

vocab.inContext: a printed schedule of when trains leave and arrive, which needs one shared time to work
took up and began to use; nations adopted the railways' shared hours
broad bands of the globe that each keep a single clock

skillTips.main-idea: The passage follows one arc: local sun-time, then a problem, then shared time. Say what caused the change (railways) and what it produced (standard zones), not just that clocks exist.
skillTips.inference: The close says 'The Sun no longer sets the hour; a shared agreement does'. Infer the trade the world accepted: less solar accuracy for clocks that agree.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Adopted' takes 'these standard hours' as its object, after 'in time'. The sense of taking something up and using it comes from that frame, not a guess.
skillTips.detail: Before-and-after passages hide details in the 'before' half. For how noon was set, go back to the Sun that 'stood highest overhead', not the railway part.

passages.title: The Coffeehouse and the Exchange of Ideas
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
When coffee first spread through the cities of Europe, it arrived with a new kind of public place: the coffeehouse. Unlike a tavern, where drink dulled the mind, a coffeehouse served a bitter drink that kept people alert and talking. For the price of a cup, almost anyone could sit for hours, read the news, and join the conversation.

These rooms became informal centers of exchange. Merchants met to strike deals, and some famous businesses grew out of trades first arranged over coffee. Writers and thinkers argued about science and politics, testing their ideas against sharp replies from strangers. Because the cost of entry was so low, people who would never meet elsewhere sat at the same table. Some called the coffeehouses 'penny universities', since a penny bought both the drink and an education in the talk around you.

Not everyone approved. Rulers sometimes feared these rooms, worried that too much free talk could stir up trouble. Yet the coffeehouse had shown something lasting: that a cheap, open space where strangers mix can move ideas as powerfully as any book.

questions.stem: The passage is mainly about
choices: how coffee is grown and roasted
the founding of the first universities
how coffeehouses became places to exchange ideas and business
questions.explanation: The passage describes coffeehouses as informal centers where deals, arguments, and ideas were exchanged.

questions.stem: Why were the coffeehouses called 'penny universities'?
choices: Because a cheap cup came with an education in the talk around you
Because only students were allowed inside
Because they charged a penny to enter and nothing more
questions.explanation: The text says a penny bought both the drink and 'an education in the talk around you'.

questions.stem: The passage suggests coffeehouses spread ideas so well mainly because
choices: the low price mixed people who would never otherwise meet
rulers encouraged people to gather there
they were quiet places to read
questions.explanation: It says the low cost of entry brought together 'people who would never meet elsewhere' — the mixing spread ideas.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'informal' most nearly means
questions.explanation: The rooms organized nothing official yet still moved ideas, so 'informal' means casual and unofficial.

lesson.strategy: A history explainer that argues one claim: the coffeehouse mattered as a place where ideas moved. Read the contrast 'Unlike a tavern' first, then collect what happened in these rooms — deals, arguments, mixing. The 'Not everyone approved' turn adds the opposing view before the closing point.

signals.phrase: Unlike a tavern
signals.means: Opens with a contrast that defines the coffeehouse by what it is not — alert talk, not dulled drinking.

signals.phrase: informal centers of exchange
signals.means: The author's label for what these rooms became; the sentences after give the examples.

signals.phrase: penny universities
signals.means: A nickname carrying the main point — cheap entry bought both a drink and an education.

signals.phrase: Not everyone approved
signals.means: The turn to the opposing view: rulers feared the free talk these rooms allowed.

vocab.inContext: a place that serves alcohol, set here against the alert, talkative coffeehouse
casual and unofficial; the coffeehouse organized nothing yet still moved ideas
traders and businesspeople who met over coffee to arrange deals

skillTips.main-idea: The nickname 'penny universities' packs the point: a cheap open room that taught through talk. Anchor the main idea to that image, not to coffee itself.
skillTips.inference: Link 'the cost of entry was so low' to 'people who would never meet elsewhere sat at the same table'. Infer the cheap mixing of strangers is what spread ideas.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Informal' pairs with 'centers of exchange'. Since the rooms organized nothing official yet still worked, read it as casual and unofficial, not disorganized in a bad sense.
skillTips.detail: Watch the contrast structure. 'Unlike a tavern' makes the tavern the foil; details about alertness and talk belong to the coffeehouse, not the tavern.

passages.title: Cracking the Rosetta Stone
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
For centuries, the picture-writing of ancient Egypt was a locked door. The symbols, called hieroglyphs, covered temple walls and tombs, but no one alive could read them. Their meaning had been forgotten for more than a thousand years.

The key turned up by accident. Soldiers digging near the Egyptian town of Rosetta uncovered a slab of dark stone carved with three blocks of writing. Scholars soon realized that all three said the same thing, but in three different scripts. One block was written in Greek, a language scholars still understood. If the three texts matched, then the Greek could serve as a translation of the other two.

Even so, the puzzle took years. Comparing the scripts letter by letter, scholars slowly worked out which symbols stood for sounds and which stood for ideas. A young French scholar finally pieced the system together, and the locked door swung open. Suddenly the writing on thousands of monuments could speak again, and a lost world of kings, gods, and daily life came back into view. The stone itself was ordinary; its power lay in the match between its three texts.

questions.stem: The passage mainly explains
choices: how the French army fought in Egypt
how the Greek language spread across the world
how a stone with matching texts let scholars finally read hieroglyphs
how ancient Egyptians built their tombs
questions.explanation: The passage tells how three matching texts, one in readable Greek, allowed hieroglyphs to be decoded.

questions.stem: What made the stone useful for decoding hieroglyphs?
choices: All three blocks said the same thing, one in readable Greek
It was larger than any other stone
It showed pictures of Egyptian kings
questions.explanation: The text says all three said the same thing, and one block was in Greek that scholars could read.

questions.stem: The passage calls the stone 'ordinary' to make the point that
choices: it was cheaply and quickly made
its worth came from the matching writing, not the rock itself
it was small and easy to carry
many stones just like it still exist
questions.explanation: It says 'its power lay in the match between its three texts' — the method, not the material, mattered.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'scripts' most nearly means
questions.explanation: Set against 'a language' in the next sentence, 'scripts' means systems of writing.

lesson.strategy: A history explainer told as a puzzle solved. Track the problem first: hieroglyphs were 'a locked door' no one could read. Then find the key — three matching texts, one in readable Greek. The payoff comes when 'the locked door swung open'. The last line states why the plain stone mattered.

signals.phrase: a locked door
signals.means: The image for the problem: writing that could be seen but not read. The passage returns to this door at the end.

signals.phrase: The key turned up by accident
signals.means: Marks the discovery and warns you the find was luck, not a planned search.

signals.phrase: all three said the same thing
signals.means: The heart of the method — matching texts let a known language unlock two unknown ones.

signals.phrase: the locked door swung open
signals.means: The payoff, echoing the opening image: the code is broken and the writing can be read.

vocab.inContext: the picture-symbols of ancient Egyptian writing that had become unreadable
systems of writing; the stone carried three of them, not three languages alone
the temples, tombs, and carved stones whose writing could now be read

skillTips.main-idea: The passage frames a locked door that finally opens. State the main idea as the solving of a problem — matching texts made hieroglyphs readable — not a story about soldiers or France.
skillTips.inference: The close calls the stone 'ordinary' yet powerful. Infer the passage values the method — a known text unlocking unknown ones — over the object itself.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Scripts' is set against 'a language' in the next sentence. Use that contrast: scripts are ways of writing, so three scripts can record one message in different symbol systems.
skillTips.detail: The decoding rests on one fact: 'all three said the same thing'. When a question asks how the stone helped, return to that matching, not its size or material.

passages.title: Why the Sky Is Blue
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
Sunlight looks white, but it is really a blend of many colors mixed together. When that light passes through the air, it does not travel untouched. Tiny molecules of gas bump the light and send it bouncing off in new directions, a process called scattering.

Not every color scatters the same amount. The blue and violet parts of sunlight travel in short, tight waves, and these short waves scatter far more strongly than the long, slow waves of red and orange. So as sunlight crosses the sky, blue light is thrown in every direction, filling the whole dome above you. When you look up, that scattered blue is the light reaching your eyes, and the sky appears blue.

The same rule explains the red of a sunset. Near the horizon, light must pass through much more air before it reaches you. Along that longer path, most of the blue has already been scattered away, leaving mainly the reds and oranges to come through. One simple fact about wave length, then, paints both the blue of noon and the red of evening.

questions.stem: The passage mainly explains
choices: how the Sun produces its heat
how the human eye sees color
what gases make up the air
why the sky looks blue and sunsets look red
questions.explanation: The whole passage uses scattering to explain both the blue sky and the red sunset.

questions.stem: According to the passage, which colors scatter most strongly?
questions.explanation: The text says the short waves of blue and violet 'scatter far more strongly' than red and orange.

questions.stem: Based on the passage, if the air held no gas molecules, the daytime sky would most likely
choices: look dark, since nothing would scatter the sunlight
questions.explanation: The blue comes from molecules scattering light; with no scatterer, no blue would fill the sky, leaving it dark.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'scattering' most nearly means
choices: light bouncing off in new directions
questions.explanation: The text defines it in place: gas molecules 'send it bouncing off in new directions, a process called scattering'.

lesson.strategy: A science explainer built on one cause: air scatters short blue waves more than long red ones. Get the mechanism first — 'a process called scattering' — then apply it twice: it makes the noon sky blue and, along a longer path, the sunset red. The last line says one fact explains both.

signals.phrase: a blend of many colors
signals.means: The starting fact: white sunlight already contains every color, so the sky's color is about which ones reach you.

signals.phrase: a process called scattering
signals.means: Names the mechanism the whole passage runs on — light bounced in new directions by gas.

signals.phrase: Not every color scatters the same amount
signals.means: The key difference that decides the color: short blue waves scatter more than long red ones.

signals.phrase: The same rule explains the red of a sunset
signals.means: Signals reuse: the same mechanism, over a longer path, now produces red instead of blue.

vocab.inContext: tiny particles of gas in the air that bump light and send it scattering
light bouncing off in new directions after striking gas particles
the line where sky meets ground; light near it crosses much more air

skillTips.main-idea: One mechanism drives everything: short waves scatter more. State the main idea as that cause, and note it explains two effects — blue noon and red sunset — not just one.
skillTips.inference: The blue depends on molecules doing the scattering. Remove the scatterer and the effect must vanish, though the passage never says so directly — that chain is the inference.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Scattering' is defined in place: 'send it bouncing off in new directions'. Let the author's own clause be your definition, not the everyday word.
skillTips.detail: The color facts are exact: short blue and violet scatter most; long red and orange least. Match a question to the specific wave length it names, not a general sense of bright colors.

passages.title: Why Ice Floats
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
Almost every substance grows denser as it cools and turns solid. Its particles pack more tightly, so the solid form is heavier than the liquid and sinks in it. Water breaks this rule, and the exception matters more than it might seem.

As water cools toward freezing, its molecules begin to line up into a rigid, open pattern. This pattern holds the molecules slightly farther apart than they sit in liquid water. Because the same molecules now take up more room, ice is less dense than the water around it, so it floats instead of sinking.

The result shapes life on Earth. When a lake grows cold, ice forms on the surface and stays there, spreading a solid lid across the top. That lid slows the loss of heat and shelters the water below, which stays liquid through the winter. Fish and other creatures survive in that hidden pool. If ice sank, lakes would freeze from the bottom up, turning solid and killing much of what lived in them. A small quirk in how water freezes helps keep whole worlds alive.

questions.stem: The passage mainly explains
choices: why ice floats and why that matters for life
questions.explanation: The passage explains the cause of ice floating and then why that keeps lake life alive.

questions.stem: Why is ice less dense than liquid water?
choices: Its molecules form an open pattern that spreads them farther apart
It traps tiny bubbles of air
It is made of different molecules
It is simply colder than the water
questions.explanation: The text says the molecules line up in 'a rigid, open pattern' that holds them farther apart, so ice takes more room.

questions.stem: Based on the passage, a substance that behaves like most others would, when frozen in a lake,
choices: sink as it turns solid, building up from the bottom
questions.explanation: Most substances grow denser and sink as solids, so a 'normal' one would sink and freeze from the bottom up.

questions.stem: In this passage, something that is 'less dense' than water will
questions.explanation: The text says 'ice is less dense than the water around it, so it floats' — less dense means it rides on top.

lesson.strategy: A science explainer built on one exception. First fix the normal rule — solids grow denser and sink. Then mark the break, 'Water breaks this rule', and follow the cause: an open molecular pattern makes ice lighter. The last paragraph shows why this small quirk keeps life alive.

signals.phrase: Water breaks this rule
signals.means: The turn from the general rule to the exception; everything after explains and uses this break.

signals.phrase: a rigid, open pattern
signals.means: The cause of floating: molecules held farther apart, so ice takes more room and weighs less per space.

signals.phrase: The result shapes life on Earth
signals.means: Shifts from the physics to its payoff — why the exception matters beyond the lab.

signals.phrase: A small quirk
signals.means: The closing frame: a tiny oddity in freezing has large effects, tying cause to consequence.

vocab.inContext: packed more tightly into the same space, so heavier for its size and likely to sink
stiff and fixed; the frozen molecules lock into a set open pattern
an odd, unusual trait; here, water's strange habit of expanding as it freezes

skillTips.main-idea: The passage pairs a cause (an open pattern makes ice float) with a consequence (life survives under lake ice). State both halves; the point is not floating alone but why it matters.
skillTips.inference: Combine the general rule — most solids sink — with the lake example. Infer a 'normal' substance would build up from the bottom, since the passage says only water avoids this.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Dense' is tied to floating and sinking here. Use 'ice is less dense... so it floats' to fix the word: less dense means lighter for its size, so it rides on top.
skillTips.detail: The mechanism is exact: an 'open pattern' holds molecules 'farther apart'. For a how question, cite that spacing, not vague ideas like trapped air or lower temperature.

passages.title: The Reflex Behind Goosebumps
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
When you feel a sudden chill or a wave of strong emotion, small bumps may rise on your skin. Each bump appears where a tiny muscle at the base of a hair tightens and pulls the hair upright. We call the effect goosebumps, though the muscles do the real work.

In furry animals, this reflex is useful. Raised fur traps a layer of air close to the skin, which helps hold in warmth on a cold day. A frightened animal with its fur standing on end also looks larger and more dangerous to a rival. The same reflex that warms can also serve as a threat.

Humans kept the reflex but lost most of the fur. Our thin body hair cannot trap much heat or make us look bigger, so the bumps do little for us now. Scientists call such a leftover trait vestigial: a feature that once had a clear use and remains after that use has faded. Goosebumps are a small reminder that our bodies still carry machinery built for an older, hairier way of life.

questions.stem: The passage mainly explains
choices: what causes goosebumps and why they are a leftover reflex
why humans feel cold more than animals
questions.explanation: The passage explains the muscle that raises goosebumps and why the reflex is now a leftover in humans.

questions.stem: What actually makes a goosebump rise?
choices: A tiny muscle tightens and pulls a hair upright
questions.explanation: The text says a tiny muscle at the base of a hair 'tightens and pulls the hair upright'.

questions.stem: Why do goosebumps do little for humans today?
choices: We no longer have any rivals
We lost most of our fur, so raised hairs cannot trap heat or add size
We feel cold far less than animals
Our muscles have grown too weak
questions.explanation: The reflex remains, but without fur the raised hairs 'cannot trap much heat or make us look bigger'.

questions.stem: In this passage, a 'vestigial' feature is one that
choices: is brand new in the body
once had a use and remains after that use has faded
questions.explanation: The text defines vestigial as 'a feature that once had a clear use and remains after that use has faded'.

lesson.strategy: A science explainer that moves from mechanism to meaning. First get what happens — a tiny muscle lifts a hair. Then compare two cases: the reflex helps furry animals but does little for humans. The key term 'vestigial' is defined in place; the close says what the leftover reflex reveals.

signals.phrase: a tiny muscle at the base of a hair
signals.means: The mechanism: the real cause of a goosebump is a muscle, not the skin itself.

signals.phrase: this reflex is useful
signals.means: Opens the animal case — where raised fur earns its keep through warmth and size.

signals.phrase: Humans kept the reflex but lost most of the fur
signals.means: The turn to us: the same action remains, but without fur it no longer pays off.

signals.phrase: Scientists call such a leftover trait vestigial
signals.means: Names and defines the key idea — a feature left over after its use has faded.

vocab.inContext: an automatic body response you do not choose, like hairs rising in the cold
a leftover feature that once had a clear use and stays after that use fades
a competitor an animal might face; puffed-up fur can look larger to one

skillTips.main-idea: The passage explains a cause and then judges its worth in humans. State both: a muscle lifts the hair, and in us the reflex is a leftover, a 'vestigial' trait.
skillTips.inference: Chain 'kept the reflex' with 'lost most of the fur'. Infer the action still fires but has nothing to work on, which is why it barely helps us now.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Vestigial' is defined right where it appears: 'once had a clear use and remains after that use has faded'. Take the meaning from that clause, not the hard word alone.
skillTips.detail: The animal benefits are specific: trapped warmth and looking 'larger and more dangerous'. Tie a detail question to which benefit it names, not a general 'it helps animals'.

passages.title: How a Vaccine Trains the Body
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
The body defends itself with an immune system that learns from experience. When a germ invades, the immune system slowly works out how to fight it and, if all goes well, wins. Just as important, it keeps a memory of that germ, so a second attack by the same invader is beaten much faster.

A vaccine uses this memory without the danger of a real illness. It shows the immune system a harmless version of a germ, or just a piece of one, that cannot make you sick. The body treats this preview as a threat and builds its defenses against it, forming memory cells tuned to that exact germ. No true infection ever takes place.

Later, if the real germ arrives, the body is no longer meeting a stranger. It recognizes the invader at once and attacks before the illness can take hold. In this way a vaccine trades a mild, controlled lesson now for strong protection later. The immune system does the actual work; the vaccine simply teaches it what to expect.

questions.stem: The passage mainly explains
choices: how the body heals a cut
how a vaccine teaches the immune system to fight a germ safely
how germs spread from person to person
why some illnesses last longer than others
questions.explanation: The passage is about how a vaccine uses the immune system's memory to prepare it without a real illness.

questions.stem: What does a vaccine show the immune system?
choices: A harmless version of a germ, or just a piece of one
questions.explanation: The text says a vaccine shows 'a harmless version of a germ, or just a piece of one'.

questions.stem: Why is a second attack by the same germ beaten much faster?
choices: The germ grows weaker on its own
The vaccine stays in the blood forever
Germs avoid a body they have entered before
The body kept a memory of it and recognizes it at once
questions.explanation: The body 'keeps a memory of that germ' and 'recognizes the invader at once', so the second fight is faster.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'preview' most nearly means
choices: an early, safe look before the real thing
questions.explanation: The 'preview' is the harmless sample the body sees before 'the real germ arrives' later.

lesson.strategy: A science explainer built on one idea: the immune system learns and remembers. First get the natural process — fight, then remember. Then see how a vaccine borrows it safely, using 'a harmless version'. The close states the trade: a mild lesson now for protection later.

signals.phrase: learns from experience
signals.means: The foundation: the immune system improves by meeting germs, which sets up everything a vaccine does.

signals.phrase: keeps a memory of that germ
signals.means: The key ability — memory — that makes a second fight faster and that a vaccine exploits.

signals.phrase: A vaccine uses this memory
signals.means: The turn from natural defense to the vaccine, which triggers memory without a real illness.

signals.phrase: no longer meeting a stranger
signals.means: The payoff image: on the real attack the body already knows the germ and reacts fast.

vocab.inContext: enters the body to cause harm; the immune system responds when a germ invades
defenders the body keeps that remember one germ and speed up the next fight
an early, safe look at a germ that lets the body prepare before the real one

skillTips.main-idea: The passage is about training, not curing. State the main idea as teaching the immune system to remember a germ safely, so it wins faster if the real one comes.
skillTips.inference: Link 'keeps a memory' to 'recognizes the invader at once'. Infer that speed on the second attack comes from stored memory, not any weakening of the germ.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Preview' names the harmless sample the body sees first. Read it against 'the real germ arrives' later — a preview is the early, safe version before the true event.
skillTips.detail: The vaccine's content is specific: 'a harmless version of a germ, or just a piece of one'. For a detail question, quote that, not a live or full-strength germ.

passages.title: The Hidden Network Under a Forest
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
A forest looks like a crowd of separate trees, each standing alone. Beneath the soil, however, many of those trees are joined. Threads of fungus, far thinner than roots, wrap around and reach into the roots of the trees, linking one plant to another underground.

This partnership helps both sides. The fungus cannot make its own food, so it draws sugar from the tree, which produces sugar in its leaves using sunlight. In return, the fungal threads spread through the soil far better than roots can, gathering water and minerals and passing them to the tree. Each partner gives what the other lacks.

The threads also link tree to tree. Through this shared web, a large tree in good health can pass some of its extra sugar to a smaller, shaded neighbor that receives little light. Warning signals about insects or drought may travel the same paths. Scientists sometimes call this underground system the 'wood wide web', because, like a hidden network of wires, it quietly carries goods and messages among trees that appear to stand alone.

questions.stem: The passage is mostly about
choices: how fungal threads connect forest trees and let them share resources
how trees grow tall toward the light
questions.explanation: The passage describes the underground fungal network that links trees and lets them trade and share.

questions.stem: What does the fungus give the tree in their partnership?
choices: Water and minerals gathered from the soil
questions.explanation: The tree gives sugar; the fungus gathers 'water and minerals' from the soil and passes them to the tree.

questions.stem: Why can a healthy tree help a shaded neighbor?
choices: They slowly grow into one tree
They share the very same roots
The shared fungal web links them, so extra sugar can pass along it
questions.explanation: The 'shared web' links tree to tree, so a healthy tree can pass extra sugar to a shaded one.

questions.stem: In this passage, calling the system a 'network' suggests it is
choices: a web of many connections that carries things between trees
questions.explanation: The text compares it to 'a hidden network of wires' that 'carries goods and messages' — many links between points.

lesson.strategy: A nature explainer that overturns a first impression. It opens with trees that 'look like a crowd of separate trees', then shows them joined by fungus. Track the two-way trade first (sugar for minerals), then the tree-to-tree sharing. The 'wood wide web' name states the main idea.

signals.phrase: many of those trees are joined
signals.means: Corrects the opening image of separate trees; the whole passage builds on this hidden link.

signals.phrase: This partnership helps both sides
signals.means: Signals the exchange to come — what the tree gives and what the fungus gives back.

signals.phrase: Each partner gives what the other lacks
signals.means: Sums up the trade in one line: sugar from the tree, water and minerals from the fungus.

signals.phrase: wood wide web
signals.means: The author's name for the system, packing the main idea — a network that carries goods and messages.

vocab.inContext: a living thing, not a plant, whose thin threads link tree roots and cannot make its own food
nutrients in the soil that the fungal threads gather and pass to the tree
a web of many connections; here, threads linking many trees like hidden wires

skillTips.main-idea: The passage moves from 'separate trees' to a joined network. State the main idea as the hidden connection and sharing, not how a single tree lives.
skillTips.inference: Link the 'shared web' to the healthy tree passing sugar to a shaded one. Infer the connection, not chance, is what lets trees help each other.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Network' is unpacked by 'like a hidden network of wires... carries goods and messages'. Use that comparison: a network means many links moving things between points.
skillTips.detail: The trade has two exact halves: the tree gives sugar, the fungus gives 'water and minerals'. For a give-and-take question, name the correct direction rather than mixing them.

passages.title: The Long Flight of the Monarch
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
Each autumn, monarch butterflies leave the cooling fields of the north and travel enormous distances to spend the winter in warmer forests far to the south. For an insect that weighs less than a paperclip, the journey is astonishing. Yet the strangest part is not the distance but who makes it.

No single butterfly flies the whole round trip. The monarchs that travel south in the fall are not the same ones that return. On the way back north in spring, the butterflies breed, lay eggs, and die, and it is their children and grandchildren that continue the trip. Several generations may pass before the family reaches the northern fields again.

This raises a hard question. If the butterflies heading south have never seen the winter forest, how do they know where to go? They were not taught the route, for their parents died long before. Somehow the direction is written into them from birth, carried in their bodies rather than learned. How a tiny brain stores such a map remains one of nature's open puzzles.

questions.stem: The passage is mainly about
choices: why forests grow in the south
the puzzle of a long migration that no single butterfly completes
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on the mystery of how monarchs make a multi-generation migration they were never taught.

questions.stem: Who completes the trip back north?
choices: The same butterflies that flew south
The children and grandchildren of those that flew south
questions.explanation: The text says the butterflies that go south are not the ones that return; 'their children and grandchildren' continue.

questions.stem: Why is the monarchs' sense of direction described as a puzzle?
choices: They always travel in large groups
They were never taught the route, yet still find their way
questions.explanation: Their parents die before them, so they cannot be taught; yet they find the route, which is why it puzzles scientists.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'generations' refers to
choices: the successive parents, children, and grandchildren in the family
questions.explanation: The word follows 'their children and grandchildren', marking steps in a family line.

lesson.strategy: A nature explainer that sets up a mystery. Notice the redirection early: 'the strangest part is not the distance but who makes it'. Follow the generations — no butterfly does the round trip. That leads to the puzzle at 'This raises a hard question': how an untaught route is known.

signals.phrase: the strangest part is not the distance
signals.means: Redirects your attention from the obvious (how far) to the real subject (who and how).

signals.phrase: No single butterfly flies the whole round trip
signals.means: The core fact: the journey is completed across several generations, not by one insect.

signals.phrase: This raises a hard question
signals.means: Marks the pivot to the puzzle — how can a butterfly know a route it never learned?

signals.phrase: written into them from birth
signals.means: The proposed answer: the direction is inborn, carried in the body rather than taught.

vocab.inContext: the orange-and-black butterfly whose long yearly migration the passage describes
the successive parents, children, and grandchildren that together complete the trip
the path of the migration; the butterflies find it without being taught

skillTips.main-idea: The author twice steers you past distance toward the mystery of the route. State the main idea as that puzzle — an inborn map — not just 'butterflies migrate far'.
skillTips.inference: Combine 'never seen the winter forest' with 'their parents died long before'. Infer the route cannot be learned, which is exactly why the author calls it a puzzle.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Generations' is clarified by 'their children and grandchildren'. Use that phrase to read the word as steps in a family line, not distance or seasons.
skillTips.detail: The passage is precise about who returns: 'their children and grandchildren', not the original travelers. For a who question, hold that difference firmly.

passages.title: Seeing With Sound
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
Many bats hunt in complete darkness, chasing insects through the night air without ever seeing them. They manage this by using sound the way we use light. As a bat flies, it sends out a stream of high, sharp calls, most of them too high for human ears to hear.

When a call strikes an object, part of the sound bounces back as an echo. The bat listens for these returning echoes and reads them closely. A nearer object sends its echo back sooner, so the delay tells the bat how far away something is. Changes in the echo hint at the object's size and even the direction it is moving. From a rapid stream of echoes, the bat builds a picture of the space around it.

This method is called echolocation: finding the location of things by their echoes. It is so precise that a bat can catch a single insect in midair or weave through branches it cannot see. Where our eyes fail in the dark, the bat has learned to let sound do the seeing.

questions.stem: The passage mainly explains
choices: why bats sleep during the day
how bats use sound and echoes to find things in the dark
questions.explanation: The passage explains echolocation: how bats read echoes to sense their surroundings in the dark.

questions.stem: What does the delay of an echo tell the bat?
choices: How far away the object is
questions.explanation: The text says a nearer object echoes back sooner, so 'the delay tells the bat how far away something is'.

questions.stem: Why can bats hunt where our eyes fail?
choices: They hunt very slowly and carefully
They use echoes, which do not need light
The insects they chase give off light
questions.explanation: Bats sense by sound bouncing back, so they work 'in complete darkness' where sight, needing light, fails.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'echolocation' means
choices: finding where things are by their echoes
questions.explanation: The text defines it: 'finding the location of things by their echoes'.

lesson.strategy: A nature explainer built on one analogy: 'using sound the way we use light'. Hold that comparison, then follow the steps — call sent, echo returns, delay read for distance. The key term 'echolocation' is defined in place; the close shows how precise the method is.

signals.phrase: using sound the way we use light
signals.means: The guiding analogy; every later step maps a part of vision onto hearing.

signals.phrase: bounces back as an echo
signals.means: The core mechanism — sound reflecting off objects is what the bat reads.

signals.phrase: the delay tells the bat how far
signals.means: Explains how timing becomes distance: a sooner echo means a nearer object.

signals.phrase: This method is called echolocation
signals.means: Names and defines the whole process the passage has been describing.

vocab.inContext: sound that bounces back after hitting an object, which the bat listens for
finding the location of things by reading their echoes, as a bat does
exact and accurate; precise enough to catch one insect in the air

skillTips.main-idea: The passage explains one skill through an analogy to sight. State the main idea as bats using echoes to sense their surroundings, the point the analogy serves.
skillTips.inference: Link 'too high for human ears' and echoes 'in complete darkness' to why bats beat our eyes. Infer that sound, unlike sight, needs no light, so it works at night.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Echolocation' is defined right after it appears: 'finding the location of things by their echoes'. Split the word into echo plus location and let the clause confirm it.
skillTips.detail: The cues are specific: 'delay' gives distance, changes in the echo give size and direction. Match a detail question to which cue carries which fact.

passages.title: The Bystander Effect
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
You might expect that a person in trouble is safest in a large crowd, where many people could help. Studies of human behavior suggest the opposite can be true. The more people who witness an emergency, the less likely any single one is to step in. Psychologists call this the bystander effect.

The cause is not that crowds are cruel. When you are the only witness, the responsibility to act rests fully on you. But in a group, that responsibility feels spread out, or diffused, among everyone present. Each person assumes someone else will act, or waits to see how others react. If no one moves, each takes the stillness as a sign that help is not needed. So the very size of the crowd, which seems like safety, can freeze it into doing nothing.

Knowing this pattern is useful. A person who understands the bystander effect is better able to resist it, either by acting despite the crowd or, if they need help, by pointing to one person and asking directly. A single clear request breaks the spell, turning a faceless crowd back into an individual with a job to do.

questions.stem: The passage mainly explains
choices: why people in a crowd may be less likely to help, and how to counter it
why some people are naturally cruel
questions.explanation: The passage defines the bystander effect, explains its cause, and gives a way to counter it.

questions.stem: According to the passage, what happens to a sense of responsibility in a group?
choices: It feels spread out among everyone present
It grows stronger for each person
questions.explanation: The text says responsibility 'feels spread out, or diffused, among everyone present'.

questions.stem: Why does pointing to one person and asking directly help?
choices: It makes the crowd grow larger
It hides the emergency from others
It gives that one person clear responsibility to act
questions.explanation: A direct request turns 'a faceless crowd back into an individual with a job to do', ending the shared, diffused responsibility.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'diffused' most nearly means
questions.explanation: The text pairs it with 'spread out', so 'diffused' means thinned across many people.

lesson.strategy: A psychology explainer that overturns a guess. It states the surprise — 'the opposite can be true' — then explains the cause: responsibility gets diffused in a group. Watch it move from what happens to why, then to a fix. The last lines give the practical move.

signals.phrase: the opposite can be true
signals.means: Flags the counterintuitive claim: a crowd can make help less likely, not more.

signals.phrase: spread out, or diffused
signals.means: Names the mechanism — shared responsibility that feels lighter for each person, so no one acts.

signals.phrase: Knowing this pattern is useful
signals.means: The turn from explaining the effect to using that knowledge against it.

signals.phrase: breaks the spell
signals.means: The fix in an image: a direct request to one person ends the group freeze.

vocab.inContext: a person present at an event but not acting; here, a witness to an emergency
spread out among many, so each person feels less responsible to act
a sudden situation needing quick help, which the crowd may fail to answer

skillTips.main-idea: The passage names a surprising effect, explains its cause, then offers a fix. State all three, or at least the effect plus its cause, not just 'crowds are bad at helping'.
skillTips.inference: Link 'responsibility... diffused' to 'pointing to one person'. Infer that naming one person undoes the diffusion by handing that person the responsibility back.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Diffused' comes right after 'spread out, or', so the author defines it for you. Read the pair as one idea: responsibility thinned across a group.
skillTips.detail: The cause has parts: each person 'assumes someone else will act' and reads stillness as 'a sign that help is not needed'. Cite the exact step, not 'people are lazy'.

passages.title: The Trap of Sunk Cost
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
Imagine you have paid for a ticket to a film. Halfway through, you realize the film is dull and you would rather leave. Many people stay to the end anyway, telling themselves they should not 'waste' the money already spent. This is a small example of a wider trap.

The money for the ticket is gone whether you stay or go. Economists call it a sunk cost: a cost already paid that cannot be recovered, no matter what you choose next. A wise choice should weigh only what lies ahead, here more dull minutes against a free evening. The past payment should count for nothing, because nothing you do now can bring it back.

Yet people find this hard. Having put money, time, or effort into something, they feel bound to continue, as if quitting would insult the investment. This is the sunk cost fallacy, and it keeps people in bad films, failing projects, and even unhappy plans long after the sensible move is to stop. The clearest question to ask is not 'How much have I already spent?' but 'What is the best choice from here?'

questions.stem: The passage mainly explains
choices: how to save money when buying tickets
why past spending should not drive future choices
questions.explanation: The passage explains the sunk cost fallacy: unrecoverable past spending should not decide what you do next.

questions.stem: What is a 'sunk cost'?
choices: The price of a film ticket only
A cost you will have to pay later
A cost already paid that cannot be recovered
questions.explanation: The text defines a sunk cost as 'a cost already paid that cannot be recovered'.

questions.stem: The passage says the best question is 'What is the best choice from here?' because
choices: films are always a waste of time
the past payment cannot be changed, so only what lies ahead matters
questions.explanation: Since the sunk cost cannot be recovered, only future outcomes can still change, so a wise choice weighs 'what lies ahead'.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'fallacy' most nearly means
questions.explanation: The 'sunk cost fallacy' is treated as an error, so 'fallacy' means a mistaken way of reasoning.

lesson.strategy: A psychology and economics explainer that starts with a familiar scene, then names the idea behind it. Get the definition — a 'sunk cost' cannot be recovered — then watch the 'Yet' turn to why people ignore it. The last line hands you the better question to ask.

signals.phrase: a wider trap
signals.means: Signals the film scene is just an example; the real subject is a general pattern of thinking.

signals.phrase: a cost already paid that cannot be recovered
signals.means: The exact definition of a sunk cost — the fact the whole argument rests on.

signals.phrase: Yet people find this hard
signals.means: The turn from the logical rule to the human feeling that fights it.

signals.phrase: the sunk cost fallacy
signals.means: Names the error: staying with something only because of what was already spent.

vocab.inContext: gotten back; a sunk cost is money or effort that cannot be recovered
a mistaken way of reasoning; here, letting past spending decide future choices
money, time, or effort already put in, which people feel bound to protect

skillTips.main-idea: The film is an example, not the point. State the main idea as the general fallacy — that past, unrecoverable costs should not steer future choices.
skillTips.inference: Link 'cannot be recovered' to 'What is the best choice from here?'. Infer the reason: since the past cannot change, only future outcomes deserve weight.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Fallacy' attaches to 'sunk cost' as a labeled error. The passage treats the behavior as a mistake, so read 'fallacy' as faulty reasoning, not a neutral term.
skillTips.detail: The definition is exact: 'already paid that cannot be recovered'. For a detail question about sunk cost, use that line, not the loose idea of any money spent.

passages.title: Why Spacing Out Study Works
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
Before a test, many students try to learn everything in one long session the night before. This feels productive, since the material seems fresh by morning. But research on memory points to a better method: spreading the same study time across several shorter sessions on different days.

The reason lies in how memory settles. When you learn something and then let a little time pass, you begin to forget part of it. Reviewing it just as it starts to fade forces your brain to pull the memory back and rebuild it, and each rebuild makes the memory stronger and longer-lasting. Cramming skips this helpful struggle, so what feels solid at night often slips away within days.

This pattern is called the spacing effect. The same hour of study, split into pieces with gaps between them, produces deeper learning than one unbroken block. The gaps are not wasted time; the small forgetting they allow is exactly what makes the later review work. To remember something for a long time, then, do not study harder in one sitting. Study the same amount, but spread it out.

questions.stem: The passage mainly argues
choices: that spreading study over time beats cramming for lasting memory
that tests are unfair to students
that students should study far more hours
questions.explanation: The passage argues the spacing effect: the same study time spread out produces deeper, longer-lasting learning.

questions.stem: What does reviewing material 'just as it starts to fade' do?
choices: It forces the brain to rebuild the memory, making it stronger
questions.explanation: The text says reviewing as it fades 'forces your brain to pull the memory back and rebuild it', making it stronger.

questions.stem: Based on the passage, why does cramming often fail within days?
choices: It skips the helpful struggle of forgetting and rebuilding
The test is usually too hard
One session is too short to read everything
questions.explanation: Cramming 'skips this helpful struggle', so what feels solid at night 'slips away within days'.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'cramming' means
choices: trying to learn everything in one long session
reviewing a little over many days
questions.explanation: The opening scene defines it: 'learn everything in one long session the night before'.

lesson.strategy: A learning-science explainer that argues against a common habit. It grants the appeal of cramming ('This feels productive'), then offers 'a better method' and explains why. Follow the mechanism — forget a little, then rebuild — and end at the rule: same time, spread out.

signals.phrase: a better method
signals.means: Marks the thesis: spread study out instead of cramming it into one session.

signals.phrase: just as it starts to fade
signals.means: The key timing — reviewing at the edge of forgetting is what strengthens memory.

signals.phrase: the spacing effect
signals.means: Names the idea the passage is built to explain and recommend.

signals.phrase: The gaps are not wasted time
signals.means: Answers the obvious objection: the pauses do the work, through the small forgetting they allow.

vocab.inContext: trying to learn everything in one long session, usually right before a test
to grow weaker in memory; reviewing just as a memory begins to fade helps most
feeling like real progress; cramming feels productive even when it works poorly

skillTips.main-idea: The passage compares two methods and picks one. State the main idea as the recommendation — spread study over time — plus its reason, not just 'studying helps'.
skillTips.inference: Link 'skips this helpful struggle' to 'slips away within days'. Infer that cramming fails because it removes the forgetting-and-rebuilding that fixes memory.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Cramming' is shown before it is named: 'learn everything in one long session the night before'. Use that opening scene to define the word.
skillTips.detail: The mechanism is specific: forget a little, then 'pull the memory back and rebuild it'. For a how question, cite that two-step, not a general 'practice more'.

passages.title: The Puzzle of the Placebo
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
In a medical test, some patients are given a real drug while others receive a pill with no medicine in it at all. This dummy pill is called a placebo. Strangely, patients who take the placebo often feel better too, even though it contains nothing that could act on their illness. This puzzling improvement is known as the placebo effect.

Part of the answer is expectation. Believing you are being treated can ease pain, lift mood, and calm the body in real, measurable ways. The mind, it turns out, has some power over how the body feels. The relief is genuine, even when the pill is not.

The placebo effect is also why careful drug trials are built the way they are. If a new medicine only matched the improvement seen in the placebo group, it would not have proved itself; belief alone could explain the result. To count as effective, a drug must do better than the dummy pill. By comparing the two groups, researchers separate the true power of the medicine from the power of simply expecting to be helped.

questions.stem: The passage mainly explains
choices: how doctors choose which patients to treat
what the placebo effect is and why drug trials compare against a dummy pill
questions.explanation: The passage defines the placebo effect and shows why trials must compare a drug against a placebo.

questions.stem: What is a placebo?
choices: A dummy pill with no medicine in it
questions.explanation: The text calls a placebo 'a pill with no medicine in it at all'.

questions.stem: Why must a real drug do better than the placebo group to count as effective?
choices: Because otherwise belief alone might explain the improvement
questions.explanation: If a drug only matched the placebo, 'belief alone could explain the result', so it must do better to prove itself.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'expectation' refers to
choices: a patient's belief that they are being treated
questions.explanation: The text explains expectation as 'Believing you are being treated', which can itself ease symptoms.

lesson.strategy: A psychology and science explainer with two jobs. First it defines a surprise: a pill with no medicine still helps, the 'placebo effect'. Then it uses that fact to explain how drug trials are designed. Track the shift from what the effect is to why researchers must control for it.

signals.phrase: a pill with no medicine in it at all
signals.means: Defines the placebo — the empty treatment that still seems to work.

signals.phrase: Part of the answer is expectation
signals.means: Gives the cause: belief in treatment produces real relief.

signals.phrase: The relief is genuine
signals.means: Guards against a misread — the improvement is real, not imagined or faked.

signals.phrase: careful drug trials are built
signals.means: The turn to the practical use: the effect shapes how medicines are tested.

vocab.inContext: a dummy pill with no active medicine, used to compare against a real drug
the belief that you are being treated, which can itself ease symptoms
real and true; the relief from a placebo is genuine, not pretended

skillTips.main-idea: The passage does two things: defines the placebo effect and explains its use in trials. State both, or at least the effect and why it forces a comparison group.
skillTips.inference: Link 'belief alone could explain the result' to the rule that a drug must beat the placebo. Infer the comparison exists to rule out expectation as the cause.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Genuine' answers a doubt the reader might have. Set against 'even when the pill is not', it means real, confirming the relief is not faked.
skillTips.detail: The design point is exact: a drug must 'do better than the dummy pill'. For a detail question, use that standard, not a looser 'the drug must work'.

passages.title: The Rule of the Vital Few
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
People often assume that results are shared out evenly: put in twice the work, get twice the reward. In practice, outcomes are frequently lopsided. A small part of the causes tends to produce a large part of the effects. This rough pattern is known as the 80/20 rule, or the Pareto principle.

The numbers are not exact, and they are not magic. The point is only that effort and result are often unequal. A few products may bring in most of a company's sales. A handful of streets may see most of a city's traffic. A short list of tasks may deliver most of what you truly care about, while a long tail of minor tasks adds little.

Seen this way, the rule is a tool for choosing. If a small share of your work produces most of the value, the smart move is to find that share and protect it, rather than treating every task as equal. The lesson is not to do less, but to notice that not all effort counts the same, and to aim your best energy where it matters most.

questions.stem: The passage mainly explains
choices: that cities should reduce their traffic
that a small share of causes often produces most results, and how to use that
that companies should sell fewer products
that hard work always pays double
questions.explanation: The passage explains the 80/20 pattern of uneven results and how to use it to choose where to focus.

questions.stem: According to the passage, what does the 80/20 rule say about causes and effects?
choices: A small part of the causes tends to produce a large part of the effects
Causes and effects are always equal
Effects always come before their causes
questions.explanation: The text states 'A small part of the causes tends to produce a large part of the effects'.

questions.stem: Why does the passage call the rule 'a tool for choosing'?
choices: Because it gives exact, fixed numbers
Because spotting the high-value share lets you aim your effort there
Because it applies only to company sales
Because it tells you to work far less
questions.explanation: If a small share produces most value, you can 'find that share and protect it' — the rule guides where to spend effort.

questions.stem: In this passage, 'lopsided' most nearly means
questions.explanation: Set against 'shared out evenly', 'lopsided' means uneven and tilted toward one side.

lesson.strategy: An economics explainer that corrects an assumption and turns it into advice. It denies that results are 'shared out evenly', names the pattern (the 80/20 rule), warns the numbers are loose, then makes it useful. Read the examples as proof of one idea, not separate facts.

signals.phrase: outcomes are frequently lopsided
signals.means: The claim that overturns the even-effort assumption: results tilt toward a few causes.

signals.phrase: the 80/20 rule, or the Pareto principle
signals.means: Names the pattern; the two names point to the same rough idea.

signals.phrase: The numbers are not exact
signals.means: A guard against misreading — treat 80/20 as a tendency, not a precise law.

signals.phrase: a tool for choosing
signals.means: The turn to use: the pattern becomes a way to decide where to spend effort.

vocab.inContext: uneven, weighted heavily to one side; a few causes carry most of the effect
not the same in size; effort and result often do not match one to one
the many small tasks that each add little, set against the vital few

skillTips.main-idea: The examples all serve one claim: results are uneven, so focus on the vital few. State that idea plus its use, not any single example like sales or traffic.
skillTips.inference: Link 'a small share... produces most of the value' to 'find that share and protect it'. Infer the rule helps you prioritize, which is why it is called a tool.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Lopsided' opposes 'shared out evenly' from the first line. Use that contrast: lopsided means tilted and uneven, not balanced.
skillTips.detail: The rule's wording is careful: 'a small part of the causes... a large part of the effects', and the numbers are 'not exact'. Keep that looseness, not a fixed 80 and 20.

passages.title: Why Even the Best Should Trade
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
Suppose one worker is better than her neighbor at two jobs, both baking bread and fixing shoes. It seems she should simply do both herself. Yet economics shows that both people can gain if each focuses on one job and they trade. The reason is an idea called comparative advantage.

The key is not who is better, but what each person gives up. Every hour the skilled worker spends fixing shoes is an hour she cannot bake, and baking is where her time is worth the most. Her neighbor, though slower at both, gives up very little bread by mending shoes, since he was never a strong baker. So he should mend the shoes while she bakes, and they exchange.

By each doing the job they sacrifice the least to do, the pair produces more bread and more repaired shoes together than if both split their time. This is why trade can help both sides even when one seems better at everything. The gain comes not from being best, but from spending your hours where giving them up costs you the least.

questions.stem: The passage mainly explains
choices: why two people can both gain by specializing and trading, even if one is better at everything
how to repair a pair of shoes
why some workers are slower than others
questions.explanation: The passage explains comparative advantage: both gain by each doing the job they give up the least to do.

questions.stem: According to the passage, what should the skilled worker do?
choices: Bake, while her neighbor mends the shoes
Mend shoes, while her neighbor bakes
questions.explanation: Because baking is where her time is worth most, 'he should mend the shoes while she bakes'.

questions.stem: Why should the slower neighbor mend the shoes?
choices: He gives up very little bread, so mending shoes costs him the least
The skilled worker refuses to do it
Repaired shoes are worth more than bread
He is actually faster at shoes
questions.explanation: He 'gives up very little bread by mending shoes', so that job costs him the least to choose.

questions.stem: In this passage, to 'sacrifice' the least means to
questions.explanation: Shown by 'an hour she cannot bake', to sacrifice is to give up; the best job is the one you give up the least to do.

lesson.strategy: An economics explainer that argues against an obvious answer. It sets up the puzzle — why trade if one person is better at both jobs — then shifts the question from skill to cost: 'not who is better, but what each person gives up'. Follow that shift to the payoff.

signals.phrase: both people can gain if each focuses
signals.means: The surprising thesis: specializing and trading beats doing everything yourself.

signals.phrase: The key is not who is better
signals.means: The turn that reframes the problem — the answer is about cost, not raw skill.

signals.phrase: comparative advantage
signals.means: Names the principle: do the job you give up the least to do.

signals.phrase: even when one seems better at everything
signals.means: States the counterintuitive result the whole passage exists to prove.

vocab.inContext: doing the job you give up the least to do, so trade helps both sides
to give up; each person should take the job that costs them the least to choose
to trade one person's work for another's; the baker and shoe-mender swap results

skillTips.main-idea: The passage overturns the guess that the better worker should do all. State the main idea as the principle — both gain by specializing in what they give up least.
skillTips.inference: The reason the neighbor mends shoes is buried in cost: he 'gives up very little bread'. Infer the choice from what each loses, not who is faster.
skillTips.vocabulary: 'Sacrifice' is used as 'give up', shown by 'an hour she cannot bake'. Read it as cost or loss, not anything noble or painful.
skillTips.detail: The assignment is exact: she bakes, he mends. For a who-does-what question, tie it to the cost logic — each takes the job they sacrifice least to do.

passages.title: Why Every Flat Map Lies
passages.attribution: Original passage © Studio AM, written for Hone Literacy.
passages.text:
A globe shows the Earth truthfully, but a globe is hard to carry and hard to print in a book. So mapmakers flatten the round Earth onto a flat sheet. The trouble is that a sphere cannot be flattened without stretching some parts of it. Every flat map, therefore, is wrong in some way. The only choice is which error to accept.

One famous world map, the Mercator projection, keeps the shapes of countries and the directions between them correct, which made it useful for sailors steering a course. But it pays for this by stretching areas near the top and bottom of the map. Lands close to the poles look far larger than they really are. On such a map, Greenland can appear as big as Africa, though Africa is many times its size.

This does not make the map useless; it makes it a tool with a known bias. A map that is honest about distances may distort shapes instead. The wise reader asks not 'Is this map right?' but 'What does this map get right, and what has it traded away?'

questions.stem: The passage mainly argues
choices: that every flat map must distort the round Earth, trading one accuracy for another
that globes are better than maps in every way
that the Mercator map is completely useless
that Greenland is truly larger than Africa
questions.explanation: The passage argues that flattening a sphere forces distortion, so each map trades one kind of accuracy for another.

questions.stem: What does the Mercator projection keep correct?
choices: The shapes of countries and the directions between them
The natural colors of each country
The true sizes of all lands
